topics.title: Introduction to Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback components to perform a wide variety of linear and non-linear mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The op-amp is a multi-stage, DC-coupled amplifier that forms the fundamental building block of modern analog electronics. The name "operational" originates from its original use in performing mathematical operations in analog computers.

## 1.2 Standard Op-Amp Symbol and Terminals

The standard triangular symbol of an op-amp has **five (5) essential terminals**:

1. **Inverting Input Terminal (V⁻ or V₁)** — Marked with a **minus (−) sign**. Signals applied here appear inverted at the output.
2. **Non-Inverting Input Terminal (V⁺ or V₂)** — Marked with a **plus (+) sign**. Signals applied here appear in the same phase at the output.
3. **Output Terminal (Vₒ)** — The amplified signal is available at this pin.
4. **Positive Supply Rail (+V_CC or +V_DD)** — Typically **+15 V** for standard bipolar op-amps.
5. **Negative Supply Rail (−V_EE or −V_SS)** — Typically **−15 V** for standard bipolar op-amps.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lever of Electronics"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Hearing Aid" Analogy:** Imagine an op-amp as a highly sophisticated **hearing aid for tiny electrical whispers**. If you whisper (a small voltage difference) into its ear, it shouts (a much larger voltage) out of its mouth. The op-amp doesn't create energy from nothing; it uses the power supply (like a battery in the hearing aid) to amplify the signal. It specifically amplifies only the *difference* between two inputs — this is what makes it a **differential amplifier**.

## 1.4 Historical Context and Significance

The first commercial op-amp was the **µA702** introduced by **Fairchild Semiconductor in 1964**. The legendary **IC 741 (µA741)**, released in **1968**, became the industry benchmark and is still widely referenced in KTU textbooks and laboratory work today. The "741" remains so popular that it is often used as a generic synonym for "op-amp" in undergraduate electronics.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for Exams:** The IC 741 is an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** IC with the most commonly used op-amp pinout in the KTU curriculum. Pin 2 is Inverting, Pin 3 is Non-Inverting, Pin 6 is Output, and Pins 7 and 4 are the +V and −V supply rails respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Internal Architecture of an Op-Amp

A typical monolithic op-amp consists of **four distinct functional stages** working in cascade:

### Stage 1: Input Stage (Differential Amplifier)
- Provides high **input impedance**, low **offset voltage**, and high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**.
- Usually built using a **BJT differential pair** with an active current source load.

### Stage 2: Gain Stage (Intermediate / Voltage Amplifier)
- Provides the **major portion of voltage gain** (often 60–80 dB).
- Implemented as a **common-emitter amplifier** with a constant-current source load.

### Stage 3: Level Shifter (Bias Network)
- Shifts the DC level of the signal to allow the next stage to operate correctly.
- Required because direct coupling accumulates DC offsets.

### Stage 4: Output Stage (Push-Pull)
- Provides **low output impedance** and the ability to drive heavy loads.
- Usually a **Class-AB complementary push-pull emitter follower** (e.g., the classic "totem-pole" output of the 741).

## 2.2 Ideal vs. Practical Op-Amp Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus:** "Compare ideal and practical op-amp characteristics" is one of the most frequently asked 7-mark questions. Memorize the table below thoroughly.

| Parameter | Ideal Op-Amp Value | Practical Op-Amp Value (IC 741) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Open-Loop Voltage Gain (A_OL) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **2 × 10⁵** (≈ 106 dB) |
| Input Impedance (Z_in) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **2 MΩ** |
| Output Impedance (Z_out) | **0 (Zero)** | **75 Ω** |
| Bandwidth (BW) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **1 MHz** |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **90 dB** |
| Slew Rate (SR) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **0.5 V/µs** |
| Input Offset Voltage (V_io) | **0 (Zero)** | **±2 mV** |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) | **∞ (Infinite)** | **76 dB** |
| Output Swing | **±V_supply** | **±13 V (with ±15 V supply)** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units / Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Differential Output Voltage | $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^{+} - V^{-})$ | V (Volts) |
| 2 | Common-Mode Voltage | $V_{CM} = \dfrac{V^{+} + V^{-}}{2}$ | V (Volts) |
| 3 | Gain in Decibels | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_{OL})$ | dB |
| 4 | Common-Mode Gain | $A_{CM} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{CM}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | CMRR Definition | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert A_{OL} \vert}{\vert A_{CM} \vert}\right)$ | dB |
| 6 | Slew Rate Relation | $SR = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}\bigg\vert_{max}$ | V/µs |
| 7 | Maximum Sine Wave Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_{p}}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Power Dissipation (Quiescent) | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} - (-V_{EE}) \cdot I_{EE}$ | W (Watts) |
| 9 | Input Bias Current | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | A (Amperes) |
| 10 | Input Offset Current | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | A (Amperes) |

## 2.4 The Two "Golden Rules" of the Ideal Op-Amp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two rules form the **foundation of every op-amp circuit analysis** in KTU exams. They are derived directly from the infinite-gain, infinite-impedance assumptions.

1. **Rule 1 — The Virtual Short Concept:** The differential input voltage is zero because the open-loop gain is infinite.
   $$V^{+} - V^{-} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^{+} = V^{-}$$
2. **Rule 2 — Infinite Input Impedance:** No current flows into the input terminals of the op-amp.
   $$I^{+} = I^{-} = 0$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Op-amps are the **workhorse of analog electronics** and are found in virtually every modern electronic system:
- **Audio Systems:** Preamplifiers, equalizers, mixers, headphone amplifiers.
- **Instrumentation:** Sensor signal conditioning (strain gauges, thermocouples).
- **Active Filters:** Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass realizations.
- **Oscillators & Waveform Generators:** Wien bridge, function generators.
- **Comparators & ADCs:** Zero-crossing detectors, flash ADC front-ends.
- **Power Electronics:** Closed-loop control in SMPS and motor drivers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Loop Transfer Characteristic

Let us mathematically derive the fundamental input-output relationship of an op-amp.

**Step 1: Define the two input voltages.**

Let the voltages applied to the non-inverting and inverting terminals be $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively. By universal op-amp convention:

$$V_1 = V^{+} \quad \text{(Non-inverting input voltage)}$$

$$V_2 = V^{-} \quad \text{(Inverting input voltage)}$$

**Step 2: Define the differential input voltage.**

The op-amp responds to the *difference* between the two input voltages. The differential input is therefore:

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2 = V^{+} - V^{-}$$

**Step 3: Apply the open-loop gain definition.**

The output voltage is the differential input multiplied by the open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL}$ (also denoted simply as $A$):

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_d = A_{OL} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 4: Substituting the ideal limit.**

For an **ideal** op-amp, the open-loop gain approaches infinity, which forces the differential input to approach zero for any finite output:

$$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_d \quad \text{requires} \quad V_d = 0$$

$$\therefore \quad V_1 = V_2 \quad \text{(Virtual Short)}$$

**Step 5: Derive the common-mode rejection principle.**

A real input signal can be decomposed into differential and common-mode components:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= V_{CM} + \dfrac{V_d}{2} \\[4pt]
V_2 &= V_{CM} - \dfrac{V_d}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Express the output in terms of both gains.**

The total output combines the differential amplification and the (undesired) common-mode amplification:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{CM} \cdot V_{CM}$$

**Step 7: Substitute the decomposed inputs.**

$$V_{out} = A_d(V_1 - V_2) + A_{CM} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 8: Define CMRR in terms of the two gains.**

The Common-Mode Rejection Ratio is the ratio of the differential gain to the common-mode gain:

$$\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{CM} \vert}$$

**Step 9: Expressed in decibels for engineering use.**

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{CM}}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Virtual Short Concept from First Principles

**Step 1:** From the fundamental op-amp equation: $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate the differential input:

$$V_1 - V_2 = \dfrac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}}$$

**Step 3:** For the IC 741, $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and the maximum output is approximately 13 V (with ±15 V supply):

$$V_1 - V_2 = \dfrac{13}{2 \times 10^5} = 65 \ \mu V$$

**Step 4:** Notice that 65 µV is negligibly small compared to typical input signal amplitudes (which are in millivolts or volts). Hence, in practical analysis:

$$V_1 - V_2 \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2$$

**This confirms the Virtual Short Principle.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Operating Frequency (Slew-Rate Limited)

**Step 1:** The output of an op-amp, when driven by a sine wave, is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the rate of change of output voltage:

$$\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3:** The maximum rate of change occurs when the cosine equals 1:

$$\left(\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 4:** This maximum rate cannot exceed the slew rate of the op-amp:

$$2\pi f V_p \leq SR$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the maximum undistorted frequency:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

**Step 6:** For the IC 741 with $SR = 0.5$ V/µs and $V_p = 5$ V:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} \approx 15.9 \ \text{kHz}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Operation

| Property | Open-Loop Configuration | Closed-Loop Configuration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback Network | **Absent** | **Present** (resistive/capacitive) |
| Effective Gain | $A_{OL}$ (very high, unstable) | $\dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ (precisely controlled) |
| Bandwidth | $BW_{OL}$ (very narrow) | $BW_{CL} = BW_{OL} \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Linearity | Highly non-linear / saturates | Linear, predictable |
| Stability | Practically unstable | Stable (with proper compensation) |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) | $A \cdot BW = \text{constant}$ | $A \cdot BW = \text{constant}$ |
| Typical Use | Comparator, zero-crossing detector | Amplifier, filter, integrator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard Op-Amp Schematic Symbol

The following Mermaid diagram shows the universal op-amp symbol with all five essential terminals properly labeled.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV1["V1 Non Inverting Input +"] --> nodeMain["TRIANGLE OP AMP SYMBOL"]
    nodeV2["V2 Inverting Input -"] --> nodeMain
    nodeMain --> nodeVout["Vout Amplified Output"]
    nodeVcc["+Vcc Positive Supply"] -.-> nodeMain
    nodeVee["-Vee Negative Supply"] -.-> nodeMain
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The triangular shape is the universal schematic for any amplifier. The "+" and "−" signs inside the triangle denote the non-inverting and inverting inputs. The supply rails (+V_CC and −V_EE) are shown as auxiliary power connections that power the internal circuitry.

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of a General Op-Amp

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInA["Input V1 +"] --> stageA["STAGE 1: DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeInB["Input V2 -"] --> stageA
    stageA --> stageB["STAGE 2: HIGH GAIN VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER"]
    stageB --> stageC["STAGE 3: LEVEL SHIFTER DC BIAS NETWORK"]
    stageC --> stageD["STAGE 4: PUSH PULL OUTPUT STAGE CLASS AB"]
    stageD --> nodeOut["Vout SINGLE ENDED OUTPUT"]
    stageA -.-> nodeCM["Common Mode Rejection Control"]
    stageB -.-> nodeCM
```

## 4.3 IC 741 Pin Configuration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIP8["IC 741 8-PIN DIP PACKAGE TOP VIEW"]
        pin1["PIN 1: OFFSET NULL"]
        pin2["PIN 2: INVERTING INPUT V-"]
        pin3["PIN 3: NON INVERTING INPUT V+"]
        pin4["PIN 4: V NEGATIVE SUPPLY -15V"]
        pin5["PIN 5: OFFSET NULL"]
        pin6["PIN 6: OUTPUT Vout"]
        pin7["PIN 7: V POSITIVE SUPPLY +15V"]
        pin8["PIN 8: NC NO CONNECTION"]
    end
    pin2 --> pin3
    pin3 --> pin4
    pin4 --> pin5
    pin5 --> pin6
    pin6 --> pin7
    pin7 --> pin8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pinout Memory Trick:** *"**N**o **I**dea, **N**o **D**oubt — **741** Sings"* is a popular student mnemonic: **N**C (8) – **I**nverting (2) – **N**on-Inverting (3) – **D**ower (4) – Offset Nulls (1, 5) – **S**upply (7) – Output (6).

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of an Op-Amp Based Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSource["INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE SENSOR OR AUDIO LINE"] --> nodeResistor["INPUT RESISTOR RI FEEDBACK ELEMENT"]
    nodeResistor --> nodeAmp["OP AMP WITH NEGATIVE FEEDBACK Rf"]
    nodeAmp --> nodeLoad["OUTPUT LOAD SPEAKER MOTOR OR NEXT STAGE"]
    nodeAmp -.FEEDBACK PATH.-> nodeResistor
    nodePSU["DUAL POWER SUPPLY +15V AND -15V"] -.-> nodeAmp
```

## 4.5 Closed-Loop Feedback Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVs["Vs INPUT SIGNAL"] --> nodeSum["SUMMING JUNCTION SIGMA"]
    nodeFB["FEEDBACK NETWORK BETA"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeA["AMPLIFIER OPEN LOOP GAIN A_OL"]
    nodeA --> nodeVo["Vout OUTPUT"]
    nodeVo --> nodeFB
    nodeFB -.FEEDBACK FRACTION.-> nodeSum
```

> [!TIP]
> **Closed-Loop Gain Formula:** When the op-amp is configured with negative feedback as shown, the overall gain is $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta}$. When $A_{OL} \beta \gg 1$, this simplifies to the famous result $A_{CL} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$, which depends *only on the external feedback network* — not on the op-amp itself.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Operational Amplifier. Mention any four ideal characteristics.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain DC amplifier that uses external feedback to perform mathematical operations on analog signals. It is a fundamental building block of linear electronic circuits.

The four **ideal characteristics** of an op-amp are:

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain** $(A_{OL} = \infty)$
2. **Infinite input impedance** $(Z_{in} = \infty)$ — draws no current from the source.
3. **Zero output impedance** $(Z_{out} = 0)$ — can drive any load without loss.
4. **Infinite bandwidth** and **infinite CMRR** — amplifies signals of any frequency with perfect rejection of common-mode noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Each ideal characteristic carries **0.5 mark**. A 0.5-mark deduction applies if the symbol and units are missing.

---

### Question 2: Compare the input impedance and output impedance of an ideal op-amp with that of IC 741.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Ideal Op-Amp | IC 741 (Practical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input Impedance (Z_in)** | **∞ Ω (Infinite)** | **2 MΩ** |
| **Output Impedance (Z_out)** | **0 Ω (Zero)** | **75 Ω** |

**Explanation:** The input impedance of an ideal op-amp is infinite so that it does not load the source. The output impedance is zero so that the entire amplified signal is delivered to the load. In the IC 741, although these values are not infinite or zero, they are sufficiently high and low respectively to give near-ideal performance for most practical applications.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal stages of an operational amplifier. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

An op-amp consists of **four functional stages** working in cascade:

1. **Input Stage — Differential Amplifier:** This is the first stage and uses a BJT differential pair with an active current source load. It provides high input impedance, low input offset voltage, low noise, and a high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR). It also provides differential-to-single-ended conversion.

2. **Gain Stage — High Gain Voltage Amplifier:** This is an intermediate stage, usually a common-emitter amplifier with a constant current source load. It provides most of the voltage gain (60–80 dB) and performs DC level shifting.

3. **Level Shifter / Bias Network:** Because the op-amp is direct-coupled, DC offsets accumulate across stages. This stage shifts the DC operating point to allow the next stage to operate in the linear region.

4. **Output Stage — Push-Pull (Class-AB):** This is a complementary symmetry emitter follower (totem-pole). It provides a low output impedance (≈ 75 Ω) and high current-driving capability. Short-circuit protection circuitry is usually included.

#### (b) List and explain any five ideal characteristics of an op-amp. Why are they not achieved in practice? (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Ideal Characteristic | Practical Value (IC 741) | Reason for Deviation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Infinite Open-Loop Gain | $2 \times 10^5$ | Limited by transistor gain and current-source matching. |
| 2 | Infinite Input Impedance | 2 MΩ | Limited by base current of input BJTs. |
| 3 | Zero Output Impedance | 75 Ω | Finite output resistance of emitter followers. |
| 4 | Infinite Bandwidth | 1 MHz | Internal parasitic capacitances form low-pass poles. |
| 5 | Zero Offset Voltage | ±2 mV | Mismatch between input BJTs in the differential pair. |
| 6 | Infinite CMRR | 90 dB | Imperfect symmetry in the input stage. |
| 7 | Infinite Slew Rate | 0.5 V/µs | Internal compensation capacitor limits charging current. |

**Why ideal characteristics are not achieved:** Real transistors have finite current gain $\beta$, finite input resistance, and inherent noise. Manufacturing tolerances lead to mismatched device parameters. Additionally, internal parasitic capacitances create frequency-dependent poles. These physical limitations mean the op-amp behaves ideally only under specific conditions (closed loop, low frequency, low source impedance).

---

### Question B — Choice 2  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Define CMRR. Derive the expression for CMRR in decibels. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode voltage gain $(A_d)$ to the common-mode voltage gain $(A_{CM})$. It measures the ability of the op-amp to reject signals common to both inputs.

$$\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{CM} \vert}$$

**Derivation in Decibels:**

The CMRR expressed in decibels is:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{CM}}\right) \ \text{dB}$$

**Substituting** the standard values for IC 741 where $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_{CM} = 6.32$:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{6.32}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(3.16 \times 10^4) \approx 90 \ \text{dB}$$

#### (b) For the IC 741 op-amp, calculate the maximum operating frequency when delivering a sine wave of peak amplitude 5 V. Given: Slew Rate = 0.5 V/µs. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** State the slew rate limit formula derived earlier:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values: $SR = 0.5$ V/µs $= 0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s and $V_p = 5$ V.

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 5}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator: $2\pi \times 5 = 31.416$

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{500000}{31.416} = 15,915.5 \ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 15.92 \ \text{kHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make the following mistakes and lose 1–2 marks:
> 1. **Unit conversion of Slew Rate:** Forgetting to convert 0.5 V/µs to 0.5 × 10⁶ V/s. This single error cascades into a wrong answer.
> 2. **Using peak-to-peak amplitude:** $V_p$ is **peak** amplitude, not peak-to-peak. If $V_{pp}$ is given, divide by 2 first.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor:** A common mistake is to write $f_{max} = SR/V_p$, missing the $2\pi$. This gives an answer ~6× too high.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist for the night-before-exam quick revision.**

- [ ] **Op-Amp** = Direct-coupled, high-gain, differential-input, single-ended-output DC amplifier.
- [ ] **Five Terminals:** V⁺, V⁻, V_out, +V_CC, −V_EE.
- [ ] **IC 741 Pinout:** Pin 2 = Inverting, Pin 3 = Non-Inverting, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 4 = −V, Pin 7 = +V.
- [ ] **Fundamental Equation:** $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^{+} - V^{-})$.
- [ ] **Golden Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** $V^{+} = V^{-}$.
- [ ] **Golden Rule 2 — Zero Input Current:** $I^{+} = I^{-} = 0$.
- [ ] **Ideal Z_in = ∞, Z_out = 0, A_OL = ∞, BW = ∞, CMRR = ∞.**
- [ ] **Practical 741 Values:** Z_in = 2 MΩ, Z_out = 75 Ω, A_OL = 2 × 10⁵, BW = 1 MHz, CMRR = 90 dB, SR = 0.5 V/µs.
- [ ] **CMRR Formula:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{CM})$.
- [ ] **Slew Rate Formula:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$.
- [ ] **Four Internal Stages:** Differential Amp → Gain Stage → Level Shifter → Push-Pull Output.
- [ ] **Open-Loop = Comparator mode; Closed-Loop = Amplifier mode.**
- [ ] **Closed-Loop Gain:** $A_{CL} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ when $A_{OL} \beta \gg 1$.
- [ ] **Power Supply for 741:** Standard ±15 V, with output swing of approximately ±13 V.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-amp Characteristics and Ideal Op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Op-amp Characteristics and Ideal Op-amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain, differential input electronic voltage amplifier with a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback components to perform a wide variety of linear and non-linear signal-processing operations. The term "operational" originates from its original use in analog computers to perform mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBRUT304)**, an op-amp is treated as a fundamental active building block characterized by a set of idealized electrical parameters that govern its behavior in analog circuit design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> An ideal op-amp is a differential amplifier with infinite open-loop voltage gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite bandwidth, infinite common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and zero output when both inputs are equal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an op-amp as a **"Super-Precise Weighing Scale with a Lever"**:
- It has **two input probes** (the Inverting `(-)` and Non-Inverting `(+)` terminals) that barely draw any current (high input impedance — like a scale that doesn't push down on what it's measuring).
- It **amplifies the tiny difference** between the two input signals by an enormous factor (infinite open-loop gain — like a giant lever that magnifies a small push into a huge motion).
- The output can drive any load easily (zero output impedance — like a powerful water pump that maintains pressure regardless of demand).
- It completely **ignores any signal that is common to both inputs** (infinite CMRR — like a noise-cancelling headphone that rejects background hum).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$ or $A$):** Typically $\geq 10^5$ (practical), $\infty$ (ideal)
- **Input Resistance ($R_{in}$):** $\geq 10^6 \, \Omega$ (practical), $\infty \, \Omega$ (ideal)
- **Output Resistance ($R_{out}$):** $\leq 100 \, \Omega$ (practical), $0 \, \Omega$ (ideal)
- **Bandwidth (BW):** $\leq$ few MHz (practical), $\infty$ Hz (ideal)
- **CMRR:** $\geq 80$ dB (practical), $\infty$ dB (ideal)
- **Slew Rate (SR):** $\leq$ few V/$\mu$s (practical)
- **Supply Voltages:** Typically $\pm 15\text{V}$ (standard for IC 741)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The most commonly referenced op-amp in KTU problems is the **IC 741** (e.g., $\mu$A741). Always verify that the assumed op-amp is **ideal** unless the question specifies otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Diagram of an Op-amp (Internal Architecture)

A practical monolithic op-amp is composed of four cascaded stages:

1. **Input Stage (Differential Amplifier):** Provides high input impedance, differential gain, and common-mode rejection. Usually a differential pair with active load.
2. **Voltage Gain Stage (Intermediate Amplifier):** Provides the bulk of the voltage gain (typically a CE or common-source stage with active load).
3. **Level Shifter (Translator Stage):** Translates the DC level to make output compatible with zero-level reference.
4. **Output Stage (Power Amplifier / Push-Pull):** Provides low output impedance and high current-driving capability, usually a Class-AB complementary symmetry emitter follower.

### 2.2 Ideal Op-Amp Characteristics — The Golden Five

The **Ideal Op-amp** is described by five infinite / zero parameters:

| # | Parameter | Ideal Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | Amplifies any differential input without limit |
| 2 | Input Impedance ($R_{in}$) | $\infty$ | Draws **zero** current from the source |
| 3 | Output Impedance ($R_{out}$) | $0$ | Output voltage is independent of load |
| 4 | Bandwidth (BW) | $\infty$ | Amplifies signals from DC to infinite frequency |
| 5 | CMRR | $\infty$ | Perfectly rejects common-mode signals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Derived Properties of the Ideal Op-amp:**
> 1. **Virtual Short (Virtual Ground concept):** $V_d = V_+ - V_- = 0 \Rightarrow V_+ = V_-$
> 2. **No current into terminals:** $I_+ = I_- = 0$

### 2.3 Practical Op-Amp Non-Ideal Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

#### 2.3.1 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)
The small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to make the output exactly zero (in the absence of any input signal). Caused by mismatches in the input transistor pair.

$$V_{io} \approx 1 \text{ mV to } 6 \text{ mV (for IC 741)}$$

#### 2.3.2 Input Bias Current ($I_B$)
The average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals:

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

For IC 741, $I_B \approx 80 \text{ nA (typical)}$.

#### 2.3.3 Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)
The difference between the two input bias currents:

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

For IC 741, $I_{io} \approx 20 \text{ nA (typical)}$.

#### 2.3.4 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) \text{ dB}$$

For IC 741, CMRR $\geq 70$ dB (typical) to 90 dB (minimum).

#### 2.3.5 Slew Rate (SR)
The maximum rate of change of output voltage with respect to time, when a step input is applied. Limited by internal capacitances and bias currents.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} \quad \text{(in V/}\mu\text{s)}$$

For IC 741, $SR \approx 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$.

#### 2.3.6 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)
The product of the open-loop gain and the -3dB bandwidth, which is constant for a given op-amp:

$$\text{GBW} = A_{OL} \times f_c = \text{constant}$$

For IC 741, GBW $\approx 1$ MHz.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units / Remarks |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| Output Voltage | $V_o = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-) = A_{OL} \cdot V_d$ | Volts |
| CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB |
| Slew Rate | $SR = 2\pi f V_{omax}$ | V/$\mu$s |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = A_{OL} \cdot f_c$ | Hz |
| Input Bias Current | $I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | Amperes |
| Input Offset Current | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2}\vert$ | Amperes |
| Maximum Output Voltage | $V_{omax} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f_m}$ | Volts (for sine wave of freq. $f_m$) |
| PSRR | $\text{PSRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\Delta V_{supply}}{\Delta V_{io}}\right)$ | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Op-amps are the **backbone of analog electronics**, used in active filters, instrumentation amplifiers, ADC/DAC buffers, comparators, oscillators (Wien bridge, astable multivibrator), voltage regulators, and precision rectifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-amp Analysis

**Given:** An ideal op-amp with $A_{OL} = \infty$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$, BW $= \infty$, CMRR $= \infty$.

**Step 1 — Express the output equation.**

The fundamental equation of any op-amp is:

$$V_o = A_{OL} (V_+ - V_-)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the infinite gain constraint.**

Since the output voltage $V_o$ must be a **finite** value (limited by supply rails, say $\pm V_{sat}$), and $A_{OL} \to \infty$, the only way the equation remains consistent is if the differential input tends to zero:

$$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} V_o = \lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} A_{OL} \cdot V_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_d = 0$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Virtual Short condition.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= V_+ - V_- = 0 \\
\Rightarrow V_+ &= V_-
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Virtual Short** concept — the two inputs appear to be at the same potential, even though no current actually flows between them.

**Step 4 — Apply the infinite input impedance constraint.**

Since $R_{in} \to \infty$, by Ohm's law:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{in} &= \frac{V_d}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_d}{\infty} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow I_+ &= I_- = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion (The Two Golden Rules):**
1. **Rule 1 (Virtual Short):** $V_+ = V_-$
2. **Rule 2 (Infinite Input Impedance):** $I_+ = I_- = 0$

> These two rules form the foundation of **every** op-amp circuit analysis in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Slew Rate Limitation on Sinusoidal Output

**Given:** An op-amp is required to produce a sinusoidal output $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$ and has a finite slew rate $SR$.

**Step 1 — Write the output expression.**

$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{where } \omega = 2\pi f$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find the rate of change of output voltage.**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum rate of change.**

The maximum value of $\cos(2\pi f t)$ is unity, so:

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 4 — Equate to Slew Rate.**

For distortion-free operation, this rate of change must not exceed the slew rate:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the maximum permissible frequency (Full-Power Bandwidth).**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** This is a **frequently tested derivation**. Always show the differentiation and the $\cos$ maximization step clearly to get full marks.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Ideal vs Practical Op-amp

| Parameter | Ideal Value | Practical Value (IC 741) | Effect / Significance |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | Sets the precision of mathematical operations |
| Input Impedance ($R_{in}$) | $\infty$ | $2 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Determines loading on source |
| Output Impedance ($R_{out}$) | $0$ | $75 \, \Omega$ | Determines loading capability |
| Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $1$ MHz | Limits high-frequency operation |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $90$ dB | Measures noise rejection |
| Slew Rate | $\infty$ | $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ | Limits large-signal response |
| Input Offset Voltage | $0$ | $2$ mV | Causes output DC error |
| Input Bias Current | $0$ | $80$ nA | Causes output DC error |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) | $\infty$ | $76$ dB | Measures immunity to supply noise |
| Output Voltage Swing | $\pm V_{supply}$ | $\pm 13$ V (with $\pm 15$V supply) | Saturation limits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Op-amp Internal Architecture

```mermaid

    inN[Inverting Input V-] --> stage1

    stage2 --> stage3[Level Shifter Stage]
    stage3 --> stage4[Output Push-Pull Amplifier Stage]

```

    gainBlock --> voutNode[Output Vout = AOL times Vd]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six ideal characteristics of an op-amp.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six ideal characteristics of an op-amp are:

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL} = \infty$):** The differential input is amplified without any limit.
2. **Infinite input impedance ($R_{in} = \infty$):** The op-amp draws no current from the input source.
3. **Zero output impedance ($R_{out} = 0$):** The output voltage is independent of the load connected.
4. **Infinite bandwidth (BW = $\infty$):** The op-amp can amplify signals from DC to infinite frequency with equal gain.
5. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR = $\infty$):** The op-amp perfectly rejects any signal common to both inputs.
6. **Zero output when inputs are equal:** When $V_+ = V_-$, the output $V_o = 0$ V (perfect balance).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define slew rate and CMRR of an op-amp. Mention the typical values for IC 741.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Slew Rate (SR):** Slew rate is defined as the maximum rate of change of output voltage per unit time, when a step input is applied to the op-amp. It is expressed in V/$\mu$s.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$$

**Typical value for IC 741:** $SR = 0.5$ V/$\mu$s.

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):** CMRR is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_c$), expressed in decibels (dB):

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Typical value for IC 741:** CMRR $\geq 90$ dB.

It measures the ability of the op-amp to reject signals that are common to both input terminals (such as noise or interference).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of an op-amp, listing the function of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the two ideal op-amp analysis rules (virtual short and zero input current) from the basic op-amp equation. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

The internal block diagram of a monolithic op-amp consists of **four cascaded stages**:

**Stage 1 — Input Differential Amplifier Stage:**
- Provides high input impedance, differential gain, and common-mode rejection.
- Typically uses an emitter-coupled pair (BJT) or source-coupled pair (MOSFET) with an active current-mirror load.
- Output is a differential signal proportional to $(V_+ - V_-)$.

**Stage 2 — Voltage Gain Stage (Intermediate Amplifier):**
- Provides the bulk of the voltage gain (often 60–80 dB).
- Usually a common-emitter (CE) or common-source stage with an active load.
- Converts the differential signal to a single-ended signal.

**Stage 3 — Level Shifter (DC Translator):**
- Shifts the DC level of the signal to make it compatible with zero-level output reference.
- Uses a constant-current source and emitter follower, or a voltage divider with a zener / diode arrangement.
- Essential because the high-gain stage cannot tolerate large DC offsets.

**Stage 4 — Output Stage (Push-Pull Power Amplifier):**
- Provides low output impedance and high current-driving capability.
- Usually a Class-AB complementary symmetry emitter follower (NPN + PNP pair).
- Output can source or sink current to drive low-impedance loads.

**Auxiliary Circuits:** Biasing network, short-circuit protection, and offset null adjustment.

(Mark distribution: 2 marks for diagram + 5 marks for explanation of each stage = 7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental op-amp output equation.**

$$V_o = A_{OL} (V_+ - V_-) = A_{OL} \cdot V_d$$

where $V_d$ is the differential input voltage.

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal condition $A_{OL} = \infty$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A_{OL} \cdot V_d \\
V_d &= \frac{V_o}{A_{OL}} \\
\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} V_d &= \lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} \frac{V_o}{\infty} \\
\Rightarrow V_d &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow V_+ - V_- &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow V_+ &= V_-
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Rule 1: Virtual Short** — both inputs appear at the same potential, though no current actually flows between them.

**Step 3 — Apply the ideal condition $R_{in} = \infty$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{in} &= \frac{V_d}{R_{in}} \\
\lim_{R_{in} \to \infty} I_{in} &= \frac{V_d}{\infty} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow I_+ &= I_- = 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Rule 2: Infinite Input Impedance** — no current flows into either input terminal.

(Mark distribution: 2 marks for output equation + 3 marks for derivation of Rule 1 + 2 marks for derivation of Rule 2 = 7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare the ideal and practical characteristics of an op-amp using a table. List any **five DC** and **five AC** non-ideal parameters with typical values for IC 741. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has a slew rate of 0.5 V/$\mu$s. Find the maximum amplitude of a sinusoidal output at a frequency of 10 kHz. If the frequency is doubled to 20 kHz, what is the new maximum permissible amplitude? **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Ideal vs Practical Op-amp Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Ideal | Practical (IC 741) |
|-----------|-------|--------------------|
| Open-Loop Gain | $\infty$ | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Input Impedance | $\infty$ | $2 \text{ M}\Omega$ |
| Output Impedance | $0$ | $75 \, \Omega$ |
| Bandwidth | $\infty$ | $1$ MHz |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $90$ dB |

**Five DC Non-Ideal Parameters (IC 741):**
1. Input Offset Voltage: $V_{io} = 2$ mV
2. Input Bias Current: $I_B = 80$ nA
3. Input Offset Current: $I_{io} = 20$ nA
4. Power Supply Rejection Ratio: PSRR = $76$ dB
5. Output Voltage Swing: $\pm 13$ V (with $\pm 15$ V supply)

**Five AC Non-Ideal Parameters (IC 741):**
1. Slew Rate: $SR = 0.5$ V/$\mu$s
2. Gain-Bandwidth Product: GBW = $1$ MHz
3. Common-Mode Input Range: $\pm 13$ V
4. Differential Input Resistance: $2 \text{ M}\Omega$
5. Rise Time: $0.3 \, \mu$s

(Mark distribution: 3 marks for table + 2 marks for DC params + 2 marks for AC params = 7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/$\mu$s $= 0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s, $f_1 = 10$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Apply the slew rate formula for sinusoidal output.**

For a sine wave, the maximum rate of change of the output is:

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 2 — Equate to slew rate and solve for $V_m$ at $f_1 = 10$ kHz.**

$$SR = 2\pi f_1 V_{m1}$$

$$V_{m1} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f_1} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{m1} = \frac{500000}{62831.85} \approx 7.958 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $V_{m2}$ at $f_2 = 20$ kHz.**

$$V_{m2} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f_2} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 20 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{m2} = \frac{500000}{125663.7} \approx 3.979 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Observe the inverse relationship.**

Notice that $V_{m2} = \frac{V_{m1}}{2} = \frac{7.958}{2} \approx 3.979$ V. This confirms the inverse proportionality $V_m \propto 1/f$.

**Final Answers:**
- $V_{m1} \approx 7.96$ V (at 10 kHz)
- $V_{m2} \approx 3.98$ V (at 20 kHz)

(Mark distribution: 2 marks for formula + 3 marks for Part 1 calculation + 2 marks for Part 2 calculation = 7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Always convert $SR$ from V/$\mu$s to V/s (multiply by $10^6$) before substituting in equations with frequency in Hz.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2π factor:** Students often write $SR = fV_m$, missing the $2\pi$ factor. This leads to 0 marks.
> 3. **Confusion between CMRR (dB) and CMRR (ratio):** The formula $20\log(A_d/A_c)$ gives dB. The ratio form is $A_d/A_c$, not the dB value itself.
> 4. **Ideal vs Practical confusion:** KTU 2024 explicitly tests whether students can identify when "ideal op-amp" assumptions apply. Always state the two golden rules at the start of your solution.
> 5. **No mention of unit (V/$\mu$s) in slew rate definition:** Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An op-amp is a **direct-coupled, high-gain, differential input, single-ended output** voltage amplifier.
- [x] **Ideal op-amp = 5 infinite/zero parameters:** $A_{OL} = \infty$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$, BW = $\infty$, CMRR = $\infty$.
- [x] **Two Golden Rules (MUST MEMORIZE):**
  - Rule 1: $V_+ = V_-$ (Virtual Short)
  - Rule 2: $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (Infinite input impedance)
- [x] Op-amp internal structure = **4 stages:** Differential amp → Voltage gain → Level shifter → Output push-pull.
- [x] **CMRR (dB) = $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$** — measures noise rejection.
- [x] **Slew Rate (SR) = $2\pi f V_m$** — limits large-signal high-frequency response.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product = $A_{OL} \times f_c =$ constant** — trade-off between gain and bandwidth.
- [x] **IC 741 typical values to remember:** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $R_{in} = 2$ M$\Omega$, $R_{out} = 75 \, \Omega$, BW = 1 MHz, CMRR = 90 dB, SR = 0.5 V/$\mu$s.
- [x] **Input offset voltage** is the small DC voltage required to make $V_o = 0$ with no input.
- [x] **Input bias current** is the average of the two DC input currents; **Input offset current** is their difference.
- [x] The op-amp is the **fundamental building block** of all analog signal-processing circuits in KTU Module 1 (inverting, non-inverting, summing, integrator, differentiator).
- [x] Always specify whether the op-amp is **ideal** or **practical (IC 741)** before solving any KTU numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) – Formal Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output. It is the fundamental active building block of analog electronics and is constructed almost universally using integrated circuit (IC) technology (e.g., the classic **µA741**, **LM324**, or **TL081**). In the KTU 2024 scheme, the op-amp is treated as an *ideal* device for analytical derivations, and then extended to practical non-ideal limitations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (KTU Standard Model)**
> - **Open-loop differential voltage gain ($A_{OL}$) → ∞**
> - **Input impedance ($R_i$) → ∞** (draws no current into input terminals)
> - **Output impedance ($R_o$) → 0** (ideal voltage source behavior)
> - Infinite bandwidth (Gain-Bandwidth Product → ∞)
> - Zero output when both inputs are equal (zero offset voltage & current)
> - Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Golden Rules of the Ideal Op-Amp (KTU Board Favourite)**
> 1. **The Virtual Short Concept:** $V_+ = V_-$ (The voltage difference between the two input terminals is effectively zero because the open-loop gain is infinite.)
> 2. **The Infinite Input Impedance Concept:** $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (No current flows into either input terminal.)

These two rules are the **only analytical tools** you need to solve *every* op-amp circuit problem in the KTU examination.

## 1.2 Configurations – The Closed-Loop Family

In Module 1, the syllabus focuses on **three foundational closed-loop configurations**, where negative feedback is used to trade raw open-loop gain for predictable, stable, and controllable behavior:

1. **Inverting Amplifier** – Input applied to the inverting (−) terminal through $R_1$.
2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier** – Input applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
3. **Differential (Difference) Amplifier** – Two inputs are combined to amplify only their *difference*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "See-Saw" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine the op-amp as a perfectly balanced see-saw with a powerful hydraulic piston underneath.**
> The two input terminals (+ and −) are the two ends of the see-saw. The op-amp's massive internal gain is the hydraulic piston that tries *at any cost* to keep the see-saw perfectly horizontal. The moment a tiny voltage difference appears between the two ends, the piston pushes the output *fiercely* until the negative feedback signal nullifies the difference. This "balance restoration" mechanism is what we exploit in all three configurations below.

### 1.3.1 Inverting Amplifier – The "Reversing Mirror" Analogy
Think of it like looking into a **rear-view mirror of a car**: the scene in front of you (input) appears in the mirror (output), but **upside down (inverted)**. You control how big the reflection appears by adjusting the ratio of the mirror-to-eye distance to the eye-to-scene distance. The op-amp inverting amplifier does exactly this — the gain magnitude is set by $\frac{R_f}{R_1}$, and the sign is flipped.

### 1.3.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier – The "Magnifying Glass" Analogy
Place a magnifying glass (with magnification controlled by the feedback resistor ratio) over your input signal. The output is the **same orientation** as the input (no sign flip), but **scaled larger**. The minimum magnification possible is **1×** (a follower).

### 1.3.3 Differential Amplifier – The "Subtractor" Analogy
Imagine a **weighing scale in a market**. The scale reads only the *difference* in weight between two items placed on its two pans, ignoring the absolute weight of either. The differential amplifier does this for voltages — it outputs a voltage proportional only to $(V_2 - V_1)$, suppressing any common signal (noise) appearing equally at both inputs.

## 1.4 Symbol and Pin Identification

The standard op-amp symbol has **5 essential terminals**:
- **Inverting Input (−)**
- **Non-Inverting Input (+)**
- **Output ($V_{out}$)**
- **Positive Supply ($+V_{CC}$ or $+V_{DD}$)**
- **Negative Supply ($-V_{EE}$ or $-V_{SS}$)** — for dual-suprail operation.

A complete block-level representation is given later in **SECTION 4**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Need for Negative Feedback

The open-loop gain of a practical op-amp is enormous (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$) and **drifts with temperature, supply voltage, and process variations**. To make the gain *predictable*, we sacrifice some of this raw gain by feeding a portion of the output back to the inverting input. This technique is called **Negative Feedback**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Negative feedback provides **four critical benefits**:
> 1. Stabilizes the closed-loop gain against component tolerances.
> 2. Increases the input impedance (especially for non-inverting).
> 3. Decreases the output impedance.
> 4. Linearizes the transfer characteristic and extends the bandwidth.

## 2.2 Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Architecture

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through resistor $R_1$ to the **inverting (−)** terminal. The non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting input.

**Why negative feedback works here:** A portion of the output (via $R_f$) is subtracted from the input at the virtual ground node. The op-amp adjusts $V_{out}$ until the inverting input is held at *virtual ground* (i.e., 0 V, because the (+) terminal is grounded and the virtual short rule forces $V_- = V_+ = 0$).

## 2.3 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Architecture

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied **directly to the non-inverting (+) terminal**. The feedback network ($R_f$ and $R_1$) is connected between the output, the inverting input, and ground. The closed-loop gain is determined by the **feedback attenuation ratio** $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$.

**Why the gain is always ≥ 1:** The output must always be *at least as large as the input* to drive enough current through $R_f$ to maintain the virtual short at the (−) terminal. The minimum possible gain (when $R_f = 0$) is **exactly 1**, giving a **Voltage Follower** (buffer).

## 2.4 Differential Amplifier — Circuit Architecture

This is the most analytical of the three. The circuit has **four precisely matched resistors**: $R_1$, $R_f$ at the inverting branch, and $R_2$, $R_3$ at the non-inverting branch. Input $V_1$ enters the inverting side via $R_1$, and input $V_2$ enters the non-inverting side via $R_2$ (with $R_3$ to ground).

**Why we use the matching condition:** $V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ holds **only if** the resistor ratios are matched: $\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}$. If this is violated, common-mode signals leak into the output, destroying the difference function.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these formulas alone cover 90% of Module 1 numerical problems.**

| **Parameter** | **Inverting Amplifier** | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | **Differential Amplifier** (matched) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed-Loop Voltage Gain $A_{CL}$** | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $A_v = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ |
| **Phase Relationship** | $180^\circ$ (inverted) | $0^\circ$ (in-phase) | $V_2$ → in-phase, $V_1$ → inverted |
| **Input Impedance $Z_{in}$** | $Z_{in} = R_1$ (finite) | $Z_{in} \rightarrow \infty$ (ideal) | $Z_{in1} = R_1 + R_f$, $Z_{in2} = R_2 + R_3$ |
| **Output Impedance $Z_{out}$** | $\approx 0$ (ideal) | $\approx 0$ (ideal) | $\approx 0$ (ideal) |
| **Virtual Node Voltage** | $V_- = V_+ = 0$ (Virtual Ground) | $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$ (Virtual Short) | $V_- = V_+ = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3} \cdot V_2$ |
| **Output Voltage Expression** | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ |
| **Min/Min Gain Limit** | Can be $< 1$ (e.g., attenuator) | $\geq 1$ always | Same ratio $\geq$ any value |
| **Unity-Gain Special Case** | Not applicable | $R_f = 0$ → Voltage Follower | $R_f = R_1$, $R_3 = R_2$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Configuration** | **Industrial Application** |
|---|---|
| **Inverting** | Summing amplifier (audio mixers), active filters, integrators, current-to-voltage converters (trans-impedance) |
| **Non-Inverting** | Sensor signal pre-conditioning, buffer/isolation stages, high-impedance probe amplifiers, voltage references |
| **Differential** | Instrumentation amplifiers (ECG/EEG front-ends), bridge sensor readout (strain gauges, thermocouples), noise-immune signal transmission (twisted-pair receivers) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** The differential amplifier is the **heart of every instrumentation amplifier**. A common follow-up Module 1 question asks: *"How do you convert a single-op-amp difference amplifier into a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier?"* — so internalize its gain equation $V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Gain of the Inverting Amplifier

**Given:** Ideal op-amp, input $V_{in}$ through $R_1$ to inverting terminal, $R_f$ as feedback, non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Goal:** Prove $A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$.

### Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short Rule
Since the (+) terminal is grounded at **0 V**, the virtual short forces the (−) terminal to also be at **0 V**.

$$V_- = V_+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

This node is called **Virtual Ground** because it is at 0 V but is *not* physically connected to ground.

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node
The currents flowing into the inverting node must sum to zero (no current enters the op-amp by the infinite input impedance rule).

$$\frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_f} = 0$$

### Step 3: Substitute $V_- = 0$

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

### Step 4: Isolate the Two Current Terms

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Voltage Gain

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Gain of the Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Given:** Ideal op-amp, input $V_{in}$ applied to (+) terminal, $R_f$ from output to (−) terminal, $R_1$ from (−) terminal to ground.

**Goal:** Prove $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$.

### Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short
Since $V_+ = V_{in}$, the virtual short forces:

$$V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$$

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node
No current enters the op-amp, so the current through $R_1$ (from $V_-$ to ground) must equal the current through $R_f$ (from $V_{out}$ to $V_-$).

$$\frac{V_- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_f}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $V_- = V_{in}$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

### Step 4: Cross-Multiply

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

### Step 5: Expand and Group Terms

$$V_{in} R_f = V_{out} R_1 - V_{in} R_1$$

$$V_{out} R_1 = V_{in} R_f + V_{in} R_1$$

### Step 6: Factor Out $V_{in}$ and Solve for the Gain

$$V_{out} R_1 = V_{in} (R_f + R_1)$$

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_f + R_1}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The gain is **positive** (no inversion) and always **≥ 1**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Voltage of the Differential Amplifier (Superposition Method)

**Given:** Ideal op-amp, $V_1$ at inverting side via $R_1$, feedback $R_f$, $V_2$ at non-inverting side via $R_2$, with $R_3$ to ground. We require matching: $\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} = k$.

**Goal:** Derive $V_{out} = k(V_2 - V_1)$.

### Step 3.3.1: Contribution of $V_2$ Alone (Set $V_1 = 0$)
When $V_1 = 0$, $R_1$ is grounded at the inverting side. This becomes a **non-inverting amplifier** for $V_2$, but with the input scaled by a voltage divider formed by $R_2$ and $R_3$.

The voltage at the (+) terminal is:

$$V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

The non-inverting gain (with feedback network $R_f$ and $R_1$) gives:

$$V_{out2} = V_+ \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

### Step 3.3.2: Contribution of $V_1$ Alone (Set $V_2 = 0$)
When $V_2 = 0$, the (+) terminal is grounded through $R_3$ (since no current flows into the op-amp, $R_2$ has no voltage drop). This becomes a standard **inverting amplifier** for $V_1$.

$$V_{out1} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_1$$

### Step 3.3.3: Apply Superposition
The total output is the algebraic sum of the two contributions:

$$V_{out} = V_{out2} + V_{out1} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_1$$

### Step 3.3.4: Apply the Matching Condition
Let $k = \frac{R_f}{R_1}$. Substitute into the gain bracket:

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + k = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1}$$

For the equation to reduce to a clean difference, we require:

$$\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

Cross-multiplying both sides:

$$R_3(R_1 + R_f) = R_1(R_2 + R_3)$$

$$R_3 R_1 + R_3 R_f = R_1 R_2 + R_1 R_3$$

$$R_3 R_f = R_1 R_2$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_2}{R_3}$$

Equivalently, $\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}$ — this is the **matching condition**.

### Step 3.3.5: Simplify Under Matching
Substitute $\frac{R_3}{R_2+R_3} = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_f}$ back into the superposition equation:

$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} - k \cdot V_1$$

$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot 1 - k \cdot V_1$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = k(V_2 - V_1) = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)}$$

**Conclusion:** Under matching conditions, the differential amplifier *completely rejects* any common-mode component $V_{CM} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ and amplifies only the differential component $V_D = V_2 - V_1$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) — Quick Definition

For completeness (and as a high-yield follow-up), the CMRR is defined as:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_{DM} \vert}{\vert A_{CM} \vert}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $A_{DM}$ is the differential-mode gain (from $V_2 - V_1$) and $A_{CM}$ is the common-mode gain. A perfectly matched difference amplifier has $A_{CM} = 0$, so its CMRR → ∞.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style numerical problem setup:** If $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 50\,\text{mV}$, find $V_{out}$ for all three configurations.

| **Configuration** | **Gain Formula** | **Numerical Substitution** | **Output $V_{out}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting** | $-\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $-10$ | $-500\,\text{mV}$ |
| **Non-Inverting** | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $11$ | $+550\,\text{mV}$ |
| **Differential** ($V_2 = +50\,\text{mV}, V_1 = -50\,\text{mV}$) | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $10$ | $10 \cdot (50 - (-50))\,\text{mV} = 1.0\,\text{V}$ |
