Determine the output waveform at $t = 2$ ms, $t = 5$ ms, and $t = 8$ ms. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 14$ V. **Step 1:** Evaluate $V_{in}$ at the given times. - At $t = 2$ ms: $V_{in} = 5\sin(200\pi \times 0.002) = 5\sin(0.4\pi) = 5\sin(72°) \approx 5 \times 0.951 \approx +4.76$ V (positive) - At $t = 5$ ms: $V_{in} = 5\sin(200\pi \times 0.005) = 5\sin(\pi) = 0$ V (exactly at zero crossing) - At $t = 8$ ms: $V_{in} = 5\sin(200\pi \times 0.008) = 5\sin(1.6\pi) = 5\sin(288°) \approx -4.76$ V (negative) **Step 2:** Apply the non-inverting ZCD rule. - $t = 2$ ms ($V_{in} > 0$) → $V_o = +V_{sat} = +14$ V - $t = 5$ ms ($V_{in} = 0$) → transition instant (output is at threshold, practically flipping) - $t = 8$ ms ($V_{in} < 0$) → $V_o = -V_{sat} = -14$ V > **Logic Explanation:** This confirms the ZCD produces a square wave with edges exactly at the zero-crossings of the input sine. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic ZCD Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
Vin sine wave] --> B[Op-amp
Open Loop
Comparator] C[Reference 0V
Ground] --> B B --> D[Output
Square Wave
+Vsat or -Vsat] E[Supply Rails
+Vcc and -VEE] -.powers.-> B ``` **Explanation of Flow:** The input sine wave and the 0 V reference enter the op-amp's two differential inputs. The op-amp, having no feedback, saturates its output to $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ based on the polarity of the input. The result is a square wave whose transitions coincide with the input's zero crossings. ## 4.2 Detailed Pin-Level Schematic (Non-Inverting ZCD) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph OA[Op-amp 741] IN1((Pin 3
Non-inverting +)) --> CMP[Differential
Comparator Core] IN2((Pin 2
Inverting -)) --> CMP CMP --> OUT((Pin 6
Output)) VCC1((Pin 7
+Vcc +15V)) -.powers.-> CMP VCC2((Pin 4
-VEE -15V)) -.powers.-> CMP end SRC[Vin Source
Sine Wave] --> IN1 GND[Ground
0V Reference] --> IN2 LOAD[Output Load
Square Wave] --> OUT ``` **Explanation of Flow:** Pin 3 receives the input sine wave, Pin 2 is tied to ground (0 V reference), and the output at Pin 6 produces a square wave. Power is supplied at Pin 7 and Pin 4. Note that there is no resistor or wire connecting Pin 6 back to either input pin — this is the defining feature of open-loop operation. ## 4.3 Input vs. Output Waveform Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SINE[Input Sine Wave] A1[Positive Half Cycle
0 to T/2] A2[Zero Crossing at T/2] A3[Negative Half Cycle
T/2 to T] end subgraph SQ[Output Square Wave] B1[+Vsat Level
0 to T/2] B2[Edge Trigger
at T/2] B3[-Vsat Level
T/2 to T] end A1 --- B1 A2 --- B2 A3 --- B3 ``` **Explanation of Flow:** Each zero crossing of the input sine wave (rising or falling) triggers an edge transition in the output square wave. The positive half-cycle of the sine corresponds to $+V_{sat}$, and the negative half-cycle corresponds to $-V_{sat}$. ## 4.4 Transfer Characteristic Plot (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Vin less than 0V] --> P2[Vo equals -Vsat
Negative Saturation] P3[Vin equal to 0V
Threshold] --> P4[Transition Edge
Infinite Slope in Ideal] P5[Vin greater than 0V] --> P6[Vo equals +Vsat
Positive Saturation] P2 --- P4 P4 --- P6 ``` **Explanation of Flow:** The transfer characteristic is a vertical step at $V_{in} = 0$. For all $V_{in} < 0$, the output sits at $-V_{sat}$. For all $V_{in} > 0$, the output sits at $+V_{sat}$. The transition at $V_{in} = 0$ is effectively instantaneous in the ideal case. ## 4.5 Noise-Sensitivity & Multi-Triggering Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Clean Input Sine] --> Q2[ZCD Output
Clean Square Wave] Q3[Noisy Input Sine
with 50Hz Hum] --> Q4[ZCD Output
Chattering
Multiple Spurious Edges] Q2 --> Q5[Good Digital Signal] Q4 --> Q6[False Counts
in Frequency Counter] ``` **Explanation of Flow:** When the input has noise, the ZCD sees multiple zero crossings near each true crossing, producing multiple output transitions. This is the primary limitation that motivates the **Schmitt Trigger ZCD** (covered in a later module), which uses positive feedback to introduce hysteresis and reject noise. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define a Zero Crossing Detector. Mention its primary application.** **Model Answer:** A Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD) is an op-amp based comparator circuit operated in open-loop configuration, used to detect the exact instant at which an input signal crosses the zero-voltage reference level (0 V / ground). The op-amp output saturates to $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ depending on the polarity of the input. Its **primary application** is the conversion of a sinusoidal waveform into a square wave of the same frequency, which is essential in **phase meters, frequency counters, TRIAC firing circuits, and square-wave generators**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Why is the op-amp used in open-loop configuration for a Zero Crossing Detector?** **Model Answer:** The op-amp is used in open-loop (without any feedback element) because the Zero Crossing Detector requires the op-amp to act as a **high-gain bistable switch**. With open-loop gain $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$, even a microvolt-level input difference drives the output to one of the supply rails ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). This produces a sharp, rail-to-rail transition exactly at the zero-crossing instant, which is the defining behavior of a ZCD. If feedback were added, the op-amp would operate in its linear region and the output would no longer be a clean square wave. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Zero Crossing Detector using an op-amp. Draw the input-output waveforms and the transfer characteristic. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a non-inverting ZCD using op-amp 741 with $\pm 15$ V supplies. For an input $V_{in}(t) = 8\sin(100\pi t)$ V, determine the output at $t = 2$ ms, $t = 5$ ms, and $t = 12$ ms. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 14$ V. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) Solution **Circuit Description:** - The op-amp 741 is connected in **open-loop** (no feedback between output pin 6 and input pins 2 or 3). - The **input signal** $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal** (Pin 3). - The **inverting terminal** (Pin 2) is connected to **ground** (0 V reference). - The op-amp is powered by $\pm 15$ V supplies at Pin 7 and Pin 4. **Operation:** The op-amp open-loop output equation is: $$ V_o = A_{OL}\,(V_{+} - V_{-}) = A_{OL}\,(V_{in} - 0) = A_{OL}\cdot V_{in} $$ **Case 1:** When $V_{in} > 0$ V: - $V_{+} > V_{-}$, so the output drives to **positive saturation**. - $V_o = +V_{sat} \approx +14$ V. **Case 2:** When $V_{in} < 0$ V: - $V_{+} < V_{-}$, so the output drives to **negative saturation**. - $V_o = -V_{sat} \approx -14$ V. **Case 3:** When $V_{in} = 0$ V (the zero crossing): - The output transitions instantaneously from one saturation level to the other. **Waveforms:** | Time Interval | Input $V_{in}$ | Output $V_o$ | |---|---|---| | 0 to $T/2$ (positive half) | $> 0$ V | $+V_{sat} = +14$ V | | $T/2$ | $= 0$ V | Falling edge transition | | $T/2$ to $T$ (negative half) | $< 0$ V | $-V_{sat} = -14$ V | | $T$ | $= 0$ V | Rising edge transition | **Transfer Characteristic:** - Vertical line at $V_{in} = 0$ V. - For $V_{in} < 0$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$. - For $V_{in} > 0$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$. > **(Marks Distribution: Circuit diagram — 2, Operation explanation — 2, Waveforms — 2, Transfer characteristic — 1)** #### Part (b) Solution **Step 1:** Calculate the angular frequency of the input sine wave. $$ \omega = 100\pi \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the input voltage at each specified time using $V_{in}(t) = 8\sin(100\pi t)$. - **At $t = 2$ ms:** $$ V_{in} = 8\sin(100\pi \times 0.002) = 8\sin(0.2\pi) = 8\sin(36°) \approx 8 \times 0.5878 \approx +4.70 \text{ V} $$ - **At $t = 5$ ms:** $$ V_{in} = 8\sin(100\pi \times 0.005) = 8\sin(0.5\pi) = 8 \times 1 = +8 \text{ V (peak)} $$ - **At $t = 12$ ms:** $$ V_{in} = 8\sin(100\pi \times 0.012) = 8\sin(1.2\pi) = 8\sin(216°) \approx 8 \times (-0.5878) \approx -4.70 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3:** Apply the non-inverting ZCD rule to determine the output. | Time | $V_{in}$ | Polarity | Output $V_o$ | |---|---|---|---| | $t = 2$ ms | $+4.70$ V | Positive | $+V_{sat} = +14$ V | | $t = 5$ ms | $+8.00$ V | Positive (peak) | $+V_{sat} = +14$ V | | $t = 12$ ms | $-4.70$ V | Negative | $-V_{sat} = -14$ V | > **(Marks Distribution: Setting up input equation — 2, Evaluating at given times — 3, Applying ZCD rule and final answer — 2)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Compare and contrast the inverting and non-inverting configurations of a Zero Crossing Detector. Draw the input-output waveforms for both cases. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A non-inverting ZCD uses op-amp 741 with $\pm 12$ V supplies. The op-amp has an open-loop gain of $2 \times 10^5$ and a slew rate of 0.5 V/µs. Calculate: - (i) The minimum input voltage change required to switch the output state. **(2 Marks)** - (ii) The maximum input sine-wave frequency for a clean square-wave output, if the input amplitude is 5 V. **(3 Marks)** - (iii) The time taken for the output to slew from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$. **(2 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) Solution **Non-Inverting ZCD:** Input fed to the non-inverting (+) terminal; reference (0 V) at the inverting (−) terminal. **Inverting ZCD:** Input fed to the inverting (−) terminal; reference (0 V) at the non-inverting (+) terminal. | Parameter | Non-Inverting ZCD | Inverting ZCD | |---|---|---| | Input applied to | Pin 3 (+) | Pin 2 (−) | | Reference at | Pin 2 (−) | Pin 3 (+) | | When $V_{in} > 0$ | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ | | When $V_{in} < 0$ | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ | | Output phase w.r.t. input | In-phase (0° shift) | Inverted (180° shift) | | Use case | General square-wave generation | Phase inversion applications | **Waveforms:** Both produce square waves of the same frequency as the input sine, but they are **180° out of phase** with each other. > **(Marks Distribution: Configuration explanation — 3, Waveform comparison — 2, Tabular comparison — 2)** #### Part (b) Solution **Given:** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs, supplies $\pm 12$ V, $V_p = 5$ V. **Step 1:** Determine practical $V_{sat}$ (assume $V_{sat} = V_{CC} - 1$ V). $$ V_{sat} = 12 - 1 = 11 \text{ V} $$ **(i) Minimum input voltage to switch:** $$ \Delta V_{in,min} = \frac{V_{sat}}{A_{OL}} = \frac{11}{2 \times 10^5} = 55 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 55 \,\mu\text{V} $$ **(ii) Maximum input frequency for clean square output:** $$ f_{in,max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi \cdot V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{10\pi} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz} $$ **(iii) Slew time from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$:** $$ t_{tr} = \frac{2 V_{sat}}{SR} = \frac{2 \times 11}{0.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{22}{5 \times 10^{5}} = 44 \,\mu\text{s} $$ > **(Marks Distribution: Saturation calculation — 1, (i) — 2, (ii) — 3, (iii) — 2)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing ZCD with general comparator:** Many students write $V_{ref}$ as some arbitrary voltage in the answer. In a ZCD, the reference **must be 0 V** (ground). Writing $V_{ref} = 2$ V or similar will cost full marks on the definition. > > 2. **Forgetting the open-loop condition:** Examiners specifically look for the phrase "**no feedback**" or "**open-loop configuration**." Drawing a circuit with a feedback resistor will be marked wrong. > > 3. **Wrong output polarity for inverting vs non-inverting:** When asked about an inverting ZCD with $V_{in} > 0$, students often write $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (the non-inverting rule). Memorize: **inverting ZCD inverts the output**. > > 4. **Confusing $V_{sat}$ with $V_{CC}$:** $V_{sat} \neq V_{CC}$. Always subtract 1–2 V for a realistic BJT op-amp like 741. Using $V_{sat} = V_{CC}$ gives a slightly overestimated answer. > > 5. **Ignoring slew rate in frequency calculations:** The maximum frequency formula $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ is a **favourite KTU 14-mark sub-part**. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks. > > 6. **Not drawing both waveforms together:** When asked for input-output waveforms, examiners expect **time-aligned plots** of the sine and the resulting square wave on the same time axis. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **ZCD = op-amp comparator in open-loop with $V_{ref} = 0$ V.** *(Most important single line.)* - **No feedback network** — the op-amp gain is effectively infinite at the threshold. - **Non-inverting ZCD:** Input at Pin 3; output is **in-phase** with the input (positive input → positive output). - **Inverting ZCD:** Input at Pin 2; output is **180° phase-shifted** (positive input → negative output). - **Output is always one of two levels:** $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ (never 0 V, except at the precise transition instant). - **Practical $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1$ to $2$ V** for the 741 op-amp. - **Threshold voltage** $V_{th} = V_{ref} = 0$ V. - **Minimum detectable input:** $\Delta V_{in,min} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{A_{OL}}$ (typically tens of microvolts). - **Switching speed is limited by the slew rate**, not the gain-bandwidth product, for large output swings. - **Slew time:** $t_{tr} = \dfrac{2V_{sat}}{SR}$. - **Maximum input frequency:** $f_{in,max} \approx \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$. - **Primary applications:** Square-wave generation, phase meters, frequency counters, TRIAC firing circuits, line-sync circuits. - **Major limitation:** Noise causes **multiple spurious transitions** near $V_{in} = 0$ — solved by **Schmitt Trigger ZCD** (positive feedback / hysteresis, covered later). - **KTU Board Keyword Checklist (must appear in answers):** *open-loop, comparator, zero-voltage reference, saturation, transfer characteristic, slew rate, threshold voltage, square wave, inverting, non-inverting.*

topics.title: Window Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Window Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Window Detector** (also known as a **Window Comparator** or **Voltage Window Discriminator**) is an analog electronic circuit that uses two precision comparators to determine whether an unknown input voltage ($V_{in}$) lies within a predefined voltage band, commonly termed a *"window."* This window is bounded by an **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UTH}$)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LTH}$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> A window detector is essentially a dual-comparator circuit. Unlike a single comparator (which detects *one* threshold), the window detector monitors *two* thresholds simultaneously, classifying the input into one of three logical states: **LOW (below window), IN-WINDOW, HIGH (above window).**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Elevator Floor Check"

Imagine an elevator in a high-rise building that is programmed to stop only when the requested floor lies between the **5th floor** ($V_{LTH}$) and the **10th floor** ($V_{UTH}$):

- If you press **Floor 3** ($V_{in} < V_{LTH}$) → The elevator ignores the call (Output = **LOW / OFF**).
- If you press **Floor 7** ($V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$) → The elevator opens its doors (Output = **HIGH / ON**).
- If you press **Floor 15** ($V_{in} > V_{UTH}$) → The elevator again ignores the call (Output = **LOW / OFF**).

The "window" here is the floor range 5 to 10, exactly analogous to the voltage band $V_{LTH}$ to $V_{UTH}$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Reference Voltage Source ($V_{REF}$):** A stable DC source, typically a Zener diode (e.g., **5.1 V** or **6.2 V**) or a precision band-gap reference (e.g., **LM4040**, **TL431**).
- **Comparator Supply:** Standard op-amps like **LM741**, **LM324**, or **LM339** quad comparators operating at $\pm V_{CC}$ (commonly $\pm 9V$ or $\pm 12V$).
- **Window Width:** $\Delta V = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$ (expressed in **Volts**).
- **Hysteresis Bandwidth (when added):** Typically **50 mV to 200 mV** to suppress noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Functional Rule:** A window detector outputs a logic **HIGH** (or **LOW**, depending on topology) **if and only if** $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$. Outside this band, the output transitions to the opposite state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology and Operational Logic

A standard window detector is constructed using the following building blocks:

1. **Two Op-Amp Comparators** (e.g., IC 741).
2. **A Reference Voltage Divider Network** that derives $V_{UTH}$ and $V_{LTH}$ from a single $V_{REF}$.
3. **A Digital Logic Gate (AND or OR)** to combine the two comparator outputs into a single decision bit.
4. **Pull-up Resistors** (essential for open-collector comparator ICs like LM339).

The two comparators are configured as follows:

- **Comparator A (Upper Limit Detector):**
  - Non-inverting input: $V_{in}$
  - Inverting input: $V_{UTH}$
  - Function: Output = **HIGH** when $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$.

- **Comparator B (Lower Limit Detector):**
  - Non-inverting input: $V_{LTH}$
  - Inverting input: $V_{in}$
  - Function: Output = **HIGH** when $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$.

The outputs $A$ and $B$ are fed into an **AND gate** to produce a unified output.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Design

- **Why two comparators?** A single comparator can only answer a single question: *"Is $V_{in}$ greater than $V_{REF}$?"* A window detector must answer a *band-limited* question: *"Is $V_{in}$ between $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$?"* — requiring two simultaneous comparisons.
- **Why an AND gate?** For the output to be **HIGH** (signaling "in-window"), **both** conditions must be satisfied: $V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ **AND** $V_{in} > V_{LTH}$.
- **Why reference voltage dividers?** They allow the designer to set precise, stable thresholds using a single high-accuracy $V_{REF}$, reducing component count.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbolic Form | Description / Condition | Unit |
|---|----------------------|---------------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Lower Threshold | $V_{LTH} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{REF}$ | Set by lower divider | **V (Volts)** |
| 2 | Upper Threshold | $V_{UTH} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{REF}$ | Set by upper divider | **V (Volts)** |
| 3 | Window Width | $\Delta V = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$ | Width of acceptance band | **V (Volts)** |
| 4 | In-Window Condition | $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$ | Output = HIGH (AND topology) | **V (Volts)** |
| 5 | Out-of-Window Low | $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ | Output = LOW | **V (Volts)** |
| 6 | Out-of-Window High | $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ | Output = LOW | **V (Volts)** |
| 7 | Hysteresis Voltage | $V_{H} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Added via positive feedback | **V (Volts)** |
| 8 | Op-Amp Slew Rate Constraint | $SR \geq \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t}$ | For pulse response | **V/$\mu$s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe Rule:** All table cell contents use $\vert$ delimiters (or $\mid$) where absolute value is needed, ensuring zero markdown parsing conflicts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Battery Health Monitors:** Detecting if a Li-ion cell voltage lies within the safe operating range (**3.0 V to 4.2 V**).
- **Over/Under-Voltage Protection Relays:** Industrial three-phase power line protection.
- **Automotive Sensor Testing:** Validating that a sensor output (e.g., MAP sensor) is within calibration limits.
- **Audio Signal Limiter Clamps:** Ensuring peak audio voltages do not exceed ADC safe levels.
- **IC Tester ICs (like the LM339):** Used extensively in the ATE (Automatic Test Equipment) industry for Go/No-Go testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Full Truth Table

The table below is the **golden reference** for KTU exams. Memorize its logic.

| Condition | $V_{in}$ Range | Comparator A Output ($V_{in}$ vs $V_{UTH}$) | Comparator B Output ($V_{LTH}$ vs $V_{in}$) | AND Gate Output | Indicator State |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **1** | $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ | **HIGH** (since $V_{in} < V_{UTH}$) | **HIGH** (since $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$) | **HIGH** | In-Window (XOR logic) |
| **2** | $V_{in} = V_{LTH}$ | HIGH | LOW (threshold edge) | **LOW** | Edge |
| **3** | $V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ | **HIGH** | **LOW** | **HIGH** | **In-Window** |
| **4** | $V_{in} = V_{UTH}$ | LOW (threshold edge) | LOW | **LOW** | Edge |
| **5** | $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ | **LOW** | **LOW** | **LOW** | Out-of-Window High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Convention:** The above table assumes the **INVERTED-WINDOW topology** (OR gate-based). For a **standard non-inverting window** (AND gate-based with inputs swapped as described in §2.1), the AND gate output column would be HIGH **only in Condition 3**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Reference Voltage Divider

**Problem Statement:** Design a window detector with $V_{REF} = 10\text{ V}$, $V_{LTH} = 3\text{ V}$, and $V_{UTH} = 7\text{ V}$. Find the resistor values if $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Establish the divider equations:**

$$V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$:**

$$V_{UTH} = 10 \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{100\text{ k}\Omega} = 7\text{ V}$$

Solving for $(R_1 + R_2)$:

$$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{7 \cdot 100\text{ k}\Omega}{10} = 70\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_3$ using the lower threshold equation:**

Since $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_3 = 100\text{ k}\Omega - 70\text{ k}\Omega = 30\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Verify $V_{LTH}$ using $R_2 + R_3$:**

$$V_{LTH} = 10 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = 10 \cdot \frac{30\text{ k}\Omega}{R_2 + 30\text{ k}\Omega} = 3\text{ V}$$

Rearranging:

$$3(R_2 + 30\text{ k}\Omega) = 300\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$3R_2 + 90\text{ k}\Omega = 300\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$3R_2 = 210\text{ k}\Omega \implies R_2 = 70\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_1$:**

$$R_1 = 70\text{ k}\Omega - R_2 = 70\text{ k}\Omega - 70\text{ k}\Omega = 0\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Re-evaluate (since $R_1 = 0$ collapses a branch):**

The above implies $R_1$ is not necessary. The simpler interpretation: $V_{UTH} = 7\text{ V}$ is tapped directly between $(R_1 + R_2)$ and $R_3$. If we use a **two-resistor string** ($R_a$ from $V_{REF}$ to $V_{UTH}$ node, and $R_b$ from $V_{UTH}$ node to ground):

$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b} = 7\text{ V} \implies R_b = 0.7(R_a + R_b)$$

For $R_a + R_b = 100\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_b = 70\text{ k}\Omega,\quad R_a = 30\text{ k}\Omega$$

A second divider string of identical ratio but lower absolute resistance is then used to derive $V_{LTH} = 3\text{ V}$.

**Step 7 — Final sanity check on window width:**

$$\Delta V = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = 7\text{ V} - 3\text{ V} = 4\text{ V}$$

This is the **operating band** the circuit will accept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of a window detector. This is the safest Mermaid representation as it avoids the complexity of physical circuit drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVIN[INPUT V_in] --> compA
    nodeVREF[VOLTAGE REFERENCE V_REF] --> divNet
    divNet[VOLTAGE DIVIDER NETWORK] --> nodeVUTH[V_UTH Upper Threshold]
    divNet --> nodeVLTH[V_LTH Lower Threshold]
    nodeVUTH --> compA
    nodeVLTH --> compB
    compA[COMPARATOR A Upper Limit] --> gateAnd
    compB[COMPARATOR B Lower Limit] --> gateAnd
    gateAnd[AND GATE Logic Combiner] --> outNode
    outNode[DECISION OUTPUT V_out]
    nodeVIN --> compB

    style nodeVIN fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeVREF fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style divNet fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style compA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style compB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style gateAnd fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Input-Output Transfer Characteristic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_in less than V_LTH] -->|LOW| O[OUTPUT V_out]
    B[V_LTH less than V_in less than V_UTH] -->|HIGH| O
    C[V_in greater than V_UTH] -->|LOW| O
    style A fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Schematic Interpretation

The physical circuit consists of:

- **A Resistor Divider String:** $R_a$ and $R_b$ between $V_{REF}$ and ground, with the midpoint tapped to provide $V_{UTH}$. A similar string provides $V_{LTH}$.
- **Op-Amp 1 (Comparator A):** $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting (+) input; $V_{UTH}$ at the inverting (-) input.
- **Op-Amp 2 (Comparator B):** $V_{in}$ at the inverting (-) input; $V_{LTH}$ at the non-inverting (+) input.
- **Output Stage:** The two op-amp outputs feed a 2-input AND gate (e.g., IC 7408). LEDs (with current-limiting resistors of $\approx 330\ \Omega$) may be added for visual indication.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a window detector. State any two of its practical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A window detector is a comparator-based circuit that produces a defined output state only when the input voltage $V_{in}$ lies within a specific voltage band bounded by an upper threshold $V_{UTH}$ and a lower threshold $V_{LTH}$. It essentially performs a "go/no-go" test on $V_{in}$.

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Battery voltage monitoring** — verifying Li-ion cells stay within **3.0 V to 4.2 V**.
2. **Over/under-voltage protection relays** in industrial power systems.
3. **Sensor calibration validation** in automotive ECUs.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between a single comparator and a window detector in terms of thresholds detected.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single Comparator | Window Detector |
|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| Thresholds | One reference | Two references ($V_{LTH}$, $V_{UTH}$) |
| Comparators Used | 1 op-amp | 2 op-amps + logic gate |
| Output States | 2 (HIGH/LOW) | 3 (Below, In-Window, Above) |
| Decision Type | "Greater than" check | "Within band" check |

*(3 marks — comparison table format)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a window detector using op-amps and explain its operation with a suitable truth table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a window detector circuit to monitor a **12 V lead-acid battery** with $V_{LTH} = 10.5\text{ V}$ and $V_{UTH} = 13.8\text{ V}$. Assume $V_{REF} = 15\text{ V}$. Calculate the resistor values for the divider network if total series resistance is $50\text{ k}\Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The window detector uses two op-amp comparators (e.g., LM741):

- **Op-Amp A1:** $V_{in}$ → non-inverting input; $V_{UTH}$ → inverting input. Output $V_{OA}$ goes HIGH when $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$.
- **Op-Amp A2:** $V_{in}$ → inverting input; $V_{LTH}$ → non-inverting input. Output $V_{OB}$ goes HIGH when $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$.
- **AND gate** combines $V_{OA}$ and $V_{OB}$.

**Truth Table (AND topology):**

| $V_{in}$ | $V_{OA}$ | $V_{OB}$ | $V_{out}$ (AND) |
|-----------|----------|----------|-----------------|
| $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ | HIGH | HIGH | **HIGH** |
| $V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ | HIGH | LOW | **LOW** |
| $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ | LOW | LOW | **LOW** |

> **Note:** This is the *active-low window* (OR gate) variant. For active-HIGH window, swap comparator input connections and use AND gate.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

Given: $V_{REF} = 15\text{ V}$, $V_{LTH} = 10.5\text{ V}$, $V_{UTH} = 13.8\text{ V}$, $R_{total} = 50\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 1:** Calculate $V_{UTH}$ resistor ratio.

$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b} = 13.8\text{ V}$$

$$\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{13.8}{15} = 0.92$$

$$R_b = 0.92 \times 50\text{ k}\Omega = 46\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_a = 50 - 46 = 4\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2:** For the $V_{LTH}$ divider, use a separate string (say, $R_c$ and $R_d$):

$$\frac{R_d}{R_c + R_d} = \frac{10.5}{15} = 0.70$$

Assuming the lower divider also sums to $50\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_d = 0.70 \times 50 = 35\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_c = 15\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Verify window width:

$$\Delta V = 13.8 - 10.5 = 3.3\text{ V}$$

This is well-suited for a 12 V lead-acid battery's healthy float range.

*(7 marks: 3 for $V_{UTH}$ derivation, 2 for $V_{LTH}$, 2 for window width and conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a window detector with the help of a block diagram. Compare it with a Schmitt trigger. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A window detector has $V_{LTH} = 2.5\text{ V}$ and $V_{UTH} = 5.5\text{ V}$. Sketch the input-output transfer characteristic when $V_{in}$ varies from 0 to 7 V. List the output for: (i) $V_{in} = 1\text{ V}$, (ii) $V_{in} = 4\text{ V}$, (iii) $V_{in} = 6.5\text{ V}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A window detector consists of:
- A **reference voltage source** $V_{REF}$.
- A **voltage divider** generating $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$.
- **Two comparators** evaluating $V_{in}$ against both thresholds.
- A **logic gate** (AND/OR) producing a unified output.

**Comparison with Schmitt Trigger:**

| Feature | Window Detector | Schmitt Trigger |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Function | Detects if $V_{in}$ is in a band | Detects single threshold with hysteresis |
| Comparators | 2 | 1 |
| Hysteresis | Optional | Inherent |
| Output | 3 states (LOW, IN-WINDOW, HIGH) | 2 states (HIGH/LOW) |

*(7 marks: 3 for block diagram explanation, 4 for comparison.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Transfer Characteristic Sketch (description):**

The output $V_{out}$ is:
- **LOW** for $0 \leq V_{in} < 2.5\text{ V}$
- **HIGH** for $2.5 \leq V_{in} \leq 5.5\text{ V}$ (window region)
- **LOW** for $5.5\text{ V} < V_{in} \leq 7\text{ V}$

The plot is a "rectangular pulse" with edges at $V_{LTH} = 2.5\text{ V}$ and $V_{UTH} = 5.5\text{ V}$.

**Output for the given inputs:**
- (i) $V_{in} = 1\text{ V}$: **$V_{out} = \text{LOW}$** (below window)
- (ii) $V_{in} = 4\text{ V}$: **$V_{out} = \text{HIGH}$** (inside window)
- (iii) $V_{in} = 6.5\text{ V}$: **$V_{out} = \text{LOW}$** (above window)

*(7 marks: 3 for sketch description, 4 for the three numerical outputs.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The most common mistake** is incorrectly connecting the comparator inputs. Students often wire $V_{in}$ to the inverting terminal of *both* comparators, which causes the logic gate to never trigger correctly. **Always verify the comparator polarity** against the truth table.
> 2. **Pull-up resistors are mandatory** for LM339 (open-collector outputs). Forgetting them yields a permanently LOW output — a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Do not confuse $V_{REF}$ with the threshold voltages.** $V_{REF}$ is the source, while $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$ are derived from it. Many students write $V_{UTH} = V_{REF}$ directly, losing **1 to 2 marks**.
> 4. **State the window condition explicitly:** $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$. KTU examiners award **partial credit** for this formal inequality.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A window detector is a **dual-comparator circuit** that outputs a specific logic state when $V_{in}$ lies within a band defined by $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$.
- **Three operational regions:** Below, Inside, Above the window.
- **Core building blocks:** Two op-amp comparators + reference voltage divider + AND/OR logic gate.
- **Key formulas:** $V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}$, $V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_d}{R_c + R_d}$, $\Delta V = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$.
- **Standard IC choices:** LM741, LM324, LM339 for comparators; 7408 (AND), 7432 (OR) for logic.
- **Pull-up resistors:** Required for open-collector comparators (typically **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ**).
- **Hysteresis option:** Positive feedback via $R_f$ adds noise immunity; the modified threshold becomes $V_{T}^{\pm} = V_{TH} \pm \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_f} (V_{OH} - V_{OL})$.
- **Applications:** Battery monitoring, voltage protection relays, sensor validation, ADC input clamping.
- **KTU trap to avoid:** Always draw the **complete circuit diagram with labeled component values** — partial diagrams cost up to **3 marks** in the 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample and Hold Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit** is an essential analog building block used in data acquisition systems, Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs), and signal processing applications. It is a **switched-capacitor analog memory device** whose function is to rapidly sample an analog input signal and then hold (freeze) its instantaneous voltage value constant for a sufficient duration, so that the subsequent stage (typically an ADC) can perform a stable and accurate conversion.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1: Linear Integrated Circuits and Their Applications), the Sample and Hold Circuit forms the critical interface between a continuously varying **analog domain** and the **discrete-time/digital domain**. It essentially converts a continuous-time analog waveform into a **staircase-discrete-time waveform** without losing amplitude fidelity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, the Sample and Hold Circuit is studied as a foundational Op-Amp application building block. It is directly linked to Data Acquisition Systems (DAS) and is a prerequisite for understanding Successive Approximation (SAR) ADCs and Flash ADCs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **photographer capturing a still photograph of a fast-moving race car**:

- The **race car** is the *continuous, rapidly changing analog input signal* $V_{in}(t)$.
- The **camera shutter** represents the *electronic switch* (typically a MOSFET or JFET).
- The **photographic film/sensor** acts as the *holding capacitor* $C_H$ that "remembers" the captured image.
- The **photograph itself** is the *held output voltage* $V_{out}$.

When the shutter is open (sample mode), the film records the current scene. When the shutter closes (hold mode), the image is **frozen** in time, no matter how fast the car keeps moving. The photographer (the downstream ADC) now has unlimited time to study the frozen image (the held voltage) and convert it into digital data.

## 1.3 Core Functional Modes

A Sample and Hold circuit operates in **two distinct temporal modes**:

1. **Sample Mode (Tracking Mode):** The analog switch is **CLOSED (ON)**. The output $V_{out}$ faithfully tracks the input $V_{in}$, and the holding capacitor $C_H$ charges to the instantaneous value of $V_{in}$.
2. **Hold Mode:** The analog switch is **OPEN (OFF)**. The capacitor $C_H$ is isolated from the input source, and the output voltage $V_{out}$ remains **constant** at the last sampled value, ideally independent of further input variations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics:** The two most critical figures of merit are the **acquisition time** (how fast the capacitor charges during sample mode) and the **droop rate** (how slowly the capacitor discharges during hold mode due to leakage currents).

## 1.4 Why is a Sample and Hold Circuit Necessary?

Without an S/H circuit, an ADC would be directly exposed to a rapidly changing input. This causes three critical errors:

- **Aperture Error:** Even high-speed ADCs require a finite conversion time $T_{conv}$. If the input changes by $\Delta V = (dV_{in}/dt) \cdot T_{conv}$ during this window, the digital output becomes ambiguous.
- **Dynamic Range Limitation:** High-frequency input components would exceed the ADC's input bandwidth.
- **Code Jitter:** Successive conversions of the same DC input produce different digital codes due to input ripple.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A canonical Sample and Hold Circuit consists of **three fundamental sub-blocks**:

1. **Analog Switch (S):** A voltage-controlled semiconductor switch, typically implemented using a **MOSFET**, **JFET**, or a dedicated analog switch IC (e.g., CD4066, ADG419). Driven by a digital control signal.
2. **Holding Capacitor ($C_H$):** A high-quality capacitor (often polypropylene, Teflon, or MOS-type) with very low leakage, low dielectric absorption, and high insulation resistance.
3. **Input/Output Buffer Amplifiers:** High-input-impedance unity-gain buffers (using Op-Amps like LF356, OP-07) to prevent loading of the source during sample mode and to provide low output impedance during hold mode.

### 2.1.1 Sample Mode (Tracking Phase) — The "Why" and "How"

**Why:** The switch must close to allow the capacitor to charge rapidly to the input voltage.

**How:**
- The control logic drives the MOSFET gate HIGH (for an N-channel enhancement MOSFET).
- Channel resistance $R_{on}$ (typically 10 $\Omega$ to 1 k$\Omega$) appears between drain and source.
- The capacitor $C_H$ charges through this ON-resistance.
- The charging follows the classical **first-order RC transient equation**.
- The buffer amplifier (input buffer) presents high input impedance, isolating the source from the switch.
- The output buffer presents low output impedance, driving the load without discharging $C_H$.

### 2.1.2 Hold Mode — The "Why" and "How"

**Why:** The switch must open to isolate the capacitor from the input, freezing the voltage.

**How:**
- The control logic drives the MOSFET gate LOW (below threshold voltage $V_{th}$).
- The switch becomes an open circuit (off-resistance $R_{off} > 10^{10}$ $\Omega$).
- Ideally, no current flows into or out of $C_H$.
- The voltage across $C_H$ remains constant at the last sampled value $V_{out}(t_{hold})$.
- Real-world leakage paths (MOSFET off-current, Op-Amp input bias current, PCB leakage) cause slow discharge → **droop**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **every equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Sample and Hold Circuit topic.

| # | Parameter Name | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charging Time Constant | $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ | $R_{on}$ in ohms ($\Omega$), $C_H$ in farads (F), $\tau$ in seconds (s) | Time constant of the sample-mode RC network. |
| 2 | Acquisition Time (n-bit accuracy) | $T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2)$ | $n$ = ADC resolution (bits) | Time required for $C_H$ to settle within $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB of the final value. |
| 3 | Practical Acquisition Time | $T_A \approx 7 \cdot \tau$ to $10 \cdot \tau$ | — | Rule of thumb for 8 to 10-bit accuracy (settling within 0.1%). |
| 4 | Hold Step Voltage (Switch Feedthrough) | $\Delta V_H = \dfrac{C_{gd}}{C_H} \cdot (V_{G(on)} - V_{th})$ | $C_{gd}$ = gate-drain capacitance, $V_{G(on)}$ = gate drive voltage | Unwanted voltage jump transferred to $C_H$ via gate-to-drain capacitive coupling when the switch opens. |
| 5 | Droop Rate | $\dfrac{dV_H}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_H}$ | $I_L$ = total leakage current (A), droop in V/s | Rate at which held voltage decays during hold mode. |
| 6 | Droop Voltage | $\Delta V_{droop} = \dfrac{I_L \cdot T_{hold}}{C_H}$ | $T_{hold}$ = hold duration (s) | Total voltage lost during the entire hold interval. |
| 7 | Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$) | $t_{ap} = t_{hold-command} - t_{actual-hold}$ | measured in nanoseconds (ns) | Delay between the digital hold command and the actual opening of the switch. |
| 8 | Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter) | $\Delta t_{ap} = t_{ap,max} - t_{ap,min}$ | measured in picoseconds (ps) | Sample-to-sample variation in aperture time; critical for high-frequency sampling. |
| 9 | Feedthrough Attenuation | $\alpha_{FT} = \dfrac{R_{off}}{R_{off} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_H}}$ | unitless ratio | Ratio of input signal leaking to output during hold mode (ideally $\to 0$). |
| 10 | Settling Error | $e_{ss} = e^{-T_{sample}/\tau}$ | ratio | Fractional error at the end of sample duration $T_{sample}$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Markdown Note:** All absolute value and ratio bars in the above table have been intentionally written using LaTeX `\dfrac`, `\cdot`, and fraction notation. The vertical pipe character `\`vert\` or `\mid` has been avoided to prevent breaking the markdown table rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Sample and Hold circuit is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG and EEG digitizers where microvolt signals must be frozen precisely.
- **Oscilloscopes (Digital Storage Oscilloscopes — DSO):** Capturing single-shot transient events.
- **Audio Engineering:** Sampling analog audio at 44.1 kHz for CD-quality digital storage.
- **Telecommunications:** Baseband signal sampling in software-defined radios (SDRs).
- **Industrial Control Systems:** Multiplexed data acquisition from hundreds of sensors sharing one ADC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **fully exhaustive, line-by-line mathematical derivations** of every critical Sample and Hold Circuit equation. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Acquisition Time Equation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the time $T_A$ required for the holding capacitor $C_H$ to charge from an initial voltage of zero to within $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB (Least Significant Bit) of a final DC input voltage $V_{in}$.

### Step 1: Write the Basic RC Charging Equation

When the switch closes, the capacitor charges through the ON-resistance $R_{on}$. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL), the loop equation is:

$$V_{in} = i(t) \cdot R_{on} + V_C(t)$$

### Step 2: Express Current as Function of Capacitor Voltage

The current charging the capacitor is related to its voltage by the fundamental capacitor relationship $i(t) = C_H \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$. Substituting:

$$V_{in} = R_{on} \cdot C_H \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t)$$

### Step 3: Define the Time Constant

We define the **time constant** $\tau$ of this first-order RC network as:

$$\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$$

### Step 4: Formulate and Solve the First-Order Linear ODE

The governing differential equation becomes:

$$\tau \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t) = V_{in}$$

This is a first-order linear ordinary differential equation with constant coefficients. Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{\tau} V_C(t) = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\tau}$$

The general solution is the sum of the **steady-state (particular) solution** and the **transient (homogeneous) solution**:

$$V_C(t) = V_{C,particular} + V_{C,homogeneous}$$

The particular solution is $V_{C,p} = V_{in}$ (as $t \to \infty$, the capacitor voltage equals the input).

The homogeneous solution is $V_{C,h}(t) = A \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$.

Thus the complete solution, with initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$, becomes:

$$V_C(t) = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

### Step 5: Apply the $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB Settling Criterion

For an n-bit ADC, the size of **1 LSB** in voltage terms is:

$$1 \text{ LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale input range. For the capacitor voltage to settle to within $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB of the final value $V_{in}$, we require:

$$V_{in} - V_C(T_A) = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute and Solve for $T_A$

Inserting the expression for $V_C(T_A)$:

$$V_{in} - V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T_A/\tau}\right) = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

$$V_{in} \cdot e^{-T_A/\tau} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

$$e^{-T_A/\tau} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1} \cdot V_{in}}$$

In the worst case, we take $V_{in} = V_{FS}$ (full-scale swing), giving:

$$e^{-T_A/\tau} = \dfrac{1}{2^{n+1}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T_A}{\tau} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2^{n+1}}\right) = -(n+1) \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2)}$$

### Step 7: Numerical Verification

For an 8-bit system with $\tau = 1 \text{ }\mu s$:

$$T_A = 9 \times 1 \text{ }\mu s \times 0.693 = 6.237 \text{ }\mu s$$

This validates the common rule-of-thumb that acquisition requires approximately **7 to 10 time constants** for 8 to 10-bit accuracy.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Droop Rate

**Problem Statement:** Derive the rate at which the held voltage decays during the hold mode due to the total leakage current $I_L$.

### Step 1: Identify Leakage Sources

During hold mode, the switch is OFF. However, several parasitic leakage paths discharge $C_H$:
- MOSFET off-state leakage current $I_{DSS}$
- Op-Amp input bias current $I_{B}$ of the output buffer
- PCB surface leakage current
- Capacitor dielectric absorption reversal current

The total leakage current is $I_L$.

### Step 2: Apply the Capacitor Discharge Equation

For a capacitor discharging with a constant current $I_L$, the change in voltage is governed by:

$$I_L = -C_H \cdot \dfrac{dV_H(t)}{dt}$$

The negative sign indicates that current flowing OUT of the capacitor causes the voltage to DECREASE.

### Step 3: Solve for the Voltage Decay

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{dV_H(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_L}{C_H}$$

Integrating both sides with respect to time, with the initial condition $V_H(0) = V_{held}$:

$$V_H(t) = V_{held} - \dfrac{I_L}{C_H} \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Identify the Droop Rate Constant

The **droop rate** is defined as the magnitude of the rate of change of voltage:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dV_H}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_H}}$$

### Step 5: Total Droop Over Hold Duration

If the hold mode persists for duration $T_{hold}$, the total voltage lost is:

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \dfrac{I_L \cdot T_{hold}}{C_H}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example

Consider a sample-and-hold circuit with $C_H = 1 \text{ nF}$ and total leakage $I_L = 1 \text{ nA}$. Over a hold duration of $T_{hold} = 10 \text{ }\mu s$:

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}} = 10 \text{ mV}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hold Step (Switch Feedthrough)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the unwanted voltage jump that occurs at the instant the switch opens.

### Step 1: Identify the Physical Mechanism

When the MOSFET switch turns OFF, the gate voltage transitions rapidly from $V_{G(on)}$ to 0 V. The gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ couples this rapid voltage change through to the drain (which is connected to $C_H$), injecting a small charge packet.

### Step 2: Compute the Charge Injection

The charge transferred through $C_{gd}$ when the gate voltage changes by $\Delta V_G = V_{G(on)} - V_{th}$ is:

$$\Delta Q = C_{gd} \cdot (V_{G(on)} - V_{th})$$

### Step 3: Compute the Resulting Voltage Step

This injected charge redistributes across $C_H$, producing a voltage jump:

$$\Delta V_H = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C_H} = \dfrac{C_{gd}}{C_H} \cdot (V_{G(on)} - V_{th})$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_H = \dfrac{C_{gd}}{C_H} \cdot (V_{G(on)} - V_{th})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** To minimize the hold step, one must either **increase $C_H$** (which unfortunately increases acquisition time — a fundamental design trade-off) or **reduce $C_{gd}$** by choosing MOSFETs with low gate-drain overlap capacitance, or use series-switch topologies.

## 3.4 Summary of Derived Quantities

| Derived Quantity | Final Formula | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition Time | $T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2)$ | Determines maximum ADC sampling rate. |
| Droop Rate | $dV_H/dt = I_L / C_H$ | Limits maximum hold duration. |
| Hold Step | $\Delta V_H = (C_{gd}/C_H) \cdot \Delta V_G$ | Limits DC accuracy of held value. |
| Settling Error | $e_{ss} = e^{-T_{sample}/\tau}$ | Determines minimum sample duration. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides multiple visual aids rendered using Mermaid diagrams to illustrate the architecture, signal flow, and control logic of the Sample and Hold Circuit.

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Analog Input V in] --> B[Input Buffer Op-Amp]
    B --> C[Analog Switch MOSFET]
    C --> D[Holding Capacitor C H]
    D --> E[Output Buffer Op-Amp]
    E --> F[Analog Output V out]
    G[Digital Control Signal] --> C
    H[Clock Logic] --> G
    D -.-> I[ADC Downstream]
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **Analog Input** $V_{in}$ first enters an **Input Buffer** (high input-impedance Op-Amp) to prevent source loading.
- The buffered signal passes through the **Analog Switch** (controlled by the digital control signal).
- The **Holding Capacitor** $C_H$ charges (sample mode) or freezes (hold mode) the voltage.
- An **Output Buffer** provides low-impedance drive to the load.
- The output is fed to the downstream **ADC** for digitization.

## 4.2 Two-Phase Operational State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SampleMode: Power ON, Control Signal HIGH
    SampleMode: SAMPLE MODE
    SampleMode: Switch CLOSED, V out tracks V in
    SampleMode: Capacitor C H charges through R on
    HoldMode: HOLD MODE
    HoldMode: Switch OPEN, V out frozen at V sampled
    HoldMode: C H isolated, only leakage droop
    SampleMode --> HoldMode: Control Signal goes LOW
    HoldMode --> SampleMode: Control Signal goes HIGH
    HoldMode --> [*]: System Reset
    SampleMode --> [*]: System Reset
```

**Description of States:**
- **SAMPLE MODE** (yellow/active): The switch is closed; the output voltage exactly tracks the input.
- **HOLD MODE** (blue/passive): The switch is open; the output voltage is frozen and only decays at the droop rate.

## 4.3 Closed-Loop (Unity-Gain) S/H Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vin[Analog V in] --> A1[Input Buffer Op-Amp]
    A1 --> SW1[CMOS Switch S1]
    SW1 --> CH1[Hold Capacitor C H]
    CH1 --> A2[Output Buffer Op-Amp]
    A2 --> Vout[Analog V out]
    CH1 --> FB[Feedback Path]
    FB --> A1
    CTRL[Control Logic] --> SW1
    CTRL --> SW2[Dummy Switch S2]
    SW2 --> A2
    CTRL -.Clock Pulse.-> CTRL
```

**Description of Closed-Loop Topology:**
- The **feedback path** from the capacitor node back to the input buffer creates a unity-gain follower.
- This configuration **eliminates the hold step** because the Op-Amp adjusts the input buffer to compensate for charge injection.
- The **dummy switch S2** is half-sized and synchronized with S1; when S1 turns OFF, S2 turns ON, cancelling the charge injection by symmetry.

## 4.4 Signal Waveform Timeline (Visual Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph W1[Sample Mode Window]
    A1t[V in sinusoid varying] --> A1s[SAMPLE switch closed]
    A1s --> A1o[V out equals V in]
    end
    subgraph W2[Hold Mode Window]
    B1t[V in keeps changing] --> B1s[HOLD switch open]
    B1s --> B1o[V out constant frozen value]
    end
    subgraph W3[Next Sample Mode Window]
    C1t[V in has moved] --> C1s[SAMPLE switch closed]
    C1s --> C1o[V out jumps to new V in]
    end
```

**Description of Waveform:**
- During the **Sample Window**, the output waveform perfectly overlays the input.
- During the **Hold Window**, the output becomes a flat horizontal line at the last sampled value.
- At the start of the **next Sample Window**, the output jumps to the new input value (vertical transition).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Define the term "acquisition time" as applied to a Sample and Hold Circuit. What factors determine its value?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Acquisition time* $T_A$ is defined as the **time required for the holding capacitor $C_H$ to charge to its final value, within a specified accuracy band (typically $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB), after the analog switch is closed**. It is the minimum time the circuit must spend in the sample mode to ensure the output voltage accurately represents the input voltage before transitioning to the hold mode.

Mathematically:
$$T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2)$$

where $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ is the sample-mode time constant, $n$ is the resolution of the downstream ADC, and $R_{on}$ is the ON-resistance of the analog switch.

It is determined by:
1. The ON-resistance $R_{on}$ of the analog switch (typically 50 $\Omega$ to 1 k$\Omega$).
2. The capacitance value $C_H$ of the holding capacitor.
3. The required accuracy (number of bits $n$).

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: What is "droop" in a Sample and Hold Circuit? How is it related to leakage current?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Droop* is the **undesired gradual decay of the output voltage during the hold mode** of a Sample and Hold Circuit. Although the analog switch is open, the holding capacitor $C_H$ is not perfectly isolated; small leakage currents discharge it slowly, causing the held voltage to drift.

The droop rate is given by:
$$\dfrac{dV_H}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_H}$$

where $I_L$ is the total leakage current flowing out of the capacitor (sum of MOSFET off-leakage, Op-Amp input bias current, and PCB leakage). **The droop is directly proportional to the leakage current and inversely proportional to the capacitance $C_H$**. Over a hold duration $T_{hold}$, the total voltage lost is $\Delta V_{droop} = I_L \cdot T_{hold} / C_H$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **basic Sample and Hold Circuit using a MOSFET switch and a holding capacitor**. Discuss its working in both sample mode and hold mode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **acquisition time** of the Sample and Hold Circuit. Explain the design trade-off involved in selecting the value of the holding capacitor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (3 Marks):**

A basic Sample and Hold Circuit consists of:
1. An **N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET** acting as the analog switch. Its drain is connected to the analog input $V_{in}$ (typically through a buffer), and its source is connected to the holding capacitor $C_H$.
2. A **holding capacitor $C_H$** connected between the MOSFET source and ground. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across $C_H$.
3. A **digital control signal** applied to the MOSFET gate, deciding sample vs. hold.
4. A **unity-gain Op-Amp buffer** at the output to prevent loading of $C_H$.

**Working in Sample Mode (2 Marks):**
When the gate voltage $V_G > V_{th}$ (threshold voltage), the MOSFET turns ON. Its drain-to-source channel presents a small ON-resistance $R_{on}$ (typically 50–500 $\Omega$). The capacitor $C_H$ charges through this resistance, with the voltage following the first-order RC transient equation:
$$V_{out}(t) = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \text{ where } \tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$$

The output voltage tracks the input voltage faithfully.

**Working in Hold Mode (2 Marks):**
When the gate voltage $V_G < V_{th}$, the MOSFET turns OFF. Its drain-to-source resistance becomes very high ($R_{off} > 10^{10}$ $\Omega$), effectively isolating the capacitor from the input. The capacitor $C_H$ holds the last sampled voltage. Ideally, $V_{out}$ remains constant, but in practice it decays slowly due to leakage currents at the droop rate:
$$\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_L}{C_H}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of Acquisition Time (4 Marks):**

The detailed derivation has been provided in Section 3.1 above. The final result is:

$$T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2) \text{ where } \tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$$

This simplifies approximately to $T_A \approx (7 \text{ to } 10) \cdot R_{on} C_H$ for 8 to 10-bit accuracy.

**Design Trade-off in Selecting $C_H$ (3 Marks):**

There is a **fundamental conflict** in choosing $C_H$:

| If $C_H$ is too SMALL | If $C_H$ is too LARGE |
|---|---|
| Droop rate increases (poor hold stability) | Acquisition time increases (slower sampling) |
| Hold step $\Delta V_H$ is large (poor DC accuracy) | Op-Amp bandwidth may be insufficient |
| Noise pick-up increases (low capacitance picks up electromagnetic interference) | The circuit cannot track fast input changes |

**Optimal Choice:** $C_H$ is selected as the smallest value that still keeps the droop within the ADC's $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB error budget over the maximum hold duration $T_{hold, max}$:
$$C_H \geq \dfrac{I_L \cdot T_{hold, max}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \text{LSB}}$$

Typical values range from **100 pF to 10 nF**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** Explain the term **"aperture time" and "aperture uncertainty"** in a Sample and Hold Circuit. Why is aperture uncertainty critical in high-frequency sampling applications? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **closed-loop (feedback) Sample and Hold architecture**. How does it overcome the hold-step and droop limitations of the open-loop design? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Aperture Time Definition (2 Marks):**
The **aperture time** $t_{ap}$ is defined as the **delay between the instant the digital hold command is issued and the instant the analog switch actually opens (begins to isolate the capacitor)**. In practice, the MOSFET does not turn OFF instantaneously; there is a finite propagation delay through the gate-drive circuitry and a finite fall-time of the channel conductance.

Mathematically:
$$t_{ap} = t_{switch-actually-opens} - t_{hold-command-issued}$$

For a typical MOSFET switch, $t_{ap}$ ranges from **5 ns to 50 ns**.

**Aperture Uncertainty Definition (2 Marks):**
The **aperture uncertainty** (also called **aperture jitter** or **sampling jitter**) is defined as the **sample-to-sample variation in aperture time**:
$$\Delta t_{ap} = t_{ap, max} - t_{ap, min}$$

Typical values range from **10 ps to 1 ns** in high-performance S/H ICs.

**Why Critical in High-Frequency Sampling (3 Marks):**
If the input signal has a slew rate of $\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$, then the voltage error introduced by aperture jitter is:
$$\Delta V_{error} = \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$$

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$:
$$\Delta V_{error, peak} = 2\pi f A \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$$

At high frequencies $f$, even picosecond-level jitter translates to **millivolt errors**, severely degrading the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. For a 12-bit ADC, the SNR must exceed 74 dB, requiring:
$$\Delta t_{ap} < \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_{max} \cdot 2^{N}}$$

This is why aperture jitter is the **dominant performance bottleneck** in GHz-class sampling systems.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Closed-Loop Architecture Description (4 Marks):**

In a closed-loop (feedback) Sample and Hold Circuit, the holding capacitor $C_H$ is placed **inside the feedback loop of an Op-Amp**. The Op-Amp operates as an integrator during the sample mode, with its inverting input summing node forcing the capacitor to follow the input. During the hold mode, the feedback loop opens, and the capacitor voltage is buffered by a high-impedance unity-gain follower.

A typical closed-loop S/H uses a **two-amplifier architecture**:
- Amplifier A1 acts as an integrator during sample mode.
- Amplifier A2 is the output buffer.
- A **dummy switch** of half-size is added in the feedback path of A1 to cancel charge injection.

**How Closed-Loop Overcomes Limitations (3 Marks):**

1. **Hold Step Elimination:** The Op-Amp's high open-loop gain compensates for the charge injected by the switch opening. The dummy switch ensures symmetry, cancelling the feedthrough voltage.
2. **Reduced Droop:** The Op-Amp's extremely low input bias current (pA range) means that virtually no leakage current flows through $C_H$ during the hold mode.
3. **Improved Linearity:** The closed-loop feedback forces $V_{out}$ to be a precise linear function of $V_{in}$, eliminating the nonlinear ON-resistance of the MOSFET.
4. **Trade-off:** The closed-loop topology has a **limited bandwidth** because the Op-Amp must remain stable across both sample and hold transitions, reducing the maximum sampling rate compared to open-loop designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse *aperture time* (absolute delay) with *aperture uncertainty* (variation in delay). Examiners specifically test this distinction. Always remember: aperture uncertainty is the **standard deviation** or **peak-to-peak variation**, not the absolute delay.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** When deriving acquisition time, students frequently write $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ but forget to specify that this applies **only during sample mode**. During hold mode, the resistance becomes $R_{off} \to \infty$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Students incorrectly assume droop is caused by the capacitor itself. In reality, droop is caused by **leakage currents** flowing into or out of the capacitor node.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In closed-loop designs, students forget to mention the **stability-bandwidth trade-off**. The Op-Amp must have sufficient gain-bandwidth product (GBW) to settle within the sample window, or the closed-loop system becomes unstable.
> 5. **Marks Distribution Tip:** Always draw the circuit diagram (even if not explicitly asked). A neat labeled diagram is worth 1–2 marks and demonstrates clarity of thought.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Sample and Hold Circuit** = Analog switch (MOSFET) + Holding Capacitor ($C_H$) + Input/Output Buffers.
- **Two Modes:** Sample Mode (switch closed, $V_{out}$ tracks $V_{in}$) and Hold Mode (switch open, $V_{out}$ frozen).
- **Acquisition Time Formula:** $T_A = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2) \approx 7\tau$ to $10\tau$ for 8–10 bit accuracy.
- **Time Constant:** $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ — product of switch ON-resistance and holding capacitance.
- **Droop Rate Formula:** $dV_H/dt = I_L / C_H$ — leakage-driven decay during hold mode.
- **Hold Step Formula:** $\Delta V_H = (C_{gd}/C_H) \cdot (V_{G(on)} - V_{th})$ — capacitive feedthrough voltage jump.
- **Aperture Time** = absolute delay between hold command and actual switch opening.
- **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter)** = sample-to-sample variation in aperture time; critical at high frequencies.
- **Open-Loop S/H:** Simple, fast, but suffers from hold step and droop.
- **Closed-Loop S/H:** Uses Op-Amp feedback to cancel charge injection and droop; slower but more accurate.
- **Key Design Trade-off:** Larger $C_H$ reduces droop but increases acquisition time; smaller $C_H$ improves speed but degrades hold stability.
- **Typical Capacitor Values:** 100 pF to 10 nF (must be low-leakage, low-dielectric-absorption types like Teflon, polypropylene, or MOS-style).
- **Typical Switch ICs:** CD4066 (CMOS bilateral switch), ADG419 (analog switch), LF398 (monolithic S/H IC).
- **Application Domains:** ADCs (especially SAR and Flash), Digital Storage Oscilloscopes, Data Acquisition Systems, Audio Sampling, Telecommunications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rectifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition
A **rectifier** is an electronic circuit that converts a bidirectional (AC) input signal into a unidirectional (DC) output signal by allowing current to flow through it in only one direction. The conversion exploits the **unidirectional conduction property** of PN-junction diodes and is a fundamental building block of every DC power supply unit (PSU) used in electronic equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A rectifier by itself does **not** produce a pure DC waveform. The output of an ideal rectifier is a **pulsating DC** waveform (either half-wave or full-wave rectified sinusoid). To obtain a smooth, near-constant DC voltage, a **filter** (capacitor, inductor, or LC/π-section) is mandatory. The complete DC power supply chain is: **Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Voltage Regulator → Load**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy
Imagine a **one-way swing door** at the entrance of a cinema hall:
- People (electrons/current) can only **enter** from one side; they cannot return.
- If people walk past the door in both directions (AC), the door allows entry only during the forward motion, blocking the return — just like a diode.
- A **half-wave rectifier** is a single door: only one push direction is admitted per swing.
- A **full-wave rectifier** is a **double-door (revolving) system** that admits people on **both** swings, doubling throughput.
- The **filter capacitor** behaves like a **large waiting lounge** at the exit: it stores energy during peak inflow and releases it during low inflow, smoothing the crowd flow into a steady stream (pure DC).

## 1.3 Classification of Rectifiers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Categorization:**
> 1. **Uncontrolled Rectifiers** — use only diodes (silicon/Germanium). Output level is fixed by the AC source.
> 2. **Controlled Rectifiers** — use thyristors (SCRs), MOSFETs, or IGBTs. Firing angle α controls the DC output. (Covered in Power Electronics courses.)
>
> This module focuses entirely on **uncontrolled diode rectifiers**, which are sub-divided as below:

| S.No. | Type | Sub-Topology | Output Frequency |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR) | Single diode | $f_{in}$ |
| 2 | Full-Wave Rectifier (FWR) | Center-Tapped Transformer (CT-FWR) | $2f_{in}$ |
| 3 | Full-Wave Rectifier (FWR) | Bridge Rectifier (Graetz circuit) | $2f_{in}$ |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics
A rectifier is evaluated using five key **figures of merit** (each derived in §3):
1. **DC output voltage** ($V_{dc}$) — average value of the load voltage.
2. **RMS output voltage** ($V_{rms}$) — heating equivalent value.
3. **Ripple factor** ($\gamma$) — measure of residual AC content. **Lower is better.**
4. **Rectification efficiency** ($\eta$) — ratio of DC power delivered to AC power drawn.
5. **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF)** — how efficiently the transformer copper is used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)
- **Circuit:** A single diode D in series with the secondary of a transformer and load $R_L$.
- **Operation:**
  - During the **positive half-cycle** of $v_i = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, diode D is forward-biased → conducts → output follows the input.
  - During the **negative half-cycle**, D is reverse-biased → blocks → output is **zero**.
- **Output waveform:** A series of "positive half-sine humps" separated by zero gaps.

## 2.2 Full-Wave Rectifiers
### (a) Center-Tapped Transformer FWR (CT-FWR)
- Uses **two diodes** ($D_1$, $D_2$) and a transformer with a **center-tapped secondary**.
- Each half of the secondary drives one diode; the load is connected between the **common cathode junction** and the **center tap**.
- During the +ve half-cycle: $D_1$ conducts, $D_2$ is OFF.
- During the –ve half-cycle: $D_2$ conducts, $D_1$ is OFF.
- **Output frequency = 2 × input frequency** ⇒ ripple is easier to filter.

### (b) Bridge Rectifier (Graetz Circuit)
- Uses **four diodes** ($D_1$–$D_4$) arranged in a diamond bridge. **No center-tap is required.**
- During each half-cycle, **two diodes conduct in series**, delivering the **same polarity** to the load.
- **Advantages over CT-FWR:**
  - **No center-tap** ⇒ smaller, cheaper transformer.
  - **Higher TUF** (0.810 vs 0.692) ⇒ more efficient use of transformer copper.
  - **PIV per diode = $V_m$** (half of CT-FWR's $2V_m$) ⇒ suitable for high-voltage applications.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — memorize this table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table below, $V_m$ = peak voltage of the relevant secondary winding (i.e., peak of one half-secondary for CT-FWR; peak of the full secondary for HWR and Bridge). $R_L$ is the load resistance. $R_s$ is the diode + secondary winding resistance (used only for maximum-efficiency derivation).

| Parameter | Half-Wave (HWR) | Full-Wave (FWR — both types) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $V_{dc}$ (DC component) | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ |
| $V_{rms}$ (RMS value) | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| Ripple factor $\gamma$ | $1.21$ | $0.482$ |
| Rectification efficiency $\eta_{max}$ | $40.6\,\%$ | $81.2\,\%$ |
| Ripple frequency $f_r$ | $f_{in}$ | $2f_{in}$ |
| TUF (ideal) | $0.287$ | $0.692$ (CT) / $0.810$ (Bridge) |
| PIV per diode | $V_m$ | $2V_m$ (CT) / $V_m$ (Bridge) |
| Peak load current $I_m$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$ (Bridge) / $\dfrac{2V_m}{R_L}$ (CT) |
| RMS load current $I_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{I_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| Number of diodes | $1$ | $2$ (CT) / $4$ (Bridge) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility
- **Mobile/Laptop chargers, SMPS pre-regulators:** use bridge rectifiers + capacitive filters.
- **DC motor drives (small scale):** use full-wave rectifiers for high-power DC bus.
- **Signal demodulation (AM receivers):** use half-wave or full-wave envelope detectors.
- **Battery chargers:** full-wave rectifiers provide a high average charging current with low ripple.

## 2.5 Key Conceptual Points (Why & How)
- **Why does FWR have $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$?** Because the output is a full-wave rectified sinusoid (no zero gaps), doubling the area under one cycle.
- **Why is ripple factor of FWR lower?** Because $V_{rms}/V_{dc}$ is smaller for the full-wave waveform; the waveform is "purer" DC.
- **Why is PIV of CT-FWR = $2V_m$?** When one diode conducts, the other is reverse-biased by the **sum of both half-secondary voltages**.
- **Why is Bridge TUF highest?** Because the secondary of the bridge transformer carries sinusoidal current (unlike CT, where it carries half-wave current), reducing RMS heating and allowing the transformer to be rated lower.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below assume an **ideal diode** ($V_\gamma = 0$, $r_f = 0$) unless stated otherwise. $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is the secondary voltage of the transformer.

---

## 3.1 Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)

### 3.1.1 Instantaneous Output Voltage
The output across the load is a clipped sinusoid:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

### 3.1.2 Average (DC) Output Voltage $V_{dc}$
By definition, $V_{dc}$ is the mean value of the output over one full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0\, d(\omega t)\right] \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{0}^{\pi} \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-\cos\pi - (-\cos 0)\right] \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-(-1) - (-1)\right] \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\,[1+1] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\cdot 2 \\
\therefore V_{dc} &= \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$
$V_{rms}$ is the square root of the mean of the squared waveform.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{1-\cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\left[\omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi} \\
&= \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\,\pi = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4} \\
\therefore V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{2} = 0.5\, V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Ripple Factor $\gamma$
The **ripple factor** quantifies the residual AC content. Formally:

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1}$$

where $V_{ac} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^{2} - V_{dc}^{2}}$ is the RMS value of the **AC (ripple) component only**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} &= \frac{V_m/2}{V_m/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2} \\
\gamma &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{4} - 1} = \sqrt{2.4674 - 1} \\
&= \sqrt{1.4674} \approx 1.211
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Board Note:** The answer $\gamma = 1.21$ (or sometimes stated as 1.11 by older texts) is a "must-memorize" number for HWR.

### 3.1.5 Rectification Efficiency $\eta$
Efficiency = (DC power delivered to $R_L$) / (AC power drawn from the secondary).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dc} &= \frac{V_{dc}^{2}}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi^{2}R_L} \\
P_{ac} &= \frac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4 R_L} \\
\eta &= \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_m^{2}/(\pi^{2}R_L)}{V_m^{2}/(4R_L)} = \frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \\
&= 0.4053 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 40.6\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum possible efficiency of an HWR is 40.6 %.** It cannot be improved by changing $R_L$ (when diodes are ideal). The remaining 59.4 % is dissipated as ripple in $R_L$. This is a classic KTU 7-mark derivation question.

### 3.1.6 Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)
During the **negative half-cycle**, the diode is reverse-biased. The peak voltage appearing across the OFF diode equals the peak secondary voltage $V_m$.

$$\boxed{\text{PIV}_{HWR} = V_m}$$

---

## 3.2 Full-Wave Rectifier (FWR)

### 3.2.1 Instantaneous Output Voltage
The two diodes conduct alternately, giving a full-wave rectified waveform (no zero gaps):

$$v_o(t) = V_m \lvert\sin(\omega t)\rvert,\quad 0 \le \omega t \le 2\pi$$

Equivalently, integrating over the half-period $0 \to \pi$ and multiplying by 2:

### 3.2.2 Average (DC) Output Voltage $V_{dc}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{dc} &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{0}^{\pi} \\
&= \frac{V_m}{\pi}\,(2) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\, V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The factor $1/\pi$ (not $1/2\pi$) is used because the waveform repeats **twice per input cycle**, so the period is halved.

### 3.2.3 RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2} \\
\therefore V_{rms} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\, V_m
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Ripple Factor $\gamma$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} &= \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.1107 \\
\gamma &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} \\
&= \sqrt{0.2337} \approx 0.4821
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\gamma_{FWR} = 0.482$. This is **~2.5× lower** than HWR (1.21) — one of the principal reasons FWR is preferred.

### 3.2.5 Rectification Efficiency $\eta$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^{2}/R_L}{V_{rms}^{2}/R_L} = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2V_m/\pi}{V_m/\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\right)^{2} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} = 0.8106 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 81.06\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\eta_{FWR} = 81.2\,\%$ — exactly **double** the HWR efficiency.

### 3.2.6 PIV (Center-Tapped vs Bridge)

**CT-FWR:** When $D_1$ is ON, $D_2$ is OFF. The reverse voltage across $D_2$ is the sum of the voltages of both half-secondaries.

$$\boxed{\text{PIV}_{CT\text{-}FWR} = 2V_m}$$

**Bridge:** When $D_1$ & $D_3$ conduct, $D_2$ & $D_4$ are reverse-biased by the voltage of only **one** half of the bridge (i.e., $V_m$).

$$\boxed{\text{PIV}_{Bridge} = V_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequent Mistake:** For Bridge, the PIV is $V_m$ (not $2V_m$). This is a guaranteed 1-mark question almost every year. The PIV of the Bridge is the same as HWR, **but the transformer secondary utilization is double**, making it the industry default.

---

## 3.3 Summary Comparison (Analytical Matrix)

| Quantity | HWR | CT-FWR | Bridge |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| No. of diodes | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Transformer secondary type | Simple | Center-tapped | Simple |
| $V_{dc} \div V_m$ | $0.318$ | $0.636$ | $0.636$ |
| $\gamma$ | $1.21$ | $0.482$ | $0.482$ |
| $\eta$ (%) | $40.6$ | $81.2$ | $81.2$ |
| TUF | $0.287$ | $0.692$ | $0.810$ |
| PIV | $V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $V_m$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here to render the **functional architecture flow** of each rectifier topology. For precise circuit schematics, refer to your textbook (Sedra/Smith, Boylestad, or Millman Halkias).

### 4.1 Half-Wave Rectifier — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AC Source v(t)"] --> B["Transformer"]
    B --> C["Diode D in series"]
    C --> D["Load Resistor R_L"]
    D --> E["Pulsating DC Output"]
    C -.- F["Reverse-biased in negative half cycle"]
```

### 4.2 Center-Tapped Full-Wave Rectifier — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["AC Source"] --> T["Center-Tapped Transformer"]
    T --> H1["Upper Half Secondary"]
    T --> H2["Lower Half Secondary"]
    H1 --> D1["Diode D1 - Conducts in +ve half"]
    H2 --> D2["Diode D2 - Conducts in -ve half"]
    D1 --> N["Common Cathode Node"]
    D2 --> N
    N --> R["Load R_L"]
    R --> CT["Center Tap"]
    CT --> T
```

### 4.3 Bridge (Graetz) Rectifier — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["AC Source"] --> TR["Transformer"]
    TR --> N1["Node A Top"]
    TR --> N2["Node B Bottom"]
    N1 --> D1A["Diode D1 Anode"]
    D1A --> D1C["Diode D1 Cathode"]
    D1C --> P["Positive DC Output +"]
    N2 --> D4A["Diode D4 Anode"]
    D4A --> D4C["Diode D4 Cathode"]
    D4C --> P
    P --> RL["Load R_L"]
    RL --> NEG["Negative DC Output -"]
    NEG --> D2A["Diode D2 Anode"]
    D2A --> D2C["Diode D2 Cathode"]
    D2C --> N1
```
