List any two advantages of active filters over passive filters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **active filter** is an analog frequency-selective circuit that uses an **active device** (typically an operational amplifier) combined with **passive components** (resistors and capacitors) to realize low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, or band-stop response characteristics. **Two advantages over passive filters:** 1. **Gain capability** — Active filters provide signal amplification (gain > 0 dB), while passive filters can only attenuate. 2. **No inductors required** — Active filters use only resistors and capacitors, making them suitable for IC fabrication and avoiding bulky, lossy inductors. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 × 1 mark for advantages)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the transfer function of a second-order Butterworth low-pass filter. What is the value of Q for a Butterworth response?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The standard 2nd-order Butterworth LPF transfer function is: $$ H(s) = \frac{\omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} \, \omega_c s + \omega_c^2} $$ The **quality factor** for a Butterworth response is: $$ Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 $$ This value ensures **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband with the -3 dB point occurring at $f = f_c$. *(Mark distribution: 2 marks equation + 1 mark Q value)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a 2nd-order Sallen-Key low-pass filter with equal components. From the derived expression, obtain the expressions for $\omega_0$ and $Q$ in terms of $R_1, R_2, C$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter using a Sallen-Key topology with $f_c = 5$ kHz. Choose a suitable op-amp and justify the choice based on Gain-Bandwidth Product. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the circuit topology assumptions: - $C_1 = C_2 = C$ (equal-component design) - Unity-gain configuration ($K = 1$) **Step 2 (3 marks):** Apply KCL at Node A (between $R_1$ and $C_1$): $$ \frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} = sC(V_A - V_{out}) + \frac{V_A - V_{out}}{R_2} $$ KCL at Node B ($V_B = V_{out}$ for unity gain): $$ \frac{V_A - V_{out}}{R_2} = sC \cdot V_{out} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve the simultaneous equations: From Node B: $V_A = V_{out}(1 + sCR_2)$ Substituting into Node A and simplifying: $$ H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{s^2 C^2 R_1 R_2 + sC(R_1 + R_2) + 1} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Equate with the standard second-order form $s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2$: $$ \omega_0 = \frac{1}{C \sqrt{R_1 R_2}}, \qquad Q = \frac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2}}{R_1 + R_2} $$ --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Specifications: - $f_c = 5$ kHz, Butterworth → $Q = 0.7071$ - $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 5000 = 3.1416 \times 10^4$ rad/s **Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose $C_1 = C_2 = C = 10$ nF (standard value for kHz range). **Step 3 (2 marks):** Since unity-gain equal-component Sallen-Key has $Q_{max} = 0.5 < 0.7071$, we **must use gain $K > 1$**. For Butterworth, choose $K = 1 + \sqrt{2} = 2.414$ (the minimum gain for $Q = 0.7071$). $$ R_1 = \frac{1}{2 \omega_0 C (1 - 1/K) Q} \cdot K, \qquad R_2 = \frac{1}{2 Q \omega_0 C \cdot K} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute numerical values: $$ R_2 = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.7071 \times 3.1416 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.414} $$ $$ R_2 = \frac{1}{1.073 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 932 \, \Omega $$ $$ R_1 = \frac{2.414 \times R_2 \cdot 1}{1 - 1/2.414} = 1.414 \times 932 \approx 1.318 \, k\Omega $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Op-amp selection: **Choose TL082 (GBW = 3 MHz)** or **LF353 (GBW = 4 MHz)**. **Justification:** $GBW \geq 100 \times f_c = 100 \times 5 \, kHz = 500 \, kHz$. Both TL082 and LF353 satisfy this with margin. For slew-rate, $SR \geq 2\pi f_c V_p = 2\pi \times 5000 \times 10 = 0.314$ V/µs — easily met. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** With the help of frequency response plots, explain the four classical filter approximations: Butterworth, Chebyshev Type-I, Bessel, and Elliptic. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a 2nd-order Multiple Feedback (MFB) low-pass filter with $f_c = 2$ kHz and DC gain $H_0 = -5$. Use a 741 op-amp and verify if it is suitable. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** **Butterworth** — Maximally flat magnitude in passband, monotonic roll-off, no ripple. Best general-purpose filter. **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Chebyshev Type-I** — Equiripple in passband (ripple amplitude $\epsilon$ dB), sharper roll-off than Butterworth, monotonic stopband. Achieves specified attenuation with lower order. **Step 3 (1 mark):** **Bessel** — Maximally flat group delay (linear phase). Preserves pulse shape. Poor magnitude selectivity. **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Elliptic (Cauer)** — Equiripple in BOTH passband and stopband. Sharpest transition band for a given order. Most complex design. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Comparison summary table: | Filter | Passband | Transition | Pulse Response | |---|---|---|---| | Butterworth | Flat | Moderate | Good | | Chebyshev-I | Rippled | Sharp | Ringing | | Bessel | Flat | Slow | Best | | Elliptic | Rippled | Sharpest | Worst | --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** MFB LPF design equations: $$ H(s) = \frac{-H_0 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Design procedure: - $f_c = 2$ kHz → $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 2000 = 1.2566 \times 10^4$ rad/s - $H_0 = -5$ → $|H_0| = 5$ - Choose $C_1 = C_2 = C = 47$ nF (assume) **Step 3 (2 marks):** MFB design formulas: $$ R_1 = \frac{1}{2 Q \omega_0 C}, \quad R_2 = \frac{1}{2 Q \omega_0 C |H_0|}, \quad R_3 = \frac{1}{2 Q \omega_0 C (1 + |H_0|)} $$ With $Q = 0.7071$ (Butterworth): $$ R_1 = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.7071 \times 1.2566 \times 10^4 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 954 \, \Omega $$ $$ R_2 = \frac{R_1}{5} \approx 191 \, \Omega $$ $$ R_3 = \frac{R_1}{6} \approx 159 \, \Omega $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Op-amp validation: **741 op-amp GBW = 1 MHz.** Required $GBW \geq 100 \times f_c = 200$ kHz. ✅ Satisfied. **Slew rate check:** $SR_{741} = 0.5$ V/µs. For $V_{out,peak} = 10$ V: $SR_{required} = 2\pi \times 2000 \times 10 = 0.1257$ V/µs. ✅ Satisfied with margin. **Conclusion:** 741 op-amp is **suitable** for this design. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Gain Constraint:** Equal-component Sallen-Key at unity gain caps $Q$ at **0.5**. If the problem demands $Q = 0.707$ (Butterworth), you **must** use a non-unity gain configuration with $K = 1 + R_B/R_A$. *(Lose 2-3 marks if ignored)* > > 2. **Mixing up $\omega_0$ and $f_c$:** Many students use $f_c$ in the $\omega_0$ formula. Always convert: $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_c$ before substituting. > > 3. **GBW Miscalculation:** The op-amp GBW must be evaluated at the **closed-loop gain × $f_c$**, not just $f_c$. Use $GBW \geq A_{CL} \cdot f_{max}$. > > 4. **Inverting vs Non-Inverting Sallen-Key:** MFB gives **inverting** response (negative gain), Sallen-Key gives **non-inverting**. Forgetting the sign is a frequent error. > > 5. **Component Value Standardization:** Always round to **E12/E24 standard resistor values** (1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2 kΩ). Non-standard values are penalized. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Active Filter Design Considerations** > > ✅ **Core Formulas:** > - $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ (1st-order); $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{C\sqrt{R_1 R_2}}$ (Sallen-Key 2nd-order) > - $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ (Butterworth) > - $Q = 0.5$ (Bessel); $Q \to \infty$ (Chebyshev with ripple) > - Roll-off: **20 dB/decade per order** > > ✅ **Filter Approximations to Remember:** > - **Butterworth** → flat magnitude, moderate roll-off, $Q = 0.707$ > - **Chebyshev-I** → passband ripple, sharp roll-off > - **Bessel** → linear phase, best pulse response > - **Elliptic** → ripple in both bands, sharpest transition > > ✅ **Topology Selection:** > - **Sallen-Key** → non-inverting, low sensitivity, good for $Q \leq 10$ > - **MFB** → inverting, high-frequency capable, wider Q range > > ✅ **Design Constraints:** > - $GBW_{opamp} \geq 100 \times f_c$ > - $SR_{opamp} \geq 2\pi f_c V_{peak}$ > - Op-amp input bias current must be considered for high-impedance designs > - Use **1% metal-film resistors** and **NPO/C0G capacitors** for precision > > ✅ **Frequency Transformations:** > - LP → HP: substitute $s \to \omega_c / s$ > - LP → BP: substitute $s \to (s^2 + \omega_0^2)/(B s)$ > - LP → BS: substitute $s \to B s / (s^2 + \omega_0^2)$ > > ✅ **Quality Factor Formulas:** > - $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{\Delta \omega_{-3dB}}$ > - $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ where $\zeta$ is the damping factor > > ✅ **Sensitivity Rule of Thumb:** > - $S_x^y < 1$ → excellent (Sallen-Key) > - $S_x^y \approx 1$ → acceptable > - $S_x^y > 5$ → redesign the topology

topics.title: Signal Conditioning Circuits for Sensor Interfacing using Op-amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Conditioning Circuits for Sensor Interfacing using Op-amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBRUT304 – Electronic Circuits and Logic Design)** syllabus, **Signal Conditioning** is formally defined as the set of operations performed on the raw analog output of a sensor to make it suitable for further processing—typically amplification, impedance transformation, filtering, isolation, and linearization prior to Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC).

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, force, light, displacement) into an electrical signal. Because most sensors produce **low-level** (microvolt to millivolt), **high-impedance**, and **noise-contaminated** signals, they cannot be fed directly to an ADC. An **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is the universal analog building block that interfaces the sensor to the rest of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must study the use of op-amps in the following signal conditioning configurations:
> 1. Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)
> 2. Bridge Amplifier (Wheatstone bridge with strain gauge, RTD, thermistor)
> 3. Transimpedance (Current-to-Voltage) Amplifier
> 4. Charge Amplifier
> 5. Active Filters (LPF, HPF)
> 6. Sample-and-Hold / Peak Detectors

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Stethoscope" Analogy

Imagine a doctor using a stethoscope. The **heartbeat** (real-world physical signal) produces a very weak sound. The stethoscope does three jobs:
1. **Picks up** the sound (the sensor does this for a physical quantity).
2. **Amplifies** it so the doctor can hear it (the op-amp amplifier).
3. **Filters** out the unwanted lung/environment noise (active filters).

The signal conditioning circuit plays exactly the same role for an electronic sensor. The op-amp is the "electronic stethoscope" that prepares a tiny, noisy sensor output for the ADC "doctor's brain."

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Must Know for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Gain | $A_d$ | **$10^4$ to $10^6$** | Amplification of the wanted signal |
| Common-Mode Gain | $A_c$ | ideally **0** | Amplification of unwanted common noise |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | **CMRR** | **$\geq 80$ dB** | Ability to reject common-mode noise |
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | ideally **$\geq 10\ \text{M}\Omega$** | Prevents loading the sensor |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out}$ | ideally **$\leq 1\ \Omega$** | Drives the next stage easily |
| Input Offset Voltage | $V_{os}$ | **$\mu$V range** | DC error at output |
| Slew Rate | SR | **V/$\mu$s** | How fast output can change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CMRR matters for sensor interfacing:** In real environments (factories, biomedical instruments), 50 Hz power-line interference appears as a **common-mode signal** on both sensor lines. A high CMRR instrumentation amplifier rejects this interference while preserving the tiny differential sensor signal.

### 1.4 Classification of Sensors by Electrical Output

The choice of signal conditioning circuit **depends entirely on the sensor's output type** (this is a frequently tested KTU concept):

| Sensor Type | Example | Output Form | Required Conditioning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resistive** | Thermistor, RTD, Strain gauge, LDR | Change in resistance $\Delta R$ | **Wheatstone bridge** + INA |
| **Voltage-generating** | Thermocouple, Hall sensor, Piezo (low freq) | $\mu$V to mV | **High-gain INA** |
| **Current-generating** | Photodiode, phototransistor | nA to $\mu$A | **Transimpedance amplifier** |
| **Charge-generating** | Piezoelectric accelerometer | pC to nC | **Charge amplifier** |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitive pressure sensor, humidity sensor | Change in capacitance $\Delta C$ | **Capacitance-to-voltage** converter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks of Signal Conditioning

Any signal conditioning circuit performs a **chain of operations** on the raw sensor signal. The six canonical building blocks are:

1. **Amplification** — increases signal amplitude to match the ADC input range (typically 0–5 V or ±10 V).
2. **Filtering** — removes out-of-band noise (e.g., 50 Hz notch filter, low-pass anti-aliasing filter).
3. **Isolation** — protects the expensive ADC/DAQ system from high-voltage transients.
4. **Linearization** — corrects the non-linear sensor response (e.g., thermistor NTC curve).
5. **Impedance Transformation** — matches high sensor impedance to low ADC impedance.
6. **Level Shifting** — converts bipolar sensor output to unipolar 0–5 V ADC range.

### 2.2 The Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — The Workhorse of Sensor Interfacing

The **3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** is the most important circuit for KTU examinations on this topic. It consists of:

- **Stage 1**: Two non-inverting buffers with cross-coupled feedback — provides high input impedance, differential gain, and common-mode rejection.
- **Stage 2**: A unity-gain difference amplifier — converts the differential signal to a single-ended output and rejects common-mode voltage.

**Why INA is preferred over a simple difference amplifier for sensors:**
- The difference amplifier has **low input impedance** (~50–100 kΩ), which **loads** the high-impedance sensor.
- The INA has **very high input impedance** on both inputs ($\geq 10^{9}\ \Omega$), preserving sensor signal integrity.

### 2.3 The Wheatstone Bridge — Foundation for Resistive Sensors

Resistive sensors (strain gauge, RTD, thermistor) are nearly always connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** because the bridge converts a small resistance change ($\Delta R$) into a measurable differential voltage.

A bridge has four arms: $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, and $R_x$ (the sensor). It is excited by a stable DC voltage $V_s$ (or current source $I_s$).

When the bridge is **balanced** ($R_1 R_x = R_2 R_3$), output $V_o = 0$.
When the resistance changes by $\Delta R$, a **differential voltage** appears at the output.

### 2.4 The Transimpedance Amplifier — For Current-Output Sensors

Photodiodes and similar sensors behave as **current sources**. To convert this tiny current ($I_p$, typically nA–$\mu$A) into a usable voltage, a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)** is used. It is simply an inverting op-amp with the photodiode connected at the inverting input and a large feedback resistor $R_f$.

### 2.5 The Charge Amplifier — For Piezoelectric Sensors

Piezoelectric sensors generate **charge** $Q$ (proportional to applied force/pressure). A charge amplifier uses a **feedback capacitor** $C_f$ on an inverting op-amp configuration. The op-amp keeps the input node at virtual ground, allowing all the generated charge to accumulate across $C_f$.

A high-value **feedback resistor** $R_f$ (in parallel with $C_f$) provides a DC path to prevent output saturation due to op-amp input bias current.

### 2.6 Active Filters — For Noise Reduction

Active filters using op-amps provide gain and filtering in one stage. For sensor applications:
- **Low-pass filter (LPF)**: removes high-frequency noise (EMI, RF interference).
- **High-pass filter (HPF)**: blocks DC offset and 1/f noise.
- **Band-pass filter (BPF)**: used in biomedical (ECG, EMG) signals.
- **Notch filter**: removes 50 Hz power-line interference.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Circuit / Concept | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Non-inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **3-Op-Amp INA total gain** | $A = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\cdot\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | INA Stage-1 gain | $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | INA Stage-2 (diff. amp) gain | $A_2 = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Wheatstone bridge output voltage** | $V_o = V_s\left(\dfrac{R_x}{R_x+R_3}-\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right)$ | $V_s$ in V, $R$ in $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Strain gauge output (small $\Delta R$) | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_s}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $V_s$ in V |
| 8 | Strain $\varepsilon$ from gauge factor | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_F$ dimensionless, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless |
| 9 | **Transimpedance amplifier gain** | $V_{out} = -I_p \cdot R_f$ | $I_p$ in A, $R_f$ in $\Omega$ |
| 10 | TIA bandwidth limit | $f_{-3\text{dB}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | Hz (with compensation cap $C_f$) |
| 11 | **Charge amplifier output (DC)** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}$ | $Q$ in C, $C_f$ in F |
| 12 | Charge amplifier (sinusoidal) | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}$ (independent of $\omega$ below $f_c$) | V |
| 13 | Charge amp low cutoff | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | Hz |
| 14 | First-order LPF cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 15 | First-order HPF cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 16 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB |
| 17 | Log amplifier output | $V_{out} = -V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$ | $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 18 | Anti-log (exponential) amp output | $V_{out} = -R_f I_s \cdot e^{V_{in}/V_T}$ | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize formulas **#3, #6, #7, #9, and #11**. They are the five most frequently asked derivations. In the exam, always begin by drawing the **circuit diagram** before substituting any formula — partial marks depend heavily on the diagram.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Sensor | Industry / Domain | Signal Conditioning Circuit Used |
|---|---|---|
| Strain gauge in load cell | Weighing scales, bridges | Wheatstone bridge + INA (e.g., AD620) |
| Thermistor in incubator | Biomedical, pharma | Bridge + INA + linearization |
| Photodiode in smoke detector | Safety systems | Transimpedance amplifier |
| Piezo accelerometer in vibration monitor | Industrial predictive maintenance | Charge amplifier |
| ECG electrodes | Patient monitoring | INA + 0.5 Hz HPF + 40 Hz LPF + 50 Hz notch |
| LVDT in CNC machine | Precision displacement | INA + synchronous demodulator |
| RTD in furnace | Industrial process control | 4-wire bridge + INA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this section:** Every step is shown explicitly. In KTU answer sheets, write these derivations in **exactly this expanded form** — the examiner awards marks for each visible logical step, not just the final answer.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of an Unbalanced Wheatstone Bridge

**Given:** A Wheatstone bridge with arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_x$ excited by supply $V_s$. The bridge output is taken between the midpoints of the two voltage dividers.

**To Find:** The differential output voltage $V_o = V_a - V_b$.

**Step 1 — Identify the two voltage dividers.**
The left divider consists of $R_1$ and $R_x$. The right divider consists of $R_2$ and $R_3$. Both are driven by the same supply $V_s$.

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule to the left arm.**

$$V_a = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider rule to the right arm.**

$$V_b = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the differential output.**

$$V_o = V_a - V_b = V_s \left(\frac{R_x}{R_1+R_x} - \frac{R_2}{R_2+R_3}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Balanced bridge condition.** When $V_o = 0$, the bridge is balanced, giving the well-known balance equation:

$$\frac{R_x}{R_1+R_x} = \frac{R_2}{R_2+R_3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_x$$

**Step 6 — Special case: Symmetric bridge with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and sensor $R_x = R + \Delta R$.**

$$V_a = V_s \cdot \frac{R+\Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_b = V_s \cdot \frac{R}{2R} = \frac{V_s}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Use the approximation $(R+\Delta R)/(2R+\Delta R) \approx \frac{1}{2}(1 + \Delta R / 2R)$ for small $\Delta R$.**

$$V_o = V_s \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{\Delta R}{2R}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\right] = \frac{V_s}{4}\cdot\frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** For a symmetric Wheatstone bridge with a small resistance change, the output is linear in $\Delta R$ and equals $V_s \Delta R / (4R)$. This is the formula used in nearly every strain-gauge and load-cell problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

**Given:** The standard 3-op-amp INA. Op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ form the input stage, $A_3$ forms the difference amplifier output stage. Resistors $R_1$ are the feedback of $A_1$ and $A_2$; $R_G$ is the gain-setting resistor between the two input op-amp inverting inputs. Resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$ form the difference amplifier.

**Step 1 — Analyze the input stage.** Because both op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ are configured as non-inverting amplifiers (with cross-coupled feedback), their non-inverting inputs are at $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively. By virtual short, the inverting inputs are also at $V_1$ and $V_2$.

**Step 2 — Voltage across $R_G$.** The voltage difference across the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is:

$$V_{R_G} = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 3 — Current through $R_G$.** Because no current enters the op-amp inputs, the same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors and $R_G$:

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across the two $R_1$ resistors.** The total resistance in series with $R_G$ is $R_1 + R_1 + R_G$. The current $I$ flowing through it produces a voltage drop from the output of $A_1$ ($V_{o1}$) to the output of $A_2$ ($V_{o2}$):

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = I(R_1 + R_G + R_1) = (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Gain of stage 1.**

$$A_1 = \frac{V_{o1} - V_{o2}}{V_1 - V_2} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 6 — Analyze stage 2 (difference amplifier).** The standard difference amplifier has gain $A_2 = R_3 / R_2$. (The reader is expected to know this; it can be derived in 2 lines by superposition.) Its output is:

$$V_o = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1})$$

**Step 7 — Total gain of the INA.**

$$A = \frac{V_o}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The single resistor $R_G$ controls the entire gain — this is why INAs are popular. Changing one resistor changes the gain from 1 to 1000 without disturbing the differential balance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Transimpedance Amplifier Output

**Given:** A photodiode producing photocurrent $I_p$ connected to the inverting input of an op-amp. The non-inverting input is grounded. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting input.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting input (virtual ground).** The photodiode injects current $I_p$ into the node; this current must flow through $R_f$ because none can flow into the op-amp input.

$$I_p = I_{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{out} = -I_p R_f$$

**Step 3 — Transimpedance gain.** The "gain" of a TIA has units of resistance (V/A):

$$R_{T} = \frac{V_{out}}{I_p} = -R_f$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth consideration.** The photodiode's junction capacitance $C_d$ and the op-amp's input capacitance $C_{in}$ create a pole. To prevent peaking/oscillation, a small compensation capacitor $C_f$ (typically 2–10 pF) is placed across $R_f$. The closed-loop bandwidth is then:

$$f_{-3\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting that the TIA output is **negative**. If the photodiode is reversed, or if the signal must be positive, an additional inverting stage or a non-inverting TIA topology must be used.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Charge Amplifier Output for a Piezoelectric Sensor

**Given:** A piezoelectric sensor modeled as a current source $i(t) = dQ/dt$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_s$. Connected to an inverting op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$ (and feedback resistor $R_f$ for DC stability).

**Step 1 — KCL at the inverting node.** Summing currents into the virtual-ground node:

$$i(t) = i_{C_f}(t) + i_{R_f}(t) = C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state for sinusoidal excitation.** For $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$, in the frequency domain:

$$I_0 = \left(j\omega C_f + \frac{1}{R_f}\right) V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{I_0}{j\omega C_f + 1/R_f} = -\frac{Q_0 \, j\omega}{j\omega C_f + 1/R_f}$$

**Step 4 — For frequencies well above $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$, the term $1/R_f$ is negligible.** Then:

$$V_{out} \approx -\frac{Q_0}{C_f}$$

This is the **fundamental charge-to-voltage conversion equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike the open-circuit voltage of a piezoelectric sensor (which depends on its own tiny capacitance $C_s$ and therefore varies with load and temperature), the charge amplifier output depends **only on $C_f$**, which is a stable external component. This is why charge amplifiers are preferred for precise piezoelectric measurements.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Circuit

| Sensor Output Type | Best Circuit | Reason | Typical IC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential voltage (e.g., bridge) | 3-Op-Amp INA | High $Z_{in}$, high CMRR | AD620, INA128, INA333 |
| Single-ended voltage | Non-inverting amp | Simple, high $Z_{in}$ | General purpose op-amp |
| Current (photodiode) | TIA | Low $Z_{in}$ virtual ground, low noise | OPA129, OPA627, LMC662 |
| Charge (piezo) | Charge amplifier | Makes output independent of $C_s$ | OPA627, AD745 |
| Resistance change (RTD, strain) | Bridge + INA | Linearizes $\Delta R$ to $V_o$ | AD620, AD8421 |
| Capacitance change | AC bridge + synchronous detector | Capacitance needs AC excitation | AD7746 (C-to-digital) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Convention:** All node IDs are pure alphanumeric (prefixed with letters) to ensure Mermaid parses without errors. No markdown formatting is used inside node labels.

---

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PHYSICAL QUANTITY] --> B1[SENSOR TRANSDUCER]
    B1 --> C1[PRE-AMPLIFIER OR BRIDGE]
    C1 --> D1[LEVEL SHIFTER]
    D1 --> E1[ACTIVE FILTER LPF HPF]
    E1 --> F1[LINEARIZER]
    F1 --> G1[SAMPLE AND HOLD]
    G1 --> H1[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    H1 --> I1[MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP]
    I1 --> J1[DISPLAY OR ACTUATOR]
```

---

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIN1[INPUT V1] --> IA1[OPAMP A1 BUFFER]
    VIN2[INPUT V2] --> IA2[OPAMP A2 BUFFER]
    IA1 -- RF R1 --> IA1N[NODE N1]
    IA1N -- RG GAIN RESISTOR --> IA2N[NODE N2]
    IA2N -- RF R1 --> IA2[OPAMP A2 BUFFER]
    IA1 --> IA3A[OPAMP A3 STAGE2 PLUS INPUT]
    IA2 --> IA3B[OPAMP A3 STAGE2 MINUS INPUT]
    IA3A -- R2 --> IA3N[NODE N3]
    IA3N -- R2 --> IA3B
    IA3N -- R3 --> IA3OUT[NODE VOUT]
    IA3OUT -- R3 --> IA3B
    IA3OUT --> IAEND[FINAL OUTPUT VOUT]
```

---

### 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge and INA Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS[DC SUPPLY Vs] --> BR1[BRIDGE ARM R1]
    BR1 --> BRN1[NODE A]
    BRN1 --> BRSG[STRAIN GAUGE Rx R PLUS DELTA R]
    BRSG --> BRGND[GROUND]
    VS --> BR2[BRIDGE ARM R2]
    BR2 --> BRN2[NODE B]
    BRN2 --> BR3[BRIDGE ARM R3]
    BR3 --> BRGND
    BRN1 --> INAP[INA PLUS INPUT]
    BRN2 --> INAN[INA MINUS INPUT]
    INAP --> INABK[3 OPAMP INA]
    INAN --> INABK
    INABK --> OUTAMP[AMPLIFIED VOUT]
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Correct Signal Conditioning Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START1[START SENSOR OUTPUT TYPE] --> Q1{IS OUTPUT VOLTAGE}
    Q1 -- YES SMALL mV LEVEL --> PATH1[USE 3 OPAMP INA]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{IS OUTPUT CURRENT nA OR uA}
    Q2 -- YES --> PATH2[USE TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{IS OUTPUT CHARGE pC OR nC}
    Q3 -- YES --> PATH3[USE CHARGE AMPLIFIER]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{IS OUTPUT RESISTANCE CHANGE}
    Q4 -- YES --> PATH4[USE WHEATSTONE BRIDGE PLUS INA]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5[USE STANDARD OPAMP CONFIGURATION]
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Complete Charge Amplifier for Piezoelectric Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PZ1[PIEZO SENSOR] --> PZN[NODE VIRTUAL GROUND]
    PZN --> OA1[OPAMP WITH FEEDBACK CAPACITOR Cf]
    PZN -- PARASITIC CAPACITANCE Cs --> OA1
    OA1 -- FEEDBACK CAPACITOR Cf --> PZN
    OA1 -- FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf --> PZN
    OA1 --> OUT1[OUTPUT VOUT EQUALS MINUS Q BY Cf]
```

---

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Active Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[SENSOR OUTPUT AFTER AMPLIFIER] --> R1LPF[R SERIES RESISTOR]
    R1LPF --> NODELPF[NODE A]
    NODELPF --> CLPF[C FEEDBACK CAPACITOR]
    NODELPF -- TO OPAMP PLUS INPUT --> OPLPF[UNITY GAIN BUFFER]
    OPLPF --> OUTLPF[FILTERED OUTPUT TO ADC]
    CLPF --> OUTLPF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Exam Style Note:** Part A questions test **definitions, listings, and short derivations**. Answers must be 3–5 lines plus a small diagram. Aim for 1 page of answer sheet.

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Define signal conditioning. List any four functions performed by a signal conditioning circuit in sensor interfacing.**

**Model Answer:**

Signal conditioning is the process of manipulating a sensor's raw analog output to make it suitable for further processing (e.g., analog-to-digital conversion).

Four primary functions are:

1. **Amplification** — boosting low-level sensor signals to ADC-compatible levels.
2. **Filtering** — removing out-of-band noise and anti-aliasing.
3. **Isolation** — protecting downstream electronics from high-voltage transients.
4. **Linearization** — correcting the non-linear sensor transfer function.

[Diagram: A block diagram of Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → ADC may be drawn for full marks.]

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**What is an instrumentation amplifier? State two reasons why it is preferred over a simple difference amplifier for sensor interfacing.**

**Model Answer:**

An instrumentation amplifier (INA) is a precision differential amplifier with very high input impedance, high CMRR, and a gain controllable by a single external resistor. It is built using three op-amps (two input buffers + one difference amplifier).

Two reasons it is preferred:

1. **Very high input impedance** ($\geq 10^9\ \Omega$) on both inputs prevents loading of the high-impedance sensor.
2. **High CMRR** ($\geq 80$ dB) effectively rejects common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz line interference).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Exam Style Note:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-question is typically 7 marks. Always begin with a **neat circuit diagram** — this alone fetches 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A strain gauge with nominal resistance $R = 350\ \Omega$ and gauge factor $G_F = 2.1$ is fixed to a steel beam. The beam is subjected to a strain $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). The gauge is connected in one arm of a symmetric Wheatstone bridge excited by $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$. The bridge output is fed to an INA with gain $A = 250$. Calculate: (i) the change in gauge resistance $\Delta R$, and (ii) the final output voltage of the INA. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Draw the 3-op-amp INA diagram.** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Step 2 — Analyze Stage 1 (two input buffers).** Apply virtual short at op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$. The current through $R_G$ (between the two inverting inputs) equals the current through both $R_1$ resistors.

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across $R_G$ + two $R_1$ resistors:**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = I (R_G + 2R_1)$$

**Step 4 — Stage 1 gain:**

$$A_1 = \frac{V_{o1} - V_{o2}}{V_1 - V_2} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 5 — Stage 2 is a standard difference amplifier with gain:**

$$A_2 = \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

**Step 6 — Total gain:**

$$A = A_1 \cdot A_2 = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

---

##### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the change in gauge resistance.**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = R \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 350 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 0.3675\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bridge output (symmetric bridge).**

$$V_{br} = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{10}{4} \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 2.625 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 2.625\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the INA output.**

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{br} = 250 \times 2.625 \times 10^{-3} = 0.656\ \text{V} \approx 656\ \text{mV}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta R = 0.3675\ \Omega$, $V_{out} \approx 656\ \text{mV}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **charge amplifier** used for piezoelectric sensor interfacing. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A photodiode produces a photocurrent of $I_p = 2.5\ \mu\text{A}$ when illuminated. Design a transimpedance amplifier to convert this into an output voltage of $V_{out} = 5\ \text{V}$. Specify the feedback resistor and the compensation capacitor if the desired bandwidth is $f_{-3\text{dB}} = 100\ \text{kHz}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Circuit description.** Draw the piezo sensor connected to the inverting input of an op-amp. The non-inverting input is grounded. A feedback capacitor $C_f$ (and a very large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel) connects the output to the inverting input. Refer to Section 4.5 of these notes.

**Step 2 — Virtual ground principle.** Because the op-amp maintains the inverting input at virtual ground (0 V), the voltage across the piezoelectric sensor is essentially zero. This is crucial — it eliminates the loading effect of the sensor's own capacitance.

**Step 3 — KCL at inverting input.** All charge generated by the piezo element flows into $C_f$:

$$Q = -C_f \cdot V_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$

**Step 5 — For sinusoidal excitation** at angular frequency $\omega$, with charge amplitude $Q_0$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q_0}{C_f}\quad \text{(independent of } \omega\text{, below cutoff } f_L = 1/2\pi R_f C_f\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Advantages:** (i) Output depends only on stable external $C_f$, not on the variable sensor capacitance. (ii) Acts as an integrator that converts charge directly to voltage. (iii) Provides low-impedance output suitable for ADC.

---

##### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Find the feedback resistor.**

$$R_f = \frac{\vert V_{out} \vert}{I_p} = \frac{5}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega = 2\ \text{M}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Find the compensation capacitor for the desired bandwidth.**

$$C_f = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f f_{-3\text{dB}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{6} \times 100 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$C_f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{12}} \approx 0.796\ \text{pF}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_f = 2\ \text{M}\Omega$, $C_f \approx 0.8\ \text{pF}$ (round up to 1 pF in practice).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
>
> 1. **No circuit diagram drawn.** Examiners deduct 2 marks if the diagram is missing. Always draw the circuit FIRST, even if you are unsure of the derivation.
> 2. **Sign confusion in TIA.** The output of an inverting TIA is **negative** when photodiode current flows into the node. If the problem expects a positive output, mention an additional inverting stage.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_f$ in charge amplifier.** Without the feedback capacitor, the op-amp will saturate at the supply rail due to input bias current — $C_f$ is the **integrating** element.
> 4. **Using $V_s \Delta R / R$ instead of $V_s \Delta R / (4R)$.** The factor 4 arises from the symmetric bridge. Marks are deducted if you write the wrong formula.
> 5. **Units not written.** Write units at every step ($\Omega$, V, A, Hz) — the examiner looks for unit consistency.
> 6. **Not mentioning CMRR.** Whenever an INA is discussed, CMRR must be mentioned. This is a free mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Signal conditioning** = preparing a sensor output for ADC: amplify, filter, isolate, linearize.
- [x] **Sensor output types:** voltage, current, charge, resistance change, capacitance change.
- [x] **3-Op-Amp INA Gain:** $\displaystyle A = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$ — single-resistor gain control.
- [x] **INA advantages:** high $Z_{in}$, high CMRR, single-resistor gain.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge output (symmetric):** $\displaystyle V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$.
- [x] **Balance condition:** $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_x$.
- [x] **Transimpedance amplifier:** $\displaystyle V_{out} = -I_p R_f$; bandwidth $\displaystyle f_{-3\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$.
- [x] **Charge amplifier:** $\displaystyle V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$ — output independent of sensor capacitance.
- [x] **Charge amp cutoff:** $\displaystyle f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$.
- [x] **First-order LPF/HPF cutoff:** $\displaystyle f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
- [x] **CMRR** = $20 \log(A_d / A_c)$ — must be high (≥ 80 dB) for sensor work.
- [x] **Strain gauge equation:** $\displaystyle \frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Practical ICs to remember:** AD620 (INA), OPA627 (low-noise op-amp), INA128, AD8421.
- [x] **Always draw the circuit diagram before starting any derivation** — partial marks depend on it.
- [x] **Mention CMRR and input impedance** whenever INA is discussed.
- [x] **Check signs** carefully in inverting configurations (TIA, charge amplifier).
- [x] **Anti-aliasing LPF** is mandatory before the ADC in every sensor chain (Nyquist criterion).

---

**End of Module 2 — Signal Conditioning Circuits for Sensor Interfacing using Op-amps**

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for this topic:**
> 1. Memorize the **5 golden formulas** (INA gain, bridge output, strain relation, TIA output, charge amp output).
> 2. Practice drawing **at least 5 circuit diagrams** cleanly: INA, bridge+INA, TIA, charge amp, first-order LPF.
> 3. Be ready to **select a circuit** for a given sensor — this is a frequently asked 7-mark question.
> 4. Always **end** long answers with a short paragraph on real-world applications (load cell, ECG, photometry). It earns a **bonus 1 mark** in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A-D and D-A Converters using Op-amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** A-D and D-A Converters using Op-amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Data Converters?

**Data Converters** are fundamental mixed-signal interface circuits that form the critical bridge between the **analog real world** and the **digital processing world**. In modern electronic systems, natural physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, voice, video) are inherently continuous analog signals, while microprocessors, FPGAs, and digital logic operate on discrete binary numbers.

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** translates a binary digital word into a proportional analog voltage or current, while an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs the reverse operation, sampling a continuous analog signal and producing a discrete digital output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Data converters using operational amplifiers are the practical building blocks that use the **precision amplification, summing, and comparison characteristics of Op-amps** to realize high-accuracy conversion between the analog and digital domains.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Language Translator"

Think of data converters as **interpreters translating between two languages**:

| Domain | Language | Speaker |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **Analog** | Continuous voltage waveform (smooth) | Nature, Sensors, Microphones |
| **Digital** | Discrete binary codes (0s and 1s) | Microcontrollers, Computers, Logic Chips |

- A **DAC** is like converting a **written recipe** (digital list of ingredients in numbers) into the **actual taste of the food** (analog signal strength).
- An **ADC** is like a **judge scoring a singer** (analog performance) by giving whole-number points from 0 to 10 (digital rating).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Parameters** in KTU examinations:
> - **Resolution** = $2^n$ levels (where $n$ is the number of bits)
> - **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** = maximum analog output
> - **Step Size (Quantum, $q$)** = $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$
> - **Conversion Time** = time taken to complete one conversion
> - **Linearity** = how closely output follows ideal straight-line transfer curve

## 1.3 Classification of Data Converters

The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2) requires the operational analysis of the following converter types:

**Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC):**
1. **Binary Weighted Resistor DAC**
2. **R-2R Ladder DAC**

**Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC):**
1. **Counter-Type (Tracking/Servo) ADC**
2. **Successive Approximation ADC**
3. **Flash (Simultaneous/Parallel) ADC**
4. **Dual-Slope Integrating ADC**

> [!TIP]
> **Resolution vs. Accuracy:** *Resolution* is the number of distinct output levels a converter can produce (theoretical), while *accuracy* is how close the actual output is to the ideal expected value (practical). Higher resolution does not always guarantee high accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC) — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Binary Weighted Resistor DAC

The **Binary Weighted Resistor DAC** is the most intuitive DAC topology. It uses an inverting summing amplifier with input resistors whose values are inversely proportional to the binary weights of the digital input bits.

**Operational Logic:**

- Each digital bit $b_i$ controls an electronic switch (typically a MOSFET or BJT acting as a Single-Pole Double-Throw switch).
- The switch connects the corresponding input resistor to either a **reference voltage $V_{ref}$** (when bit = 1) or to **ground** (when bit = 0).
- The Op-amp, configured as a **summing inverter**, produces an output proportional to the weighted sum.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. The LSB (Least Significant Bit) is connected to the **largest resistor** ($2^{n-1} R$).
2. The MSB (Most Significant Bit) is connected to the **smallest resistor** ($R$).
3. Resistor values follow the geometric progression: $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$.
4. Current through each branch is either $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{i-1}R}$ (bit ON) or $0$ (bit OFF).
5. Virtual ground at inverting input sums all these currents through feedback resistor $R_f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Limitation of Binary Weighted DAC:** For high-resolution converters (say, 12-bit or 16-bit), the resistor values span an enormous range (e.g., $R$ to $4096R$ for a 12-bit DAC). This is practically impossible to fabricate on a single IC with matched characteristics, leading to **poor linearity**. Hence this topology is restricted to **4-bit or 5-bit** practical implementations in KTU problems.

### 2.1.2 R-2R Ladder DAC

The **R-2R Ladder DAC** elegantly solves the wide-range resistor problem by using only **two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$**, regardless of the bit count. This makes it the **industry standard for IC fabrication**.

**Operational Logic:**

- The ladder network is fed at the top by the digital switches and reference $V_{ref}$.
- At every node of the ladder, looking into the rest of the network gives a resistance of exactly $2R$ (this is the fundamental "ladder" property).
- Therefore, the voltage at each node is **halved** as we move from MSB to LSB side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why R-2R is superior:**
> 1. Only two resistor values → easy IC integration.
> 2. Excellent matching (both resistors made of same material, same temperature coefficient).
> 3. Easily scalable to 8, 10, 12, 14, or 16 bits.
> 4. Lower thermal drift and better monotonicity.

## 2.2 Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC) — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Counter-Type (Servo/Tracking) ADC

This is the **simplest closed-loop ADC**. It uses a **DAC**, an **up/down counter**, a **clock**, and a **comparator** in a feedback loop.

- If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: Counter counts **UP** (incrementing).
- If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$: Counter counts **DOWN** (decrementing).
- The output of the counter is the digital representation of $V_{in}$.

**Disadvantage:** Very **slow tracking speed**, especially for rapidly changing inputs. Conversion time is variable.

### 2.2.2 Successive Approximation ADC (SAR ADC)

The **SAR ADC is the workhorse of modern medium-speed, medium-resolution conversion.** It uses a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** that performs a **binary search** on the input voltage.

- The MSB is first set to 1, all others to 0 → DAC produces $\frac{V_{ref}}{2}$.
- Comparator checks: is $V_{in} > \frac{V_{ref}}{2}$?
  - **YES:** MSB is kept as 1.
  - **NO:** MSB is reset to 0.
- The next MSB is then tested, halving the search range each time.
- After $n$ clock cycles, the input is determined to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.

**Total Conversion Time = $n \times T_{clock}$ (fixed for any input).**

### 2.2.3 Flash (Parallel) ADC

The **Flash ADC is the fastest ADC topology.** It uses $2^n - 1$ comparators in parallel, all comparing $V_{in}$ against equally spaced reference voltages generated by a resistor ladder.

- For a 3-bit Flash ADC: 7 comparators.
- The comparator outputs feed a **priority encoder** that produces the $n$-bit binary code.

**Advantage:** Single clock-cycle conversion (effectively instantaneous).
**Disadvantage:** Hardware doubles with every additional bit — $2^n$ resistors, $2^n - 1$ comparators. Impractical beyond 8 bits.

### 2.2.4 Dual-Slope Integrating ADC

The **Dual-Slope ADC** is renowned for its **exceptional noise rejection and high accuracy**. It uses an integrator, comparator, and counter.

**Two Phases:**

1. **Run-Up Phase (Fixed Time $T_1$):** The unknown input $V_{in}$ is integrated for a fixed duration $2^n T_{clock}$. The integrator output ramps up to a value proportional to $V_{in}$.
2. **Run-Down Phase (Variable Time $T_2$):** The integrator is switched to a known negative reference $-V_{ref}$. The output ramps down at a fixed rate. The counter measures how long the rundown takes.

The final count $N$ is directly proportional to $V_{in}$ and **independent of integrator component values** (major advantage).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **DAC Formula** | **ADC Formula** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of levels (Resolution) | $2^n$ | $2^n$ |
| Step Size (Quantum) $q$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ |
| Full Scale Output (FSO) | $\dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Same |
| Binary Weighted DAC Output | $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R} V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{b_i}{2^i}$ | — |
| R-2R Ladder DAC Output | $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | — |
| SAR ADC Conversion Time | — | $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ |
| Flash ADC Conversion Time | — | $T_c = T_{clk}$ (one cycle) |
| Dual Slope Output Count | — | $N = \dfrac{2^n}{V_{ref}} \cdot V_{in}$ |
| Counter Type Conversion Time | — | $T_c = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ (worst case) |
| % Resolution | $\dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\%$ | $\dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\%$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Applications:**
> - **Audio Equipment (CD/DVD Players):** 16-bit R-2R DACs reconstruct audio waveforms.
> - **Digital Multimeters (DMMs):** Dual-slope ADCs give high noise immunity.
> - **Digital Cameras/Displays:** Flash ADCs capture high-speed video frames.
> - **Microcontroller Sensor Interfaces:** SAR ADCs in STM32/Arduino read temperature, pressure.
> - **Motor Speed Control (PWM Generation):** DACs set analog reference voltages.
> - **Medical Instrumentation (ECG, MRI):** High-resolution SAR ADCs capture biomedical signals.
> - **Automotive ECUs:** ADC reads throttle position; DAC controls fuel injection timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Binary Weighted Resistor DAC Output Voltage

### Circuit Configuration

We use an **inverting summing amplifier** with Op-amp. The inverting input is a **virtual ground** (since non-inverting input is grounded). Feedback resistor is $R_f$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the digital input word be an $n$-bit binary number represented as $b_{n-1} b_{n-2} \cdots b_1 b_0$, where $b_{n-1}$ is the MSB and $b_0$ is the LSB.

The input resistors are:
- For bit $b_0$ (LSB): $R_0 = 2^{n-1} R$
- For bit $b_1$: $R_1 = 2^{n-2} R$
- For bit $b_{n-1}$ (MSB): $R_{n-1} = R$

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the virtual ground node. The sum of currents entering the node equals the current flowing through the feedback resistor.

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} I_i = I_f$$

**Step 2:** Current from branch $i$ (when switch is ON, $b_i = 1$):

$$I_i = \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{R_i} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1-i} R}$$

When $b_i = 0$, the switch grounds that branch, so $I_i = 0$.

Combining both cases using $b_i$:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n-1-i} R}$$

**Step 3:** Current through feedback resistor:

$$I_f = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and apply KCL:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n-1-i} R} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $V_o$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{n-1-i}}$$

**Step 6:** Re-index by letting $j = n-1-i$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_{n-1-j}}{2^{j}}$$

Or, expressed with MSB as $b_{n-1}$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot V_{ref} \left( \frac{b_{n-1}}{2^{0}} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2^{1}} + \frac{b_{n-3}}{2^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$

**Step 7:** If $R_f = R$ (unity gain feedback), this simplifies to:

$$V_o = -V_{ref} \left( b_{n-1} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2} + \frac{b_{n-3}}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: R-2R Ladder DAC Output Voltage

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For an $n$-bit R-2R DAC with the standard inverting configuration and feedback resistor $R_f = R$:

**Step 1:** The key insight — the ladder has the property that at any node, the equivalent resistance looking right (and the input resistor to the left) is $2R$. Hence, the input current to each branch is **halved** compared to the branch above it.

**Step 2:** The current contributed by bit $b_{n-1}$ (MSB) at the summing junction is:

$$I_{MSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}$$

**Step 3:** The current contributed by bit $b_{n-2}$ is half of that:

$$I_{b_{n-2}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{4R}$$

**Step 4:** In general, the current from bit $b_i$ is:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n-i} R}$$

**Step 5:** Sum all branch currents at the virtual ground and equate to feedback current:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n-i} R} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $V_o$ with $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $2^n$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^{n-1-i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Numerical (3-bit R-2R DAC):** Let $V_{ref} = 8\text{ V}$, $R_f = R$. For input word $b_2 b_1 b_0 = 101$:
> - $\sum b_i \cdot 2^{n-1-i} = (1 \cdot 2^2) + (0 \cdot 2^1) + (1 \cdot 2^0) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5$
> - $V_o = -\frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{8}{8} \cdot 5 = -5\text{ V}$

## 3.3 Derivation: Dual-Slope ADC Output Equation

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** During the **Run-Up Phase**, the integrator input is $V_{in}$ for a fixed time $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ (where $2^n$ is the full-scale count).

The integrator output at end of run-up:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{T_1} V_{in} \, dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

(Assuming $V_{in}$ is approximately constant during integration.)

**Step 2:** During the **Run-Down Phase**, the integrator input is switched to $-V_{ref}$. The output ramps from $V_A$ back to zero.

The time $T_2$ taken for this rundown:

$$0 = V_A - \frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{T_2} (-V_{ref}) \, dt$$

$$0 = V_A + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_A$:

$$0 = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $RC$:

$$V_{in} \cdot T_1 = V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

**Step 5:** Express $T_1$ and $T_2$ in terms of clock counts ($T_1 = N_1 T_{clk}$, $T_2 = N_2 T_{clk}$):

$$V_{in} \cdot N_1 T_{clk} = V_{ref} \cdot N_2 T_{clk}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $T_{clk}$:

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $N_1 = 2^n$ (fixed run-up count):

$$\boxed{N_2 = \frac{2^n}{V_{ref}} \cdot V_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The $RC$ time constant has cancelled out. This means **the accuracy does NOT depend on the absolute values of R and C**, only on their **stability and linearity** during the conversion cycle. This is why dual-slope ADCs are so accurate — they are self-calibrating within each cycle.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Speed vs. Resolution Trade-off

| **ADC Type** | **Speed** | **Resolution (typical)** | **Hardware Cost** | **Noise Immunity** | **KTU Exam Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Counter-Type** | Very Slow | Up to 8 bits | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Successive Approximation** | Medium | 8 to 16 bits | Medium | Medium | **High** |
| **Flash (Parallel)** | **Fastest** | 4 to 8 bits | **Highest** ($2^n - 1$ comparators) | Low | High |
| **Dual-Slope** | Slow | 12 to 20 bits | Medium | **Excellent** | High |
