# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term 'Resolution' of a DAC. For a 10-bit DAC with a full-scale output of 10.23 V, calculate the percentage resolution and the step size.**

**Model Answer:**

**Resolution** is the smallest change in the analog output that can be distinguished as the digital input changes by 1 LSB. It is expressed as the number of discrete output levels, given by $2^n$, where $n$ is the number of bits.

**Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{FS} = 10.23 \text{ V}$

**Step 1:** Number of levels = $2^{10} = 1024$

**Step 2:** Percentage Resolution:

$$\% \text{ Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1023} \times 100\% \approx 0.0977\%$$

**Step 3:** Step Size (Quantum):

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10.23}{1023} = 0.01 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ mV}$$

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare Binary Weighted Resistor DAC and R-2R Ladder DAC in terms of resistor count, accuracy, and ease of integration.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Binary Weighted DAC** | **R-2R Ladder DAC** |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor Values | $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$ (many different values) | Only two values: $R$ and $2R$ |
| Resistor Count | $n$ resistors | $2n$ resistors |
| Range of Resistance | Very large (impractical for $n > 5$) | Small and manageable |
| IC Fabrication | Difficult | **Easy and standard** |
| Accuracy | Poor at high bits | **Excellent** |
| Temperature Drift | Higher | Lower (matched resistors) |
| Resolution Limit | Practically 4 to 5 bits | Easily 8 to 16 bits |
| Speed | Higher (fewer nodes) | Slightly lower |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Question Pattern (Mandatory under KTU 2024 Scheme)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Draw the circuit of a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC using an Op-amp. Derive the expression for the output voltage. If $V_{ref} = 10$ V and the digital input is 1011, calculate the analog output voltage (assuming $R_f = R$).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**

The 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC consists of:
- Four switches (one per bit) controlled by digital inputs $b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0$.
- A ladder network with resistors $R$ (series) and $2R$ (shunt).
- An inverting Op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f = R$.

**Step 2: Derivation of Output Voltage**

At every node of the R-2R ladder, the equivalent resistance looking into the network is $2R$. Therefore, the current from the MSB side is halved at each successive node.

The total current at the summing junction is:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right)$$

Output voltage:

$$V_o = -I_{total} \cdot R_f = -\frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{n-1-i}}$$

For $R_f = R$ and $n = 4$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^4} \left( 2^3 b_3 + 2^2 b_2 + 2^1 b_1 + 2^0 b_0 \right)$$

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{16} \left( 8b_3 + 4b_2 + 2b_1 + b_0 \right)$$

**Step 3: Numerical Calculation**

Given: $V_{ref} = 10$ V, $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1011$

- $b_3 = 1 \Rightarrow 8 \times 1 = 8$
- $b_2 = 0 \Rightarrow 4 \times 0 = 0$
- $b_1 = 1 \Rightarrow 2 \times 1 = 2$
- $b_0 = 1 \Rightarrow 1 \times 1 = 1$
- Sum = $8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$

$$V_o = -\frac{10}{16} \times 11 = -\frac{110}{16} = -6.875 \text{ V}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Successive Approximation ADC. An 8-bit SAR ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V receives an input of 3.4 V. Determine the digital output and the conversion time if the clock frequency is 1 MHz.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Diagram**

(Successive Approximation ADC consists of: SAR, DAC, Comparator, Clock, Output Register)

**Step 2: Working**

The SAR performs a **binary search**:
1. Start: Set MSB $b_7 = 1$, all others 0 → DAC output = $V_{ref}/2 = 2.5$ V.
2. If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: Keep $b_7 = 1$. Else: Reset to 0.
3. Set $b_6 = 1$ → test again. The DAC range halves each step.
4. After 8 clock cycles, all bits are determined.

**Step 3: Binary Search for $V_{in} = 3.4$ V**

| **Step** | **Bit** | **Test Value** | **DAC Output** | **Decision** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $b_7$ (MSB) | $2.5$ V | $2.5$ V | $3.4 > 2.5 \Rightarrow b_7 = 1$ |
| 2 | $b_6$ | $2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75$ V | $3.75$ V | $3.4 < 3.75 \Rightarrow b_6 = 0$ |
| 3 | $b_5$ | $2.5 + 0 + 0.625 = 3.125$ V | $3.125$ V | $3.4 > 3.125 \Rightarrow b_5 = 1$ |
| 4 | $b_4$ | $2.5 + 0.625 + 0.3125 = 3.4375$ V | $3.4375$ V | $3.4 < 3.4375 \Rightarrow b_4 = 0$ |
| 5 | $b_3$ | $2.5 + 0.625 + 0 + 0.15625 = 3.28125$ V | $3.28125$ V | $3.4 > 3.28125 \Rightarrow b_3 = 1$ |
| 6 | $b_2$ | $2.5 + 0.625 + 0.15625 + 0.078125 = 3.359375$ V | $3.359375$ V | $3.4 > 3.359375 \Rightarrow b_2 = 1$ |
| 7 | $b_1$ | $3.359375 + 0.0390625 = 3.3984375$ V | $3.398$ V | $3.4 > 3.398 \Rightarrow b_1 = 1$ |
| 8 | $b_0$ | $3.3984375 + 0.01953125 = 3.418$ V | $3.418$ V | $3.4 < 3.418 \Rightarrow b_0 = 0$ |

**Digital Output:** $b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1010 1110_2 = 0xAE = 174_{10}$

**Step 4: Conversion Time**

$$T_c = n \times T_{clk} = 8 \times \frac{1}{10^6} = 8 \text{ μs}$$

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Design a 4-bit binary weighted resistor DAC with $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $R_f = 20$ k$\Omega$. Calculate the output voltages for all 16 possible input combinations if $V_{ref} = 5$ V.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Output Formula**

For a 4-bit binary weighted DAC with feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot V_{ref} \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$\frac{R_f}{R} = \frac{20}{10} = 2$, $V_{ref} = 5$ V

$$V_o = -2 \times 5 \times \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right) = -10 \left( b_3 + 0.5 b_2 + 0.25 b_1 + 0.125 b_0 \right)$$

**Step 3: Step Size Verification**

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^4 - 1} = \frac{10 \times (15/15) \times V_{ref}}{15} = \frac{10}{1} \times \frac{1}{8} = 1.25 \text{ V per LSB}$$

Wait — let me recompute. Maximum output when $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1111$:

$$V_{max} = -10 \left( 1 + 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 \right) = -10 \times 1.875 = -18.75 \text{ V}$$

Step size $q = \dfrac{V_{max}}{15} = \dfrac{18.75}{15} = 1.25$ V (each LSB step)

**Step 4: Tabulated Output Voltages**

| **Decimal** | **$b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$** | **$V_o$ (V)** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0000 | 0 |
| 1 | 0001 | $-1.25$ |
| 2 | 0010 | $-2.50$ |
| 3 | 0011 | $-3.75$ |
| 4 | 0100 | $-5.00$ |
| 5 | 0101 | $-6.25$ |
| 6 | 0110 | $-7.50$ |
| 7 | 0111 | $-8.75$ |
| 8 | 1000 | $-10.00$ |
| 9 | 1001 | $-11.25$ |
| 10 | 1010 | $-12.50$ |
| 11 | 1011 | $-13.75$ |
| 12 | 1100 | $-15.00$ |
| 13 | 1101 | $-16.25$ |
| 14 | 1110 | $-17.50$ |
| 15 | 1111 | $-18.75$ |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the working principle of a dual-slope ADC with a neat block diagram. A dual-slope ADC uses a 12-bit counter and a 4 MHz clock. If the input voltage is 3.765 V and $V_{ref} = 5$ V, calculate the digital output count.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Working Principle**

(As explained in Section 2.2.4: Run-up phase integrates $V_{in}$ for fixed $T_1 = 2^n T_{clk}$, then Run-down phase integrates $-V_{ref}$ until integrator output returns to zero. The count $N_2$ during run-down is the digital output.)

**Step 2: Given Data**

- $n = 12$, $f_{clk} = 4$ MHz
- $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.25 \text{ μs}$
- $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $V_{in} = 3.765$ V

**Step 3: Run-Up Count and Time**

$$N_1 = 2^{12} = 4096$$
$$T_1 = N_1 \times T_{clk} = 4096 \times 0.25 \text{ μs} = 1024 \text{ μs} = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Apply Dual-Slope Formula**

$$N_2 = \frac{2^n}{V_{ref}} \cdot V_{in} = \frac{4096}{5} \times 3.765$$

$$N_2 = 819.2 \times 3.765 \approx 3083.81$$

**Step 5: Round to Nearest Integer**

$$\boxed{N_2 \approx 3084}$$

**Verification:** Converting $3084_{10}$ to binary: $3084 = 2048 + 1024 + 8 + 4 = 2^{11} + 2^{10} + 2^3 + 2^2 = 1100 0000 1100_2$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Most KTU textbook DAC circuits are **inverting** — students forget the negative sign in $V_o = -(\dots)$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **MSB vs LSB Resistor Mapping:** In Binary Weighted DAC, MSB gets **smallest** resistor $R$, LSB gets **largest** resistor $2^{n-1}R$. Students often reverse this.
> 3. **Step Size Formula:** Always use $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$, NOT $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$. The denominator is $2^n - 1$ because there are $2^n - 1$ **intervals** between 0 and full scale.
> 4. **SAR ADC Sequential Logic:** Students forget that each bit is determined in sequence, and the decision is binary (keep as 1 or reset to 0). Failing to show the comparator table loses full marks.
> 5. **Dual-Slope RC Independence:** A common KTU question asks "Why is dual-slope ADC preferred for digital voltmeters?" The answer is **noise rejection and RC independence** — students often miss the RC independence point.
> 6. **Flash ADC Comparator Count:** Students write $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ comparators for an $n$-bit Flash ADC. The $-1$ is because the highest reference voltage equals $V_{ref}$ (no comparator needed there).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **DAC Basics:** Binary Weighted uses $R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$ — limited to 4-5 bits practical.
- [x] **R-2R Ladder DAC:** Standard industrial DAC; uses only $R$ and $2R$ — scalable to 16+ bits.
- [x] **ADC Types:** Counter (slow), SAR (medium-fast), Flash (fastest), Dual-Slope (accurate).
- [x] **Resolution Formula:** $2^n$ levels, step size $q = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$.
- [x] **SAR Conversion Time:** Fixed at $n \cdot T_{clk}$ regardless of input value.
- [x] **Flash ADC Comparators:** $2^n - 1$ comparators required.
- [x] **Dual-Slope Advantage:** RC time constant cancels out — accuracy depends only on reference voltage.
- [x] **Sign of Output:** Standard inverting Op-amp configurations give **negative** output voltage.
- [x] **Inverting Summing Amplifier:** Foundation of all DAC topologies.
- [x] **Comparator:** Foundation of all ADC topologies (except dual-slope which uses integrator + zero-crossing detector).
- [x] **Monotonicity:** R-2R and current-steering DACs are inherently monotonic; binary weighted is not guaranteed.
- [x] **MSB Position:** Connected to smallest resistor (Binary Weighted) or leftmost in ladder (R-2R).
- [x] **Digital Voltmeter Standard:** Dual-slope ADC is the preferred choice due to noise rejection.
- [x] **Conversion Speed Hierarchy (slowest to fastest):** Dual-Slope → Counter → SAR → Flash.
- [x] **Hardware Complexity Hierarchy (lowest to highest):** SAR → Counter → Dual-Slope → Flash.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: D/A Converter - Specifications - Weighted Resistor Type, R-2R Ladder Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT304 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** D/A Converter - Specifications - Weighted Resistor Type, R-2R Ladder Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a precision mixed-signal electronic circuit that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital input code (typically a binary word of *n* bits) into a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog output signal (either a **voltage** or a **current**) that is proportional to the value of the input digital code.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBRUT304 – Module 2)**, the DAC is treated as the dual of the ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter), and forms the foundation of all digital signal processing (DSP), audio reproduction, and control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (Memorize This):**
> *"A D/A converter is an n-bit input device that produces an analog output voltage $V_o$ which is a fraction of a full-scale reference voltage $V_{REF}$, where the fraction is determined by the binary input word."*

### 1.2 The Core Intuition: The "Smart Weighing Scale" Analogy

Imagine you are a goldsmith with a set of standard weights: **1 g, 2 g, 4 g, 8 g, and 16 g**. These are *binary-weighted* weights — each one is exactly double the previous. If a customer places an order encoded as "10110" (meaning you need 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 22 g), you simply pick the weights corresponding to the '1' bits and place them on the scale. The total weight on the scale (22 g) is your **analog output**.

A DAC works identically:

- The **reference voltage** ($V_{REF}$) plays the role of the "maximum available weight."
- Each **bit** of the digital input is connected to a **switch**.
- The switches route *current* through either binary-weighted **resistors** (Weighted Resistor DAC) or through a clever ladder network (R-2R Ladder DAC) that produces the exact same binary-weighted current distribution.

The summed current is then converted to a voltage by an **Op-Amp summing amplifier**, giving you a precise analog output.

### 1.3 DAC Specifications — The "Quality Report Card"

Before designing a DAC, engineers evaluate it using standardized performance metrics. The KTU syllabus explicitly lists the following **Specifications of a DAC**:

| Specification | What It Really Means |
|---|---|
| **Resolution** | The smallest change in analog output for a 1-LSB change in the digital input. |
| **Full-Scale Output ($V_{FS}$)** | The maximum analog output produced when all input bits are '1'. |
| **Percentage Resolution** | Resolution expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output. |
| **Settling Time** | The time taken by the output to settle within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of its final value after a digital input change. |
| **Accuracy** | The maximum deviation of the actual output from the ideal (theoretical) output. |
| **Linearity** | How straight the DAC's transfer characteristic (staircase) is compared to the ideal straight line. |
| **Monotonicity** | A DAC is *monotonic* if its output never *decreases* when the input code increases monotonically. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution is the single most important specification for a KTU exam.** For an *n*-bit DAC, the number of discrete output levels is $2^n$, and the resolution (in volts) is:
> $$\text{Resolution} = 1 \text{ LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Architectures in Focus

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2 focuses on two fundamental resistor-based DAC architectures:

1. **Weighted Resistor DAC (Binary-Weighted DAC)**
2. **R-2R Ladder DAC**

Both rely on the principle of *summing binary-weighted currents* at the inverting input of an **Op-Amp** configured as a current-to-voltage (I-to-V) converter (summing amplifier with virtual ground).

### 2.2 Architecture 1: Weighted Resistor DAC

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- The DAC has *n* input bits: $b_1$ (MSB), $b_2$, $b_3$, ..., $b_n$ (LSB).
- Each bit controls an **SPST analog switch** (typically a CMOS transmission gate).
- Bit $b_1$ is connected through a resistor of value $R$.
- Bit $b_2$ is connected through a resistor of value $2R$.
- Bit $b_3$ is connected through a resistor of value $4R$.
- ...
- Bit $b_n$ is connected through a resistor of value $2^{n-1} R$.

**Why Binary-Weighted Resistors?**
Because the LSB must contribute exactly **half** the current of the next-higher bit, the resistance must **double** for each successive less-significant bit. This ensures that the current contributed by each bit is *inversely proportional* to its binary weight, so that the *voltage* contribution at the output is directly proportional to the binary weight.

**Why This Architecture is Problematic:**
- For a 10-bit DAC, the MSB resistor is $R$ and the LSB resistor is $2^9 R = 512 R$.
- The ratio of the largest to the smallest resistor is **1024 : 1**.
- This enormous spread is **extremely difficult to manufacture accurately** in integrated circuits.
- Consequently, the **Weighted Resistor DAC is rarely used in practice for high-resolution (n > 8 bits) applications.**

### 2.3 Architecture 2: R-2R Ladder DAC

**The Engineering Breakthrough:**
The R-2R Ladder DAC was invented to **eliminate the huge resistor spread** of the weighted-resistor design. It uses only **two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$**, regardless of the number of bits.

**Operating Principle (The Key Insight — The Ladder Theorem):**

At any node of the R-2R ladder, looking either *into the ladder* or *out of the ladder toward the termination*, the equivalent resistance is always **$R$**. This is because of the recursive structure: a $2R$ resistor in parallel with a series combination of $R$ and (the rest of the ladder, which is $R$) gives:

$$R_{eq} = 2R \parallel 2R = R$$

This elegant self-similar property means that at **every bit position**, the ladder divides the reference voltage by exactly 2, producing the correct binary-weighted current.

**Why This Architecture is Superior:**

- Uses only **two resistor values** — easy to manufacture and **monolithically integrable**.
- **Trimmable** to high precision.
- The output is **inherently monotonic** (if resistors are perfect, output always increases).
- The **R-2R Ladder is the industry standard** for integrated DACs.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are *exam-essential*. Memorize the resolution and the output voltage equations for both architectures.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of discrete output levels (for *n* bits) | $N = 2^n$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Resolution (1 LSB) — voltage mode | $1 \text{ LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Percentage Resolution | $\% \text{ Res} = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | Percent (%) |
| 4 | Full-Scale Output (unipolar, all bits = 1) | $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Ideal Output Voltage — **Weighted Resistor DAC** | $V_o = -V_{REF} \left(\dfrac{b_1}{2} + \dfrac{b_2}{4} + \dfrac{b_3}{8} + \cdots + \dfrac{b_n}{2^n}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Ideal Output Voltage — **R-2R Ladder DAC** | $V_o = -V_{REF} \left(\dfrac{b_1}{2} + \dfrac{b_2}{4} + \dfrac{b_3}{8} + \cdots + \dfrac{b_n}{2^n}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Output current contributed by bit $b_k$ | $I_k = \dfrac{b_k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{k-1} R}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | Settling time (typical) | $t_s$ in **µs** to **ns** range | Time (seconds) |
| 9 | MSB Resistor — Weighted Resistor DAC | $R_{MSB} = R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 10 | LSB Resistor — Weighted Resistor DAC | $R_{LSB} = 2^{n-1} R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 11 | Resistor spread — Weighted Resistor DAC | $\dfrac{R_{LSB}}{R_{MSB}} = 2^{n-1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Resistor values — R-2R Ladder DAC | Only $R$ and $2R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation for Board Exams:**
> Notice that the **output voltage equation is *identical* in form** for both the Weighted Resistor DAC and the R-2R Ladder DAC. The difference lies **only in the internal resistor network topology** that produces the binary-weighted currents. The KTU examiner will test whether you understand the *circuit implementation*, not just the final formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why a DAC is Needed |
|---|---|
| **Digital Audio (CD/MP3 Players)** | CD audio uses 16-bit, 44.1 kHz DACs to reconstruct analog sound from stored digital samples. |
| **Video Playback (HDMI/DisplayPort)** | R-2R and current-steering DACs reconstruct analog RGB video signals from digital pixel data. |
| **Microcontroller Output (Arduino, STM32)** | The built-in PWM can be low-pass filtered, or an external R-2R DAC can be built from precision resistors to generate true analog control voltages. |
| **Function Generators & Arbitrary Waveform Generators (AWGs)** | High-speed DACs synthesize sine, square, and arbitrary waveforms from a digital lookup table. |
| **Industrial Process Control (PLCs, Motor Drives)** | DAC outputs drive actuators, valves, and variable-frequency drives (VFDs) from digital setpoints. |
| **Precision Weighing Scales & Medical Instruments** | R-2R ladder DACs are used in the analog front-end of sensor signal conditioning. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of an *n*-bit Weighted Resistor DAC

**Circuit Setup:**
Consider an *n*-bit binary-weighted resistor DAC. The bits are denoted $b_1$ (MSB), $b_2, b_3, \ldots, b_n$ (LSB), where each $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$. Each bit is connected to a resistor $R_k = 2^{k-1} R$. The other end of each resistor is tied to the inverting input (virtual ground) of an Op-Amp with feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short Concept**
The non-inverting input of the Op-Amp is grounded, so the inverting input is also at **0 V (virtual ground)**.

**Step 2: Express the Current Through Each Resistor**
By Ohm's law, the current through resistor $R_k$ (driven by bit $b_k$) is:

$$I_k = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{REF} - 0}{R_k} = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{k-1} R}$$

> *Textual Logic:* Since the inverting node is a virtual ground (0 V), the entire reference voltage $V_{REF}$ drops across resistor $R_k$ when the switch is closed ($b_k = 1$).

**Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Sum of Input Currents)**
All currents flow *into* the virtual ground node and *out through* the feedback resistor $R_f$ (since the Op-Amp draws no input current):

$$I_f = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \cdots + I_n$$

**Step 4: Expand the Summation**

$$I_f = \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{0} R} + \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{1} R} + \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{2} R} + \cdots + \frac{b_n \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{n-1} R}$$

> *Textual Logic:* The MSB ($b_1$) sees the smallest resistance $R$ and therefore contributes the **largest** current. The LSB ($b_n$) sees the largest resistance $2^{n-1}R$ and contributes the **smallest** current — exactly the binary-weighting we desire.

**Step 5: Compute the Output Voltage**
For a standard inverting summing amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$V_o = - I_f \cdot R_f$$

Choosing $R_f = R$ for the Weighted Resistor DAC:

$$V_o = -R \cdot \left[ \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{REF}}{R} + \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{REF}}{2R} + \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{REF}}{4R} + \cdots + \frac{b_n \cdot V_{REF}}{2^{n-1} R} \right]$$

**Step 6: Simplify**

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left[ b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^{n-1}} \right]$$

> *Textual Logic:* The negative sign indicates **inverting** output. The bracketed term is a **binary fraction** between 0 and $\frac{2^n - 1}{2^{n-1}}$.

**Step 7: Rewrite in the Standard KTU Board Form**

$$\boxed{V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^n} \right)}$$

This is obtained by **multiplying and dividing the bracket by 2** to express it in fractional form $\left(\frac{x}{2^n}\right)$.

**Step 8: Verify the Full-Scale Output**
When all bits are 1 ($b_1 = b_2 = \cdots = b_n = 1$):

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \cdots + \frac{1}{2^n} \right) = -V_{REF} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2^n} \right) = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$$

This matches the full-scale formula: $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Voltage of an *n*-bit R-2R Ladder DAC

**Circuit Setup:**
The R-2R Ladder is a **current-steering** network. The bits $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$ select whether the current at each rung is steered into the Op-Amp summing node (virtual ground) or into **ground**. The ladder is terminated at the far end with a resistor of value $2R$ to ground.

**Step 1: Apply the Ladder Theorem (The Self-Similar Property)**
At any node of the R-2R ladder, looking *to the right* (toward the rest of the ladder + termination), the equivalent resistance is always $R$. Therefore, the current injected by bit $b_k$ at the $k$-th node always sees a Thevenin equivalent of $V_{Thevenin,k}$ into an equivalent resistance of $2R$ (the local $2R$ shunt resistor in parallel with the $R$-looking-right).

**Step 2: Identify the Per-Rung Thevenin Voltage**
The ladder acts as a **successive voltage divider of factor $\frac{1}{2}$** at each rung. Starting from the MSB end, the Thevenin voltage seen at each switch is:

$$V_{Th,k} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{k-1}} \quad \text{(for the k-th bit, where k=1 is MSB)}$$

> *Textual Logic:* The MSB sees nearly the full reference voltage (divided by 2 due to the first $2R$ shunt). Each subsequent bit is divided by another factor of 2, producing the binary-weighting.

**Step 3: Express the Current Injected by Bit $b_k$**
When $b_k = 1$, the current steered into the virtual ground is:

$$I_k = \frac{V_{Th,k}}{2R} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{k-1} \cdot 2R} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^k R}$$

> *Textual Logic:* The current is divided by an additional factor of 2 compared to the weighted-resistor case (because of the local $2R$ shunt to ground).

**Step 4: Sum All Currents at the Inverting Node**

$$I_f = \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$

**Step 5: Compute the Output Voltage**
With feedback resistor $R_f = 2R$ (the standard choice for the R-2R DAC, to compensate for the extra factor of 2 in the denominator):

$$V_o = - I_f \cdot R_f = -2R \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$

**Step 6: Simplify**

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$

**Step 7: Rewrite in the Standard KTU Board Form** (multiplying and dividing by 2):

$$\boxed{V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^n} \right)}$$

> *Textual Logic:* The R-2R Ladder DAC produces the **same output voltage equation** as the Weighted Resistor DAC — but it does so using only two resistor values, making it vastly more practical for IC fabrication.

**Step 8: Cross-Verification (Full-Scale Output)**
For all bits = 1:

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2^n} \right) = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n} = V_{FS} \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Typical Numerical)

**Problem:** A 4-bit Weighted Resistor DAC has $V_{REF} = 10$ V and $R = 1$ k$\Omega$. Calculate $V_o$ for the digital input **1010**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the bits: $b_1 = 1, b_2 = 0, b_3 = 1, b_4 = 0$.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \frac{b_4}{16} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$V_o = -10 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{0}{16} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$V_o = -10 \left( 0.500 + 0.000 + 0.125 + 0.000 \right) = -10 \times 0.625 = -6.25 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_o = -6.25$ V (i.e., 6.25 V in magnitude). The input 1010 corresponds to decimal 10, and 1 LSB = $\frac{10}{2^4 - 1} = \frac{10}{15} \approx 0.667$ V; $10 \times 0.667 = 6.67$ V (the slight difference is due to the $\frac{2^n-1}{2^n}$ scaling). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DAC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Digital Input Word n bits b1 to bn] --> B[Analog Switch Array CMOS Transmission Gates]
    B --> C[Resistor Network Weighted Resistor OR R-2R Ladder]
    C --> D[Summing Junction Virtual Ground of Op Amp]
    D --> E[Op Amp Current to Voltage Converter]
    E --> F[Analog Output Voltage Vo]
    G[Precision Voltage Reference VREF] --> C
    H[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The digital word controls CMOS switches that route binary-weighted currents from the resistor network into the virtual ground of an Op-Amp. The Op-Amp, with its feedback resistor, converts the summed current into an analog output voltage.

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Weighted Resistor DAC Topology (4-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VREF[VREF] --> S1[Switch b1 MSB]
    VREF --> S2[Switch b2]
    VREF --> S3[Switch b3]
    VREF --> S4[Switch b4 LSB]
    S1 --> R1[Resistor R]
    S2 --> R2[Resistor 2R]
    S3 --> R3[Resistor 4R]
    S4 --> R4[Resistor 8R]
    R1 --> SUM((Summing Node Virtual Ground))
    R2 --> SUM
    R3 --> SUM
    R4 --> SUM
    SUM --> OP[Op Amp Inverting Input]
    SUM --> Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf = R]
    Rf --> OP
    OP --> Vout[Analog Output Vo]
    GND1[Ground] --> OPnon[Op Amp Non Inverting Input]
```

> **Topology insight:** Note the **exponential spread** of resistor values: $R, 2R, 4R, 8R$. For an 8-bit DAC, the LSB resistor would be $128R$ — a fabrication nightmare.

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — R-2R Ladder DAC Topology (4-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VREF[VREF] --> N1((Node 1 MSB b1))
    N1 --> R1a[Resistor R Horizontal]
    R1a --> N2((Node 2 b2))
    N2 --> R2a[Resistor R Horizontal]
    R2a --> N3((Node 3 b3))
    N3 --> R3a[Resistor R Horizontal]
    R3a --> N4((Node 4 LSB b4))
    N4 --> Rterm[Termination Resistor 2R]
    Rterm --> GND1[Ground]
    N1 --> R1b[Resistor 2R Shunt to b1 switch]
    N2 --> R2b[Resistor 2R Shunt to b2 switch]
    N3 --> R3b[Resistor 2R Shunt to b3 switch]
    N4 --> R4b[Resistor 2R Shunt to b4 switch]
    R1b --> S1[Switch b1 to Virtual Ground or Ground]
    R2b --> S2[Switch b2 to Virtual Ground or Ground]
    R3b --> S3[Switch b3 to Virtual Ground or Ground]
    R4b --> S4[Switch b4 to Virtual Ground or Ground]
    S1 --> VG((Summing Bus Virtual Ground))
    S2 --> VG
    S3 --> VG
    S4 --> VG
    VG --> OP[Op Amp Summing Amplifier]
    OP --> Vout[Analog Output Vo]
```

> **Topology insight:** Observe that the ladder uses **only two values — $R$ and $2R$**. The horizontal resistors are $R$ and the shunt-to-switch resistors are $2R$. This uniformity is the key advantage of the R-2R architecture.

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Weighted Resistor vs R-2R Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Weighted Resistor DAC] -->|Disadvantage Huge resistor spread 2 superscript n minus 1 colon 1| B[Not IC friendly]
    A -->|Advantage Simple circuit| C[Easy to analyze]
    D[R-2R Ladder DAC] -->|Advantage Only two resistor values R and 2R| E[IC friendly Monolithically integrable]
    D -->|Advantage Inherently monotonic| F[Industry standard]
    D -->|Disadvantage More complex topology| G[Harder to analyze by hand]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define the term **Resolution** of a Digital-to-Analog Converter. A 12-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 10 V. Calculate its resolution in volts and as a percentage.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **resolution** of a DAC is defined as the **smallest change in the analog output voltage** that can be distinguished as the digital input code is incremented by 1 LSB (Least Significant Bit). It is also called the **step size** or **1 LSB**.

**Mathematical Formula:**

$$\text{Resolution} = 1 \text{ LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Given Data:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 10$ V

**Calculation:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \text{ mV}$$

**Percentage Resolution:**

$$\% \text{ Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^{12} - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{4095} \times 100 \approx 0.0244\%$$

> **[3 Marks Breakdown]:** Definition — 1 mark, Formula — 1 mark, Numerical substitution and answer — 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **four important specifications** of a DAC and briefly explain the term **Settling Time**.

**Model Answer:**

The four important specifications of a DAC are:

1. **Resolution:** The smallest increment in analog output for a 1-LSB change in input.
2. **Accuracy:** The maximum deviation of the actual DAC output from the ideal theoretical output, usually expressed as a percentage of full-scale.
3. **Linearity:** The measure of how closely the DAC's transfer characteristic follows a straight line; quantified as **INL (Integral Non-Linearity)** and **DNL (Differential Non-Linearity)**.
4. **Monotonicity:** A DAC is monotonic if its output *never decreases* as the input digital code increases monotonically from zero to full scale.

**Settling Time ($t_s$):**
The **settling time** is defined as the **time required for the DAC output to settle within and remain within a specified error band (typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB) of its final steady-state value**, after a full-scale step change in the digital input code. It is a critical specification for high-speed applications (e.g., video DACs, AWGs), where fast updates are necessary.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown]:** List of four specifications — 2 marks, Explanation of settling time — 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each: **(a)** and **(b)**. You are given an internal choice: **answer either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **4-bit weighted-resistor DAC** using an Op-Amp summing amplifier. Derive the expression for the output voltage.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a 4-bit weighted-resistor DAC with $V_{REF} = 16$ V and $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, calculate the output voltage for the input codes (i) **0001**, (ii) **1000**, and (iii) **1111**. Also find the **resolution in volts**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A**

**Part (a) — Circuit and Derivation:**

**Circuit Description:**
A 4-bit weighted-resistor DAC consists of:
- Four CMOS analog switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ controlled by input bits $b_1$ (MSB), $b_2, b_3, b_4$ (LSB).
- Four binary-weighted resistors connected to the inverting input of an Op-Amp: $R_1 = R, R_2 = 2R, R_3 = 4R, R_4 = 8R$.
- A feedback resistor $R_f = R$.
- A precision reference voltage $V_{REF}$.

When bit $b_k = 1$, the corresponding switch connects the resistor to $V_{REF}$. When $b_k = 0$, the resistor is connected to **ground**. The Op-Amp operates as a current-summing amplifier with virtual ground at its inverting input.

**Derivation:**

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \frac{b_4}{16} \right)$$

*(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above should be reproduced on the answer script — KTU expects at least 5–6 derivation steps.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations:**

**Given:** $V_{REF} = 16$ V, $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, $n = 4$ bits.

**Resolution (1 LSB):**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^n}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{16}{16} = 1 \text{ V}$$

*(Note: For $V_{FS} = V_{REF}$, the resolution is approximately $\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$.)*

**Case (i): Input 0001 (only LSB = 1):**
$b_1 = 0, b_2 = 0, b_3 = 0, b_4 = 1$

$$V_o = -16 \left( 0 + 0 + 0 + \frac{1}{16} \right) = -1 \text{ V}$$

**Case (ii): Input 1000 (only MSB = 1):**
$b_1 = 1, b_2 = 0, b_3 = 0, b_4 = 0$

$$V_o = -16 \left( \frac{1}{2} + 0 + 0 + 0 \right) = -8 \text{ V}$$

**Case (iii): Input 1111 (all bits = 1):**

$$V_o = -16 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right) = -16 \times \frac{15}{16} = -15 \text{ V} = V_{FS}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Input Code | Decimal Equivalent | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|
| 0001 | 1 | $-1$ V |
| 1000 | 8 | $-8$ V |
| 1111 | 15 | $-15$ V |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC**. State and explain the **Ladder Theorem** that governs its operation. Derive the output voltage expression.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Weighted Resistor DAC** and the **R-2R Ladder DAC** on the basis of (i) number of resistor values required, (ii) resistor spread, (iii) suitability for IC fabrication, and (iv) monotonicity. State **two disadvantages** of the R-2R ladder DAC.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B**

**Part (a) — Circuit and Ladder Theorem:**

**The Ladder Theorem:**
*"At any node of the R-2R ladder, looking either to the right (toward the rest of the ladder and its termination) or to the left, the equivalent Thevenin resistance is always equal to $R$."*

**Mathematical Proof (Recursive):**
The ladder is terminated at the LSB end with a resistor of value $2R$ to ground. At the LSB node (Node 4), the resistance to ground is $2R \parallel 2R = R$. Moving to Node 3, the equivalent resistance is $2R \parallel (R + R) = 2R \parallel 2R = R$. By induction, every node sees an equivalent resistance of $R$ to ground.

**Output Voltage Derivation:**

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \frac{b_4}{16} \right)$$

*(Full derivation as in Section 3.2 above should be reproduced on the answer script.)*

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of resistor values** | *n* different values ($R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$) | Only **2 values** ($R$ and $2R$) |
| 2 | **Resistor spread (ratio)** | $2^{n-1} : 1$ (e.g., 512:1 for 10-bit) | Always **2:1** |
| 3 | **Suitability for IC fabrication** | Poor — large spread is hard to fabricate on-chip | Excellent — uniform $R$ and $2R$ are easy to fabricate monolithically |
| 4 | **Monotonicity** | Not inherently monotonic (resistor mismatch can cause non-monotonicity) | **Inherently monotonic** (if switches are ideal) |

**Two Disadvantages of the R-2R Ladder DAC:**

1. **Larger switch count and more complex wiring:** The R-2R ladder requires each bit to drive a switch that steers current between the virtual ground and ground, leading to more intricate routing and switch control logic.
2. **Output impedance variation with code:** The output impedance of the ladder changes depending on the digital input code, which can introduce non-linear settling behavior unless the Op-Amp has very low input bias current and high bandwidth.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $V_o$:** The summing amplifier is inverting. If the question does not specify sign, write $V_o = -V_{REF}(\cdots)$ and **state the magnitude separately**. Losing the sign costs **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_{FS}$ with $V_{REF}$:** The full-scale output is $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$, which is **slightly less** than $V_{REF}$ (e.g., for a 4-bit DAC, $V_{FS} = 0.9375 \, V_{REF}$). Writing $V_{FS} = V_{REF}$ will cost you **1 mark** in numericals.
>
> 3. **Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ in the resolution formula:** The number of *steps* between zero and full scale is $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$. Always write the resolution formula as $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$.
>
> 4. **Insufficient derivation steps:** KTU examiners require **at least 5–6 clear derivation steps** (virtual short, KCL, summation, simplification). Skipping the "virtual ground" justification or the KCL statement is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Failing to draw the circuit diagram:** A circuit diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-part. If the question says "with neat circuit diagram," omitting the diagram is a fatal error.
>
> 6. **Confusing "R-2R Ladder" with "Inverted R-2R Ladder":** The standard topology has $R$ horizontal and $2R$ shunt (as derived above). The "inverted" R-2R ladder swaps these — make sure you state which version you are using.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before the Exam!)**

- [ ] A DAC converts a **binary digital word** into a **proportional analog voltage or current**.
- [ ] For an *n*-bit DAC, the number of discrete output levels = $2^n$, and the **resolution = $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$**.
- [ ] The **percentage resolution** = $\frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] The **full-scale output** $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ (slightly less than $V_{REF}$).
- [ ] The **ideal output equation** (valid for *both* architectures) is:
      $$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_n}{2^n} \right)$$
- [ ] The **Weighted Resistor DAC** uses resistors of value $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$ — one per bit.
- [ ] The **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses **only two resistor values, $R$ and $2R$**, regardless of *n*.
- [ ] The **Ladder Theorem** states that at every node of the R-2R ladder, the equivalent resistance looking outward is always $R$.
- [ ] The **Weighted Resistor DAC** has a **resistor spread of $2^{n-1} : 1$**, making it **unsuitable for high-resolution IC integration**.
- [ ] The **R-2R Ladder DAC** is the **industry standard** for integrated DACs (monotonic, easy to fabricate, only two resistor values).
- [ ] **Settling time** is the time for the output to settle within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of its final value after a digital input change.
- [ ] A DAC is **monotonic** if its output never decreases as the input code increases.
- [ ] The **Op-Amp summing amplifier** with virtual ground is the workhorse I-to-V converter for both architectures.
- [ ] The negative sign in $V_o$ arises because the architecture is fundamentally an **inverting summing amplifier**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)?

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a mixed-signal integrated circuit device that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital representation (binary code). The digital output is an n-bit binary word that is proportional to the input analog voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An ADC is a device that performs quantization of an analog input signal and produces a corresponding digital output code. The conversion process involves three fundamental operations: **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**, in accordance with the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem.

## 1.2 Block Diagram of a Generic ADC

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
 Analog          │                                         │  Digital
 Input    ──────►│  Anti-Alias  │ Sample &  │ Quantizer &  │──────► Output
  V_in            │  Filter (AAF)│  Hold (S/H)│  Encoder     │  (n-bit)
                 │              │           │              │
                 └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase" of Numbers

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a long staircase, and a friend at the top is shouting a continuous number (e.g., "3.7421... 3.7422... 3.7423..."). 

- **Sampling** is like blinking your eyes at regular intervals to "freeze" the number at a specific moment.
- **Quantization** is like rounding the frozen number to the nearest whole stair (you can only stand on stair 3 or stair 4, not in between).
- **Encoding** is like writing down the stair number as a binary code (e.g., stair 3 = `011`).

The **stair height** represents the **resolution (Q)** of the ADC. More stairs = finer resolution = a more accurate digital representation of the analog signal.

## 1.4 Why ADCs Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> ADCs are the **bridge between the physical (analog) world and the digital world of processors, microcontrollers, and FPGAs**. Applications include digital audio (smartphones, MP3 players), digital oscilloscopes, medical imaging (MRI, CT scans), automotive sensors (ECUs), IoT devices, and instrumentation systems.

## 1.5 ADC Performance Specifications — The "Vital Statistics"

| # | Specification | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resolution** | The smallest change in input that can be detected (1 LSB) | Volts (V) or Bits |
| 2 | **Quantization Error** | Maximum inherent error due to rounding (±Q/2) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | **Accuracy** | Deviation of actual output from ideal output | % of FSR or LSB |
| 4 | **Conversion Time (t_c)** | Time required to complete one A/D conversion | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | **Sampling Rate (f_s)** | Number of conversions per second | Samples/Second (S/s) |
| 6 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | Ratio of signal power to quantization noise | Decibels (dB) |
| 7 | **Dynamic Range** | Ratio of largest to smallest detectable signal | dB |
| 8 | **Linearity (INL/DNL)** | Deviation from ideal straight-line transfer | LSB |
| 9 | **Power Dissipation** | Power consumed during operation | Milliwatts (mW) |
| 10 | **Input Range (V_FS)** | Full-scale input voltage span | Volts (V) |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed ADC Specifications

### 2.1.1 Resolution (Q — Quantization Step)

The resolution of an ADC is defined as the smallest incremental change in the input analog voltage that causes the digital output to change by one LSB (Least Significant Bit).

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

Where:
- $V_{FS}$ = Full-Scale input voltage range (typically $V_{ref+}$ − $V_{ref-}$)
- $n$ = Number of bits of the ADC
- $2^n$ = Total number of discrete quantization levels

**Example:** For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5.12$ V, the resolution is:
$$Q = \frac{5.12}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.12}{1024} = 5 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### 2.1.2 Quantization Error

The maximum quantization error is always **±Q/2**, occurring because the digital output can only represent discrete levels while the input is continuous.

$$e_{q(\max)} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

### 2.1.3 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

For an ideal N-bit ADC, the theoretical SNR due to quantization noise is given by:

$$SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 6.02 dB?** Each additional bit of resolution improves the SNR by exactly **6.02 dB** (≈ 20·log₁₀(2)). This is a high-yield KTU numerical problem.

### 2.1.4 Dynamic Range

$$DR = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(2^n\right) \approx 6.02n \text{ dB}$$

---

## 2.2 Type 1: Flash Type ADC (Parallel Comparator ADC)

### 2.2.1 Architecture

The Flash ADC is the **fastest** type of ADC. For an **n-bit** converter, it uses:
- $2^n - 1$ comparators
- A precision resistor ladder (voltage divider)
- A priority encoder

### 2.2.2 Operational Principle

1. A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across a series string of $2^n$ equal resistors, generating $2^n - 1$ threshold voltages:
   $$V_{k} = \frac{k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1$$
2. Each comparator compares $V_{in}$ with its corresponding threshold $V_k$.
3. The comparator outputs a "thermometer code" (a string of 1s and 0s).
4. The priority encoder converts the thermometer code into a standard binary code.

### 2.2.3 Conversion Time

Conversion time is limited only by the propagation delay of a single comparator and the encoder:

$$t_c = t_{comparator} + t_{encoder}$$

Typical values: **5 to 50 ns** (GHz sampling rates achievable).

### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:** Ultra-fast, single-clock conversion, simple architecture.
> **Disadvantages:** Exponential hardware growth ($2^n$ comparators), high power consumption, large chip area, limited resolution (typically ≤ 8 bits practically).

---

## 2.3 Type 2: Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC

### 2.3.1 Architecture

The SAR ADC uses a **binary search algorithm** through a feedback loop comprising:
- A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit
- A **Comparator**
- A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)**
- An **n-bit DAC** (in the feedback path)

### 2.3.2 Operational Principle (Binary Search)

For an n-bit SAR ADC, conversion takes exactly **n clock cycles**:

1. **Cycle 1 (MSB):** The SAR sets the MSB = 1, all other bits = 0. DAC produces $V_{DAC} = V_{ref}/2$. If $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$, MSB is kept as 1; else, reset to 0.
2. **Cycle 2:** Next bit is set to 1, and the comparison is repeated. This continues for all n bits.
3. After n cycles, the SAR holds the final n-bit digital output.

### 2.3.3 Conversion Time

$$t_c = n \cdot T_{clock}$$

Where $T_{clock}$ is the period of the clock driving the SAR logic.

### 2.3.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:** Excellent trade-off between speed and resolution, low power, moderate complexity, widely used (12–18 bits typical).
> **Disadvantages:** Slower than flash, requires a precision DAC, susceptible to input noise during conversion.

---

## 2.4 Type 3: Dual Slope ADC (Integrating Type)

### 2.4.1 Architecture

A Dual Slope ADC uses an **integrator**, a **comparator**, a **counter**, and a **control logic** block. It is the **most accurate and noise-immune** ADC type, ideal for digital voltmeters (DVMs) and instrumentation.

### 2.4.2 Operational Principle (Two Phases)

**Phase 1 — Run-Up (Integration) Phase:** The unknown input $V_{in}$ is switched to the integrator for a fixed period $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clock}$. The integrator output ramps **down** (assuming $V_{in} > 0$):
$$V_{int}(T_1) = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC} \cdot T_1$$

**Phase 2 — Run-Down (De-integration) Phase:** The reference voltage $-V_{ref}$ (opposite polarity) is switched to the integrator. The output ramps back **up** to zero. The counter measures the time $T_2$ taken:
$$T_2 = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{V_{ref}} = \frac{T_1 \cdot V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$$

The digital output is proportional to the count $N_2 = T_2 / T_{clock}$:

$$N_2 = \frac{2^n \cdot V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$$

### 2.4.3 Conversion Time

$$t_c = T_1 + T_2 = 2^n \cdot T_{clock} \left(1 + \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\right)$$

Maximum conversion time (at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$): $t_{c(\max)} = 2 \cdot 2^n \cdot T_{clock}$

### 2.4.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:** Excellent noise rejection (integrator averages out high-frequency noise), high accuracy, inexpensive, immune to component variations (R and C cancel out).
> **Disadvantages:** Very slow (typical conversion time in milliseconds), limited to low-frequency signals.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolution / Q | $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Smallest detectable input step |
| 2 | Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm Q/2$ | Max inherent conversion error |
| 3 | SNR (Ideal) | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 4 | Dynamic Range | $DR \approx 6.02n$ dB | Ratio of max/min detectable signal |
| 5 | Flash ADC Comparators | $N_{comp} = 2^n - 1$ | Hardware count for n-bit flash |
| 6 | Flash ADC Conversion Time | $t_c = t_{comp} + t_{enc}$ | Limited by single comparator |
| 7 | SAR ADC Conversion Time | $t_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | n clock cycles for n bits |
| 8 | Dual Slope Run-Up | $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Fixed integration time |
| 9 | Dual Slope Run-Down | $T_2 = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \cdot T_1$ | Variable de-integration time |
| 10 | Dual Slope Count | $N_2 = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \cdot 2^n$ | Digital output count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Flash ADCs are used in oscilloscopes and radar. SAR ADCs dominate in microcontrollers, ADCs, and data acquisition. Dual Slope ADCs are used in digital multimeters (DMMs), weighing scales, and industrial process control where **accuracy is paramount and speed is secondary**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC operates with a full-scale input voltage of $V_{FS} = 10$ V. Calculate (a) the resolution Q, (b) the maximum quantization error, and (c) the theoretical SNR.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters:**

$$n = 12 \text{ bits}, \quad V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total number of quantization levels:**

The number of discrete output codes is $2^n$:
$$2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the resolution formula:**

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096}$$

$$Q = 2.4414 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the maximum quantization error:**

$$e_{q(\max)} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{2.4414}{2} = \pm 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the theoretical SNR:**

$$SNR = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76$$

$$SNR = 74 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flash ADC — Number of Comparators and Threshold Voltages

**Problem Statement:** Design an 8-bit Flash ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. Find (a) the number of comparators required, (b) the resolution Q, and (c) the threshold voltage of the 100th comparator.

**Step 1 — Number of comparators for an n-bit Flash ADC:**

$$N_{comp} = 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255 \text{ comparators}$$

**Step 2 — Resolution calculation:**

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{256} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold voltage of the k-th comparator:**

The threshold voltages are generated by a resistor ladder, with the k-th threshold given by:
$$V_k = \frac{k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For $k = 100$:
$$V_{100} = \frac{100 \cdot 5}{256} = \frac{500}{256}$$

$$V_{100} = 1.953 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SAR ADC — Step-by-Step Binary Search

**Problem Statement:** A 4-bit SAR ADC with $V_{ref} = 10$ V is given. The analog input is $V_{in} = 6.8$ V. Determine the final 4-bit digital output and the DAC voltage at each step.

**Step 1 — Setup:**

We have $n = 4$ bits, $V_{ref} = 10$ V, $V_{in} = 6.8$ V. Resolution $Q = 10/16 = 0.625$ V.

**Step 2 — Cycle 1 (MSB Test, b3):**

Set $b_3 = 1, b_2 = 0, b_1 = 0, b_0 = 0$. Trial code = 1000.
$$V_{DAC} = \frac{10}{2} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$
Compare: $V_{in} = 6.8 \text{ V} \geq 5.0 \text{ V}$ → **Keep $b_3 = 1$**.

**Step 3 — Cycle 2 (Test b2):**

Set $b_2 = 1, b_1 = 0, b_0 = 0$. Trial code = 1100.
$$V_{DAC} = 5.0 + 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ V}$$
Compare: $V_{in} = 6.8 \text{ V} < 7.5 \text{ V}$ → **Reset $b_2 = 0$**.

**Step 4 — Cycle 3 (Test b1):**

Set $b_1 = 1, b_0 = 0$. Trial code = 1010.
$$V_{DAC} = 5.0 + 0 + 1.25 = 6.25 \text{ V}$$
Compare: $V_{in} = 6.8 \text{ V} \geq 6.25 \text{ V}$ → **Keep $b_1 = 1$**.

**Step 5 — Cycle 4 (Test b0):**

Set $b_0 = 1$. Trial code = 1011.
$$V_{DAC} = 5.0 + 0 + 1.25 + 0.625 = 6.875 \text{ V}$$
Compare: $V_{in} = 6.8 \text{ V} < 6.875 \text{ V}$ → **Reset $b_0 = 0$**.

**Step 6 — Final digital output:**

$$\boxed{\text{Output} = 1010_2 = 10_{10}}$$

**Verification:** Expected output = $\text{Round}(V_{in}/Q) = \text{Round}(6.8/0.625) = \text{Round}(10.88) = 11$? Note: SAR may yield 1010 (=10) or 1011 (=11) depending on rounding convention. Our derivation gives **1010**, which is correct under truncation rounding.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dual Slope ADC — Complete Conversion

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit dual-slope ADC uses a clock frequency of 1 MHz and $V_{ref} = 10$ V. The analog input is $V_{in} = 3.5$ V. Find (a) the run-up time $T_1$, (b) the run-down time $T_2$, and (c) the digital output count $N_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters:**

$$n = 12, \quad f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz}, \quad T_{clk} = 1 \text{ μs}, \quad V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}, \quad V_{in} = 3.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the run-up (fixed integration) time:**

$$T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk} = 2^{12} \cdot 1 \text{ μs} = 4096 \cdot 1 \text{ μs} = 4096 \text{ μs} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the run-down (de-integration) time:**

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1 \cdot V_{in}}{V_{ref}} = \frac{4096 \cdot 3.5}{10} = \frac{14336}{10} = 1433.6 \text{ μs}$$

$$T_2 = 1.4336 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the digital output count:**

$$N_2 = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1433.6 \text{ μs}}{1 \text{ μs}} = 1433.6 \approx 1434 \text{ counts}$$

**Verification using direct formula:**
$$N_2 = \frac{2^n \cdot V_{in}}{V_{ref}} = \frac{4096 \cdot 3.5}{10} = 1433.6$$

**Step 5 — Total conversion time:**

$$t_c = T_1 + T_2 = 4.096 \text{ ms} + 1.4336 \text{ ms} = 5.5296 \text{ ms}$$

This corresponds to a maximum sampling rate of:
$$f_s = \frac{1}{t_{c(\max)}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 2^n \cdot T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{8.192 \text{ ms}} \approx 122 \text{ samples/second}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Dual Slope ADC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Complexity** | Very High ($2^n - 1$ comparators) | Moderate (SAR + DAC + Comparator) | Low (Integrator + Counter) |
| **Conversion Speed** | Fastest (ns) | Medium (μs) | Slowest (ms) |
| **Resolution (typical)** | 4 – 8 bits | 8 – 18 bits | 12 – 24 bits |
| **Power Consumption** | Very High | Low to Medium | Low |
| **Noise Immunity** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Application** | Oscilloscopes, Radar, High-speed comms | MCUs, Data Acquisition, IoT | Digital Multimeters, Weighing Scales |
| **Conversion Time Formula** | $t_c = t_{comp} + t_{enc}$ | $t_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | $t_c = 2^n \cdot T_{clk} (1 + V_{in}/V_{ref})$ |

## 4.4 Operating Waveform — Dual Slope ADC

```mermaid
gantt
    title Dual Slope ADC Operating Phases
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section Run-Up Phase
    Vin connected, integrator ramps down :a1, 0, 4096us
    
    section Run-Down Phase
    Vref connected, integrator ramps up :a2, after a1, 1434us
    
    section Counter Output
    Counter value increases from 0 to 1434 :a3, after a1, 1434us
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** During the **Run-Up** phase, the integrator output ramps linearly downward (when $V_{in} > 0$). During the **Run-Down** phase, the output ramps back up to zero. The time taken in Run-Down is directly proportional to the input voltage, and the counter captures this time as the digital output.
